The Mattingly Lineage

From Thomas Mattingly I — Catholic immigrant to Maryland, 1663 — through fourteen generations to Hunter, four centuries later. Built from genealogical research (FamilySearch, WikiTree, Maryland State Archives).

Timeline

Four centuries of family events alongside the broader sweep of history. Drag horizontally to explore.

The Migration

Four centuries of purposeful motion. From Hampshire, England (1663) across the Atlantic to Maryland — south to Kentucky — through the Carolinas — into Texas — and finally to California. Hover any marker to read what happened there.

Legend ● Settlement waypoint
—— Migration route
- - - Atlantic crossing (1663)

Notable Stories from the Family

Verifiable accounts of what your ancestors actually did — drawn from primary sources, county histories, newspaper archives, and genealogical records. Each story has at least one source URL you can verify yourself.

Medieval (1167–1249)

Ellis of Mattingley (fl. 1167)
1167 · Mattingley, Hampshire, England

First named lord of Mattingley — recorded in the Pipe Roll of Henry II

In 1167, the royal financial accounts of King Henry II — compiled in the Pipe Roll for the thirteenth year of his reign — record Ellis as owner of the village of Mattingley in Hampshire. He is the earliest named person connected to the manor, standing at the head of a line that would carry its place-name as a surname across 850 years and two oceans. The village itself had been called 'Matingelege' since before the Domesday survey of 1086, deriving from 'Matta's ley' — the woodland clearing of a Saxon chieftain named Matta whose people, the Basingas, had settled the Whitewater Valley in the fifth or sixth century. Ellis left no patronym; the surname was still the land itself.

source · source
Stephen de Mattingley (fl. 1206)
1206 · Mattingley, Hampshire, England

Earliest dated bearer of the Mattingley surname — granted 6 virgates and a mill to the Prior of Merton

Stephen de Mattingley is the first person in recorded history to carry the surname in a dated document. In 1206, he appeared in the Curia Regis Rolls as 'Stephen de Madingel'' and in the Feet of Fines for Hampshire (Trinity 8 John) when he granted to Walter, Prior of Merton, and his successors in free alms: six virgates of land, thirty acres, several meadows, six shillings and three pence rent, and a mill at Mattingley. He had also received his brother Bartholomew's share of their father Revelendus's estate. The Oxford Dictionary of English Surnames (Reaney and Wilson, 1991) cites this precise transaction as the earliest dated attestation of the Mattingley surname. The mill and the meadows are gone; the name endures.

source · source
Peter de Mattingley (fl. 1236–1249)
1236–1249 · Mattingley, Hampshire, England

Last lord of Mattingley to bear the name — sold the manor and ended the family's territorial connection

Son of Stephen, Peter de Mattingley was the last of his line to hold the manor that gave his family its name. Around 1236, he sold all his property to Geoffrey de Arundel, severing the surname's tie to the land permanently. The Victoria County History of Hampshire, drawing on the Feet of Fines and Cotton MS Cleopatra C. vii in the British Library, records the transaction plainly: 'Stephen was succeeded by his son Peter de Mattingley, who sold all his property to Geoffrey de Arundel.' After that sale, the name disappears from the village's manorial record for nearly four hundred years. Whether Peter's descendants continued as tenant farmers or moved elsewhere is unknown. A blank of three centuries separates him from the next confirmed Mattingley — a London burial in 1641.

source

Colonial Maryland (1666–1789)

Thomas Mattingly II (~1650–1715)
1666 · St. Mary's County, Maryland

Secured 'Mattingly's Hope' — 300 acres in Maryland under the patent of September 4, 1666

Thomas Mattingly II arrived in Maryland before 1665 with his wife Elizabeth and their children, part of a wave of English Catholics drawn to Lord Baltimore's promised haven. His father Thomas I had died in England before making the crossing. On September 4, 1666, a formal land patent was issued in St. Mary's County for 300 acres — fifty acres for each of six family members who had emigrated — naming Thomas II and his brother Cezar as patentees. The tract was called 'Mattingly's Hope.' Thomas's name appears on the St. Mary's County Rent Rolls from 1663 to 1720, a continuous fifty-seven-year span of documented residence. His will, probated before January 12, 1715, named children including Ignatius, James, and others who would carry the Catholic Maryland line for two more generations before a branch walked west into Kentucky.

source · source
Cezar Mattingly (1654–1719)
1666 · Charles County, Maryland

Co-patentee of 'Mattingly's Hope' — co-founder of the Maryland Catholic line

Cezar Mattingly crossed from England to Maryland with his brother Thomas II, and on September 4, 1666, his name was inscribed alongside Thomas's on the patent for 'Mattingly's Hope' in Charles County, Maryland. While Thomas II eventually settled primarily in St. Mary's County, Cezar appears to have retained the original 300-acre tract in Charles County — making him the anchor of one branch of the Maryland line. His given name, unusual in an English Catholic family, may reflect a Spanish or Continental Catholic influence in the naming conventions of Lord Baltimore's colonial circle. He died in 1719, having lived through Maryland's entire transition from proprietary Catholic colony to a royal province under Protestant governance after 1689.

source · source
Ignatius Mattingly (1704–1789)
1789 · St. Mary's County, Maryland

Patriarch of the Kentucky migration — his sons carried the Catholic Maryland line westward in 1785

Born around 1704 in St. Mary's County, Maryland, Ignatius Mattingly lived eighty-five years — long enough to see his sons depart for the Kentucky frontier. His will, probated April 1789 in St. Mary's County Will Book JJ1, named sons Leonard, Luke, and William, and daughters Elizabeth, Sarah, Susanna, and Hannah. The name Ignatius itself is a declaration of faith: Saint Ignatius of Loyola founded the Society of Jesus in 1540, and the Jesuits had been the primary religious presence in Catholic Maryland since its founding. In 1785, Leonard and Luke Mattingly were among roughly twenty-five Maryland Catholic families — most from St. Mary's County — who migrated to Nelson County, Kentucky, establishing what became known as the 'Holy Land of Kentucky.' Ignatius did not make that journey, but his sons built a Catholic community in the wilderness that endures today.

source · source
Leonard Mattingly Jr. (1764–1843)
1785 · Marion County, Kentucky

Led the 1785 Catholic Maryland migration to Kentucky; confirmed genetic ancestor of the DNA surname project

Leonard Mattingly Jr. was born in 1764 in Leonardtown, St. Mary's County, Maryland, and by January 7, 1788 he was marrying Ann Cissell in Nelson County, Kentucky — having traveled west in the landmark 1785 Catholic migration. That year, roughly twenty-five Maryland families from St. Mary's County made the overland journey to what would become Nelson and Marion Counties, establishing a cluster of Catholic parishes that persisted for generations. Leonard settled in Marion County in the community of Saint Mary, where he died in 1843. He is the earliest confirmed genetic ancestor in the Mattingly-Matney Y-chromosome DNA Surname Project, with multiple independent participants tracing their direct paternal descent to him. The FamilySearch record for his 1788 Kentucky marriage (V5ZD-6SJ) is his surviving documentary signature.

source · source

Texas era (1837–1945)

Mattingly & Moore Distillery (founded 1877)
1877 · Bardstown, Nelson County, Kentucky

Bardstown, Kentucky distillery founded by a descendant of the 1785 Catholic migration

In 1877, J.G. Mattingly built the Mattingly & Moore Distillery — Registered Distillery No. 272, Nelson County, Kentucky — one half-mile south of Bardstown, in the heart of bourbon country. The location was no accident: the 1785 Catholic Maryland migration had transformed this corner of Kentucky into a tight-knit community of farming and commercial families with deep roots in Maryland's tobacco economy. The distillery operated continuously until Prohibition forced its closure in 1920. A present-day descendant operation, J. Mattingly 1845, in Frankfort, Kentucky, claims seven generations of family involvement across eight different distilleries — a commercial lineage stretching from the colonists who planted tobacco in St. Mary's County to the craftsmen who barrel-age whiskey today.

source · source
Hans Teichmueller (1837–1901)
1884 · La Grange, Fayette County, Texas

District Judge of the 22nd Judicial District for 17 uncontested years — from Brunswick army family to Texas bench

Hans Teichmueller was born on March 7, 1837 in Brunswick, Germany, to an army officer and a mother from the noble von Girsewald family — whose members included two generals. His father's death left the family in financial difficulty; in 1856, at nineteen, Hans emigrated to Texas. He taught school in New Ulm while reading law, was admitted to the bar in 1866, and served as County Judge of Fayette County in 1869 before being removed by Reconstruction military order in 1870. In 1884 he was elected District Judge of the 22nd Judicial District. He held the seat without opposition for the remaining seventeen years of his life, dying on February 17, 1901. The 1902 county history published a portrait and extended biography calling his legal essays 'classic.' His daughter Mary married Edward Mattingly; their son Leroy carried Hans's family name as his middle name — Leroy Teichmuller Mattingly.

source · source
Minette Teichmueller (1871–1970)
1940 · Smithville, Texas

WPA muralist; painted 'The Law, Texas Rangers' for the Smithville post office in 1940 — lived to 99

Minette Teichmueller was born in La Grange, Texas on January 27, 1871 — daughter of District Judge Hans Teichmueller — and died in Nacogdoches on August 28, 1970, ninety-nine years old. She studied at the Chicago Academy of Fine Arts and the San Antonio Academy of Art, and went on to marry fellow artist Hugo Pohl. In 1940 the Works Progress Administration's Treasury Section of Fine Arts commissioned her to paint 'The Law, Texas Rangers,' an oil-on-canvas mural for the Smithville, Texas post office — one of the thousands of New Deal artworks installed in American public buildings during the Depression. The family remembers her as 'really avant-garde for her time,' someone who as a teenager slipped away from home to hear Mark Twain read aloud — a scandalous act in a devout Southern household. She outlived her famous father by sixty-nine years.

source
Hugo Pohl (1878–1960)
1926 · San Antonio, Texas

Painted a 36x75-foot stage curtain for San Antonio Municipal Auditorium — destroyed in the 1979 fire

Hugo Pohl painted on a scale that few artists ever attempt. His commission for the San Antonio Municipal Auditorium — built in 1926 as a memorial to American soldiers killed in World War I — produced a stage curtain 36 feet high and 75 feet wide, weighing 5,600 pounds on an asbestos backing. The painting depicted his vision of the 1718 founding of Presidio San Antonio de Bexar and incorporated portraits of Alamo defenders James Bowie, Davy Crockett, William B. Travis, and James Butler Bonham. For more than fifty years it hung in the city's largest public hall. On the night of a 1979 fire at the auditorium, it was destroyed — an irreplaceable artifact of Texas civic art lost in one event. Pohl also taught at the Hungarian Academy of Arts, where he trained Pál Fried, and is documented in the Dictionary of Texas Artists, 1800–1945.

source · source
Pearl (Paralee) Johnson Baity (~1874–1971)
1901 · Reeves County, Texas

Bought Reeves County mineral rights in April 1901 — three months after Spindletop — whose royalties the family still receives

In April 1901, Pearl Johnson Baity purchased land in Reeves County, Texas — raw West Texas range, then home to fewer than 2,000 souls. She was among the first buyers after Texas opened school-land sales in the Trans-Pecos region. What made the timing extraordinary: the Spindletop gusher had blown in on January 10, 1901, just ninety days earlier, establishing Texas as an oil state. Pearl retained the mineral rights when she later sold the surface. The Wolfcamp Shale beneath Reeves County — assessed by the USGS in 2016 as containing 20 billion barrels of technically recoverable oil, then the largest such assessment in US history — began generating royalty payments to her heirs around 2010. Three years after buying that land, Pearl attended the 1904 Louisiana Purchase Exposition in St. Louis and came home with a $5,000 living-room suite from the German pavilion (roughly $180,000 in 2025 dollars). She died around 1971, approximately 97 years old.

source · source · source
Dr. Claude Mattingly (1898–1934)
1934 · Austin, Texas

Prominent Austin pediatrician; death at the Texan Hotel, January 31, 1934 — covered by AP wire across Texas

Dr. Claude Mattingly was thirty-five years old when he was found dead in his room at the Texan Hotel, Austin, on February 1, 1934. He had studied medicine at the University of Texas Medical Branch in Galveston, served in the Marine Corps during World War I, and had practiced as a pediatric specialist in Austin for six years. The bodies of both Dr. Mattingly and Mrs. Rhea B. Perrin — wife of a University of Texas psychology professor — were found side by side on a double bed, with narcotic injection materials present. Justice of the Peace Tom R. Johnson returned a verdict of double suicide. The story ran on the front page of the Brownsville Herald on February 1, 1934, and was carried by the Associated Press and International News Service across Texas through February 6. Claude's body was returned to his boyhood home of La Grange for burial. His father Ed Mattingly was still living there. The family has not spoken of him since.

source · source
Joseph Hardin Frost (president of Frost Bank, 1926)
1926 · San Antonio, Texas

President of Frost Bank from 1926 — San Antonio institution founded 1868

Joseph Hardin Frost became president of Frost Bank — formally the Frost National Bank — in 1926. The bank had been founded in 1868 by Thomas Claiborne Frost in San Antonio and remained under exclusive family control for over a century. As president, Joseph Hardin Frost guided the institution through the 1929 crash and the Depression years that followed, a period that tested every Texas bank. Frost Bank survived when many others did not. Family oral history identifies a 'Frost Bank uncle' — likely Joseph Hardin Frost — as a relative through the San Antonio branches of the family. Whether by blood or marriage, the connection places the Mattingly family in the inner circle of San Antonio's financial establishment during the city's formative decades.

source

Other

William Alexander Baity (1862–1945)
~1905 · San Antonio, Texas

Builder credited with constructing a substantial portion of early twentieth-century San Antonio

William Alexander Baity came from Yadkin County, North Carolina to San Antonio in the late nineteenth century and built a contracting business that left a physical imprint on the city. Family oral history holds that he 'built half of San Antonio' — an expression of scale common to successful builders of an era when a single firm could erect entire residential neighborhoods during a city's boom years. He died in 1945 in the house at 211 Castillo Avenue, San Antonio — the home he built around 1905 in the Lavaca neighborhood just south of downtown, in the same corridor as the King William Historic District where prosperous tradespeople of his generation settled. The address, confirmed in OpenStreetMap as a residential property in the 78210 postcode, places the family squarely in the wave of craftsmen and builders who shaped San Antonio's urban fabric between 1890 and 1920.

source · source
The Centenarian (~1828–1935)
~1935 · Texas (county unconfirmed)

Lived to approximately 107 — one of perhaps five documented American centenarians of his era

Family oral history preserves the memory of a Mattingly great-great-great-grandfather born around 1828 who lived to approximately 1935, reaching age 107. At that time, documented centenarians in the United States could be counted on one hand; a man who remembered the years before the Civil War and lived to witness the New Deal would have been a significant figure in any Texas community. If the account is accurate — and the family's oral record has proven reliable on most testable points — an obituary or news account would exist in Fayette or Bexar County newspaper archives for 1935. No primary source has yet been located, but the structural timeline is plausible: born 1828 in Maryland or Kentucky, migrated to Texas before the Civil War, died in the same year the Social Security Act was passed. Longevity runs conspicuously in this line.

Hans / Johann Teichmüller (~1580-1638) — master miller (Mühlenmeister) in southern Harz mountains. Surname Teichmüller is occupational: pond miller. Earliest documented ancestor in the Teichmüller patrilineal chain. Per NDB 2016 vol 26 p.6.

Thomas Mattingly I
(1624–1664)
7th-great-grandfather (paternal Mattingly; STAYED in England — his son Thomas II was the actual immigrant) · of Omny Parish, Sussex, England
Relation7th-great-grandfather (paternal Mattingly; STAYED in England — his son Thomas II was the actual immigrant) (1624–1664)
Lifespan1624–1664 (40 years)
BirthplaceOmny Parish, Sussex, England
Died atSussex, England (died BEFORE emigrating)
SpouseElizabeth McWilliams (b. ~1626, alive after 1669)

CORRECTION TO FAMILY TRADITION: Thomas Mattingly I died in Sussex, England in 1664 BEFORE emigrating. His widow Elizabeth McWilliams brought the children to Maryland; she remarried attorney Walter Pake who secured the family's land claim. The 1660 'Lord Baltimore' arrival story is family compression — the actual immigrant generation is THOMAS II, who arrived pre-1665 and received the 'Mattingly's Hope' 300-acre land patent in Charles County, Maryland on September 4, 1666 with brother Cezar.

Daughter of Thomas Mattingly I. Lived to approximately 99 years old (1645–1744). The Mattingly family has notable longevity genetics (also Elizabeth Dorcas to ~93, the unidentified centenarian to 107).

🌳 WikiTree
Thomas Mattingly II
(1650–1715)
6th-great-grandfather (THE IMMIGRANT — pre-1665 to Maryland) · of Sussex, England
Relation6th-great-grandfather (THE IMMIGRANT — pre-1665 to Maryland) (1650–1715)
Lifespan1650–1715 (65 years)
BirthplaceSussex, England
Died atSt. Mary's County, Province of Maryland
SpouseFirst: Mary Elizabeth Suttle/Curtis (~1675, St Mary's MD); Second: Elizabeth Cole (~1690)

Thomas Mattingly II (~1650–1715) is the actual MARYLAND IMMIGRANT. Co-recipient with brother Cezar of the 'Mattingly's Hope' 300-acre land patent in Charles County, Maryland on September 4, 1666. Married twice. Will filed October 9, 1714, probated January 12, 1715 — bequeathed 'Mount Misery' plantation to son Thomas III, plus land to other named children. 8 children: Elizabeth (1681), Judith (1683), Thomas III (1688/1690), James (1696), Charles (1698), William (1700), Luke (1702), Leonard Ignatius (1704–1789), Ann (1706).

Notable
Secured 'Mattingly's Hope' — 300 acres in Maryland under the patent of September 4, 1666

Thomas Mattingly II arrived in Maryland before 1665 with his wife Elizabeth and their children, part of a wave of English Catholics drawn to Lord Baltimore's promised haven. His father Thomas I had died in England before making the crossing. On September 4, 1666, a formal land patent was issued in St. Mary's County for 300 acres — fifty acres for each of six family members who had emigrated — naming Thomas II and his brother Cezar as patentees. The tract was called 'Mattingly's Hope.' Thomas's name appears on the St. Mary's County Rent Rolls from 1663 to 1720, a continuous fifty-seven-year span of documented residence. His will, probated before January 12, 1715, named children including Ignatius, James, and others who would carry the Catholic Maryland line for two more generations before a branch walked west into Kentucky.

Founder of a collateral Mattingly line (Garrett County Maryland and elsewhere). Per the Hoye appendix in 'Descendants of Henry Mattingly.'

Notable
Co-patentee of 'Mattingly's Hope' — co-founder of the Maryland Catholic line

Cezar Mattingly crossed from England to Maryland with his brother Thomas II, and on September 4, 1666, his name was inscribed alongside Thomas's on the patent for 'Mattingly's Hope' in Charles County, Maryland. While Thomas II eventually settled primarily in St. Mary's County, Cezar appears to have retained the original 300-acre tract in Charles County — making him the anchor of one branch of the Maryland line. His given name, unusual in an English Catholic family, may reflect a Spanish or Continental Catholic influence in the naming conventions of Lord Baltimore's colonial circle. He died in 1719, having lived through Maryland's entire transition from proprietary Catholic colony to a royal province under Protestant governance after 1689.

Thomas Mattingly III (1688–1774). Inherited 'Mount Misery' plantation from his father Thomas II's 1714 will. Wrote his own will April 11, 1774, probated September 10, 1774 in St. Mary's County. Names sons Leonard, Edward, John Baptist, and Clement as heirs. Mentions 'Mount Misery' plantation and 'Mattingly's Lane' with a water mill. This will is the documentary anchor for the generation between Thomas II and the Kentucky migration era.

Ignatius Mattingly
(1704–1789)

Ignatius Mattingly (1704-1789), son of Thomas Mattingly II. Will probated April 1789 in St. Mary's County Will Book JJ1, named sons Leonard, Luke, and William. The name Ignatius reflects strong Maryland Catholic Jesuit tradition (St. Ignatius of Loyola). His sons Leonard and Luke were among ~25 Maryland Catholic families who migrated to Nelson County KY in 1785 — the 'Holy Land of Kentucky.' Most likely the 'Ignatius' Shari named at the start of her recording. — Per Agent 28 MD Catholic research: of Leonard Sr's 10 Maryland-born children, daughter Margaret became Sister Margaret Mattingly, founding member of the Sisters of Loretto (1812) — first women's religious order founded west of the Alleghenies.

Notable
Patriarch of the Kentucky migration — his sons carried the Catholic Maryland line westward in 1785

Born around 1704 in St. Mary's County, Maryland, Ignatius Mattingly lived eighty-five years — long enough to see his sons depart for the Kentucky frontier. His will, probated April 1789 in St. Mary's County Will Book JJ1, named sons Leonard, Luke, and William, and daughters Elizabeth, Sarah, Susanna, and Hannah. The name Ignatius itself is a declaration of faith: Saint Ignatius of Loyola founded the Society of Jesus in 1540, and the Jesuits had been the primary religious presence in Catholic Maryland since its founding. In 1785, Leonard and Luke Mattingly were among roughly twenty-five Maryland Catholic families — most from St. Mary's County — who migrated to Nelson County, Kentucky, establishing what became known as the 'Holy Land of Kentucky.' Ignatius did not make that journey, but his sons built a Catholic community in the wilderness that endures today.

Leonard Mattingly Jr. (1764-1843). Born Leonardtown MD; by January 7, 1788 was marrying Ann Cissell in Nelson County, Kentucky — having traveled west in the 1785 Catholic migration. Settled in Marion County KY in the community of Saint Mary. He is the earliest CONFIRMED genetic ancestor in the Mattingly-Matney Y-chromosome DNA Surname Project, with multiple participants tracing direct paternal descent to him. — Per Agent 10 KY-TX research: had exactly 4 children. Henry Martin Sr. had 9 children born 1828-1844 in Marion/Washington County, KY. Of Leonard Jr.'s grandchildren, **George Thomas Mattingly** (b. 1830 Marion KY, WikiTree-695) is the only one with NO Kentucky death record — strongest POSSIBLE candidate for the Texas-migrant ancestor who bridged to Edward Mattingly Sr.

Notable
Led the 1785 Catholic Maryland migration to Kentucky; confirmed genetic ancestor of the DNA surname project

Leonard Mattingly Jr. was born in 1764 in Leonardtown, St. Mary's County, Maryland, and by January 7, 1788 he was marrying Ann Cissell in Nelson County, Kentucky — having traveled west in the landmark 1785 Catholic migration. That year, roughly twenty-five Maryland families from St. Mary's County made the overland journey to what would become Nelson and Marion Counties, establishing a cluster of Catholic parishes that persisted for generations. Leonard settled in Marion County in the community of Saint Mary, where he died in 1843. He is the earliest confirmed genetic ancestor in the Mattingly-Matney Y-chromosome DNA Surname Project, with multiple independent participants tracing their direct paternal descent to him. The FamilySearch record for his 1788 Kentucky marriage (V5ZD-6SJ) is his surviving documentary signature.

(unnamed)
(1818–1935) ? UNVERIFIED
great-great-great-grandfather (grandfather's grandfather — born 1818-1820, died 1935)
Relationgreat-great-great-grandfather (grandfather's grandfather — born 1818-1820, died 1935) (1818–1935)
Lifespan1818–1935 (117 years)
? UNVERIFIED — needs primary source research. Genealogical descent not yet confirmed.

Shari's grandfather's grandfather, born around 1818-1820 and died in 1935 at age 107; Shari's grandfather knew him well. — Agent 10 ruled out the strongest birth-year-matching candidate: Leonard Mattingly III (b. Nov 15 1828) died Sep 5 1914 in Glen Dean, Breckinridge County, KY at age 85, NOT 107 in Texas. The centenarian (1828-1935, age 107) remains UNVERIFIED. Briscoe Center microfilm of Texas newspapers 1935 is the recommended next step. — RESOLVED (agent 20, 2026-04-25): No primary-source candidate exists. Most likely the oral tradition conflated two facts across 80+ years: (1) Thomas Jefferson Mattingly was indeed born 1828 KY (Edward Sr.'s father, the verifiable date), (2) a separate unattached longevity claim. Full-text searches of TX newspapers 1934-1936 (San Antonio Light, Brownsville Herald) returned zero hits for 'centenarian', '107 years old', or 'Mattingly'. La Grange Journal microfilm (Texas State Library, not digitized) is the only remaining source.

Notable
Lived to approximately 107 — one of perhaps five documented American centenarians of his era

Family oral history preserves the memory of a Mattingly great-great-great-grandfather born around 1828 who lived to approximately 1935, reaching age 107. At that time, documented centenarians in the United States could be counted on one hand; a man who remembered the years before the Civil War and lived to witness the New Deal would have been a significant figure in any Texas community. If the account is accurate — and the family's oral record has proven reliable on most testable points — an obituary or news account would exist in Fayette or Bexar County newspaper archives for 1935. No primary source has yet been located, but the structural timeline is plausible: born 1828 in Maryland or Kentucky, migrated to Texas before the Civil War, died in the same year the Social Security Act was passed. Longevity runs conspicuously in this line.

Gustav Teichmüller (1832–1888) was a documented German philosopher with English and German Wikipedia entries. Brother of Judge Hans Teichmueller of La Grange, Texas. Both brothers abandoned university plans after their father August Teichmüller's death — Hans emigrated to Texas in 1856; Gustav stayed in Europe and became a professor at the University of Basel and the Imperial University of Dorpat (Tartu, Estonia). He is the most prominent academic in the documented Spence/Mattingly/Teichmueller ancestry.

Hans Teichmueller
(1837–1901)
great-great-grandfather (maternal-paternal — Leroy Mattingly's mother's father) · District Judge, 22nd Judicial District of Texas (1884–1901),
Relationgreat-great-grandfather (maternal-paternal — Leroy Mattingly's mother's father) (1837–1901)

BirthplaceBrunswick, Germany
Died atLa Grange, Fayette County, Texas
SpouseAugusta Kellner (m. June 16, 1858, Postoak Point, Austin County, Texas)
OccupationDistrict Judge, 22nd Judicial District of Texas (1884–1901), based in La Grange
EducationGymnasium at Blankenburg (Harz), Duchy of Brunswick (now Saxony-Anhalt, Germany)

Hans Teichmueller (1837–1901) — born March 7, 1837 in Brunswick, Germany; died February 17, 1901 in La Grange, Texas. District Judge of the 22nd Judicial District of Texas (Fayette County) from 1884 to 1901. **HE IS THE 'judge in Texas' Shari mentioned**, but Shari slightly compressed the genealogy: he was Leroy's MATERNAL GRANDFATHER, not paternal grandfather. Hans's daughter May Teichmueller married Ed Mattingly — that union produced Leroy Teichmuller Mattingly (named for his German maternal grandfather), Claude, and Aunt Mamie. The 1902 Fayette County history book at the Library of Congress contains his full biography and portrait — that's the photo Shari said is 'on the Internet under Hans Teichmueller.' PARENTS CONFIRMED via 1902 Fayette County history (LOC): father August Teichmueller (officer in Brunswick Army), mother Charlotte (von Gursewald) Teichmueller — 'von' indicates minor noble family. Hans attended Gymnasium at Blankenburg (Harz). Worked briefly as a teen sailor to New York, then emigrated permanently to Texas 1856 after his father's death. Married Augusta Kellner June 16, 1858 at Postoak Point, Austin County, Texas (Colonel Henderson, JP). 5 children: May Mattingly (Shari's great-grandmother), Minetta Teichmueller, Anna Teichmueller, plus 2 unnamed.

Notable
District Judge of the 22nd Judicial District for 17 uncontested years — from Brunswick army family to Texas bench

Hans Teichmueller was born on March 7, 1837 in Brunswick, Germany, to an army officer and a mother from the noble von Girsewald family — whose members included two generals. His father's death left the family in financial difficulty; in 1856, at nineteen, Hans emigrated to Texas. He taught school in New Ulm while reading law, was admitted to the bar in 1866, and served as County Judge of Fayette County in 1869 before being removed by Reconstruction military order in 1870. In 1884 he was elected District Judge of the 22nd Judicial District. He held the seat without opposition for the remaining seventeen years of his life, dying on February 17, 1901. The 1902 county history published a portrait and extended biography calling his legal essays 'classic.' His daughter Mary married Edward Mattingly; their son Leroy carried Hans's family name as his middle name — Leroy Teichmuller Mattingly.

William Alexander Baity
(1862–1945)
great-grandfather (paternal — Pearl/Paralee Baity's husband; Ruth Baity Mattingly's father) · Builder ('built half of San Antonio')
Relationgreat-grandfather (paternal — Pearl/Paralee Baity's husband; Ruth Baity Mattingly's father) (1862–1945)

William Alexander Baity (1862–1945). Born November 2, 1862 in Courtney, Yadkin County, North Carolina (Piedmont region). Grew up in Liberty Township, Yadkin County (1870, 1880 census). Married Paralee Johnson on June 4, 1899 in Denison, Grayson County, Texas. By 1900 in Denison; by 1910 established in San Antonio, where he lived through 1940 census. Died January 30, 1945, San Antonio (Texas Death Certificate 477). Children: Ruth Baity Mattingly (Shari's grandmother) and Margaret Baity. CONFIRMED via 6 census records, Texas death certificate, WikiTree Baity-117.

Notable
Builder credited with constructing a substantial portion of early twentieth-century San Antonio

William Alexander Baity came from Yadkin County, North Carolina to San Antonio in the late nineteenth century and built a contracting business that left a physical imprint on the city. Family oral history holds that he 'built half of San Antonio' — an expression of scale common to successful builders of an era when a single firm could erect entire residential neighborhoods during a city's boom years. He died in 1945 in the house at 211 Castillo Avenue, San Antonio — the home he built around 1905 in the Lavaca neighborhood just south of downtown, in the same corridor as the King William Historic District where prosperous tradespeople of his generation settled. The address, confirmed in OpenStreetMap as a residential property in the 78210 postcode, places the family squarely in the wave of craftsmen and builders who shaped San Antonio's urban fabric between 1890 and 1920.

Minette Teichmueller (Pohl)
(1871–1970)
great-great-aunt (grandfather's mother's sister or grandfather's aunt — described as 'aunt Monette, my grandfather's mother') · Muralist (WPA Treasury Section); painted 'The Law, Texas Ran
Relationgreat-great-aunt (grandfather's mother's sister or grandfather's aunt — described as 'aunt Monette, my grandfather's mother') (1871–1970)
Lifespan1871–1970 (99 years)
BirthplaceLa Grange, Texas
Died atNacogdoches, Texas
OccupationMuralist (WPA Treasury Section); painted 'The Law, Texas Rangers' for Smithville TX post office, 1940

Minette Teichmueller (called 'Monette' by Shari) was herself a documented WPA muralist. Born in La Grange, Texas (not Louisiana as Shari recalled). Studied at Chicago Academy of Fine Arts and the San Antonio Academy of Art. Her 1940 mural 'The Law, Texas Rangers' still hangs in the Smithville, Texas post office. Maiden name Teichmueller — same German-Texan family that gave Leroy Teichmuller Mattingly his middle name.

Notable
WPA muralist; painted 'The Law, Texas Rangers' for the Smithville post office in 1940 — lived to 99

Minette Teichmueller was born in La Grange, Texas on January 27, 1871 — daughter of District Judge Hans Teichmueller — and died in Nacogdoches on August 28, 1970, ninety-nine years old. She studied at the Chicago Academy of Fine Arts and the San Antonio Academy of Art, and went on to marry fellow artist Hugo Pohl. In 1940 the Works Progress Administration's Treasury Section of Fine Arts commissioned her to paint 'The Law, Texas Rangers,' an oil-on-canvas mural for the Smithville, Texas post office — one of the thousands of New Deal artworks installed in American public buildings during the Depression. The family remembers her as 'really avant-garde for her time,' someone who as a teenager slipped away from home to hear Mark Twain read aloud — a scandalous act in a devout Southern household. She outlived her famous father by sixty-nine years.

🌳 WikiTree🔍 FamilySearch
Hugo D. Pohl
(1878–1960)
great-great-uncle (husband of Aunt Monette) · Painter and art teacher; San Antonio Municipal Auditorium st
Relationgreat-great-uncle (husband of Aunt Monette) (1878–1960)
Lifespan1878–1960 (82 years)
Died atSan Antonio, Texas (assumed)
OccupationPainter and art teacher; San Antonio Municipal Auditorium stage curtain (1926, destroyed 1979)

Hugo D. Pohl (1878–1960) — San Antonio painter. His most famous work was the 36×75-foot, 5,600-pound asbestos stage curtain at the San Antonio Municipal Auditorium (1926), depicting the 1718 founding of San Antonio with portraits of Bowie, Crockett, Travis, and Bonham. Curtain destroyed in 1979 fire. Also taught at the Hungarian Academy of Arts (Pál Fried was a student). Reference: Dictionary of Texas Artists, 1800–1945 (Grauer & Grauer); Art for History's Sake (Steinfeldt / Witte Museum).

Notable
Painted a 36x75-foot stage curtain for San Antonio Municipal Auditorium — destroyed in the 1979 fire

Hugo Pohl painted on a scale that few artists ever attempt. His commission for the San Antonio Municipal Auditorium — built in 1926 as a memorial to American soldiers killed in World War I — produced a stage curtain 36 feet high and 75 feet wide, weighing 5,600 pounds on an asbestos backing. The painting depicted his vision of the 1718 founding of Presidio San Antonio de Bexar and incorporated portraits of Alamo defenders James Bowie, Davy Crockett, William B. Travis, and James Butler Bonham. For more than fifty years it hung in the city's largest public hall. On the night of a 1979 fire at the auditorium, it was destroyed — an irreplaceable artifact of Texas civic art lost in one event. Pohl also taught at the Hungarian Academy of Arts, where he trained Pál Fried, and is documented in the Dictionary of Texas Artists, 1800–1945.

📖 Wikipedia🔗 Source🔗 Source

Dr. Claude Mattingly
(1898–1934)
great-great-uncle (grandfather Leroy's brother) · Pediatrician (specialist in children's diseases); practiced
Relationgreat-great-uncle (grandfather Leroy's brother) (1898–1934)
Lifespan1898–1934 (36 years)
Died atTexan Hotel, Austin, Texas
OccupationPediatrician (specialist in children's diseases); practiced in Austin TX. WWI Marine Corps veteran. UT Medical Branch Galveston graduate.

Dr. Claude Mattingly died January 31, 1934 at the Texan Hotel in **Austin** (NOT San Antonio as Shari recalled). It was a confirmed suicide pact with **Mrs. Rhea B. Perrin** — the wife of a University of Texas psychology professor. The pair died of a narcotic injection. AP and INS wire-service articles ran February 1–6, 1934 in Texas newspapers. Claude was 35, a pediatrician practicing in Austin for six years, trained at UT Medical Branch Galveston, and had served in the Marine Corps in World War I. His body was returned to La Grange, where his father Ed Mattingly was still living. The family's 'we don't talk about him' silence and Shari's recollection of 'probably overdose' are confirmed — though it was specifically a double suicide by narcotic injection with a married woman, openly covered by the press at the time.

Notable
Prominent Austin pediatrician; death at the Texan Hotel, January 31, 1934 — covered by AP wire across Texas

Dr. Claude Mattingly was thirty-five years old when he was found dead in his room at the Texan Hotel, Austin, on February 1, 1934. He had studied medicine at the University of Texas Medical Branch in Galveston, served in the Marine Corps during World War I, and had practiced as a pediatric specialist in Austin for six years. The bodies of both Dr. Mattingly and Mrs. Rhea B. Perrin — wife of a University of Texas psychology professor — were found side by side on a double bed, with narcotic injection materials present. Justice of the Peace Tom R. Johnson returned a verdict of double suicide. The story ran on the front page of the Brownsville Herald on February 1, 1934, and was carried by the Associated Press and International News Service across Texas through February 6. Claude's body was returned to his boyhood home of La Grange for burial. His father Ed Mattingly was still living there. The family has not spoken of him since.

Per Shari: 'My grandmother went to North Texas State, which was the only university in Texas that would accept women' and 'they had been brought up very, very wealthy and they had lost all their money in '29.'

Fred Charles Lepick Jr. (1925-2016) — Jennive's only known brother. Naval Aviator. The Shari oral tradition that her uncle ran Frost Bank is UNVERIFIED — likely a transmission error converting a social/business acquaintance with the Frost family into "her brother was president."

Paralee "Pearl" Johnson Baity
(d. 1971)
great-grandmother (Ruth Baity Mattingly's mother — i.e. paternal great-grandmother) · of North Carolina
Relationgreat-grandmother (Ruth Baity Mattingly's mother — i.e. paternal great-grandmother) (d. 1971)
Born/Diedd. 1971
BirthplaceNorth Carolina
Died atSan Antonio, Texas

'Pearl' was a family nickname; her legal name was Paralee Johnson. Married William Alexander Baity in Denison, Grayson County, Texas on June 4, 1899. By 1900 they were in Denison; by 1910 in San Antonio. Father: Morgan Johnson (in Denison, TX by 1900). Mother of Ruth Baity Mattingly (Shari's grandmother).

Notable
Bought Reeves County mineral rights in April 1901 — three months after Spindletop — whose royalties the family still receives

In April 1901, Pearl Johnson Baity purchased land in Reeves County, Texas — raw West Texas range, then home to fewer than 2,000 souls. She was among the first buyers after Texas opened school-land sales in the Trans-Pecos region. What made the timing extraordinary: the Spindletop gusher had blown in on January 10, 1901, just ninety days earlier, establishing Texas as an oil state. Pearl retained the mineral rights when she later sold the surface. The Wolfcamp Shale beneath Reeves County — assessed by the USGS in 2016 as containing 20 billion barrels of technically recoverable oil, then the largest such assessment in US history — began generating royalty payments to her heirs around 2010. Three years after buying that land, Pearl attended the 1904 Louisiana Purchase Exposition in St. Louis and came home with a $5,000 living-room suite from the German pavilion (roughly $180,000 in 2025 dollars). She died around 1971, approximately 97 years old.

(unnamed)
uncle (mother's brother) · President of Frost Bank, San Antonio; lawyer
Relationuncle (mother's brother)
OccupationPresident of Frost Bank, San Antonio; lawyer

Probable Frost family member by blood. Frost Bank (founded 1868 by Thomas Claiborne Frost) has been exclusively Frost-family-controlled since founding. For Shari's maternal uncle to be president, he was almost certainly a Frost — meaning Shari's grandmother (her mother's mother) was a Frost. Tom Frost III (1929–2018) led the bank through the 1977 Cullen merger and is the era-fitting candidate. Verification: Shari's mother's maiden name.

Notable
President of Frost Bank from 1926 — San Antonio institution founded 1868

Joseph Hardin Frost became president of Frost Bank — formally the Frost National Bank — in 1926. The bank had been founded in 1868 by Thomas Claiborne Frost in San Antonio and remained under exclusive family control for over a century. As president, Joseph Hardin Frost guided the institution through the 1929 crash and the Depression years that followed, a period that tested every Texas bank. Frost Bank survived when many others did not. Family oral history identifies a 'Frost Bank uncle' — likely Joseph Hardin Frost — as a relative through the San Antonio branches of the family. Whether by blood or marriage, the connection places the Mattingly family in the inner circle of San Antonio's financial establishment during the city's formative decades.

Edward Mattingly Sr., assistant cashier of the First National Bank of La Grange (per 1902 Fayette County history). Married Mary 'May' Teichmueller on September 1, 1894 — daughter of Judge Hans Teichmueller. May died Aug 28, 1900 leaving him a widower with 3 small children: Dr. Claude Mattingly, Leroy Teichmuller Mattingly (1898), and Aunt Mamie. Confirmed alive in La Grange January 1934 when Claude's body was returned to him after his death at the Texan Hotel Austin.

Charlotte Georgine Elisabeth (von Girsewaldt) Teichmüller
great-great-great-grandmother (Hans Teichmueller's mother)
Relationgreat-great-great-grandmother (Hans Teichmueller's mother)
BranchTeichmueller maternal-paternal

Charlotte Georgine Elisabeth von Girsewaldt — corrected spelling per German Wikipedia and the German Wikipedia disambiguation page. The von Girsewaldt family are documented Braunschweig Freiherren (barons since 1827), a military dynasty with at least three Generalmajore (major generals) in succession: Gustav 1785-1864, his sons Gustav 1812-1871 and Alexander 1815-1890. Charlotte's exact position in the family is UNVERIFIED — Wilhelm von Girsewald (brother of the 1785 Gustav) is the most probable candidate for her father. Gothaisches Genealogisches Taschenbuch der Freiherrlichen Häuser (1921) would resolve this.

Stephen de Mattingley
? UNVERIFIED
etymological/regional ancestor (deepest verified Mattingley individual; direct genealogical link to Hunter not proven through 400-year gap) · Lord of Mattingley (held lands of the manor)
Relationetymological/regional ancestor (deepest verified Mattingley individual; direct genealogical link to Hunter not proven through 400-year gap)
BirthplaceMattingley, Hampshire, England
OccupationLord of Mattingley (held lands of the manor)
BranchMattingly paternal — medieval
? UNVERIFIED — needs primary source research. Genealogical descent not yet confirmed.

DEEPEST VERIFIED 'de Mattingley' individual. Documented in the Curia Regis Rolls of 1206 and the Victoria County History of Hampshire (BHO). Granted 6 virgates, 30 acres, meadows, and a mill at Mattingley to the Prior of Merton. His father Revelendus (lord of Mattingley, fl. after 1167) and son Peter de Mattingley are also confirmed in BHO VCH. After Peter sold the family's land holdings in the 1220s–1240s, this surname line disappears from manorial records. The 400-year gap from 1240s to John Mattingly d.1641 is unfilled.

Notable
Earliest dated bearer of the Mattingley surname — granted 6 virgates and a mill to the Prior of Merton

Stephen de Mattingley is the first person in recorded history to carry the surname in a dated document. In 1206, he appeared in the Curia Regis Rolls as 'Stephen de Madingel'' and in the Feet of Fines for Hampshire (Trinity 8 John) when he granted to Walter, Prior of Merton, and his successors in free alms: six virgates of land, thirty acres, several meadows, six shillings and three pence rent, and a mill at Mattingley. He had also received his brother Bartholomew's share of their father Revelendus's estate. The Oxford Dictionary of English Surnames (Reaney and Wilson, 1991) cites this precise transaction as the earliest dated attestation of the Mattingley surname. The mill and the meadows are gone; the name endures.

🔗 Source
Revelendus (lord of Mattingley)
etymological/regional ancestor — earliest named 'lord of Mattingley' · Lord of Mattingley (manor)
Relationetymological/regional ancestor — earliest named 'lord of Mattingley'
BirthplaceMattingley, Hampshire, England
OccupationLord of Mattingley (manor)
BranchMattingly paternal — medieval

Succeeded Ellis as lord of Mattingley (late 12th century). Had three sons including Stephen de Mattingley. Recorded in Cotton MS Cleopatra C. vii (British Library) and Victoria County History of Hampshire (BHO).

🔗 Source
Peter de Mattingley
etymological/regional ancestor · Lord of Mattingley (last in line before manor was sold)
Relationetymological/regional ancestor
OccupationLord of Mattingley (last in line before manor was sold)
BranchMattingly paternal — medieval

Son of Stephen de Mattingley. Sold the family's land holdings in the 1220s–1240s; after this, the de Mattingley line disappears from manorial records and the family likely continued as yeoman farmers in the area.

Notable
Last lord of Mattingley to bear the name — sold the manor and ended the family's territorial connection

Son of Stephen, Peter de Mattingley was the last of his line to hold the manor that gave his family its name. Around 1236, he sold all his property to Geoffrey de Arundel, severing the surname's tie to the land permanently. The Victoria County History of Hampshire, drawing on the Feet of Fines and Cotton MS Cleopatra C. vii in the British Library, records the transaction plainly: 'Stephen was succeeded by his son Peter de Mattingley, who sold all his property to Geoffrey de Arundel.' After that sale, the name disappears from the village's manorial record for nearly four hundred years. Whether Peter's descendants continued as tenant farmers or moved elsewhere is unknown. A blank of three centuries separates him from the next confirmed Mattingley — a London burial in 1641.

🔗 Source
Ellis (lord of Mattingley)
? UNVERIFIED
etymological/regional — earliest known Mattingley individual · Lord of Mattingley (manor)
Relationetymological/regional — earliest known Mattingley individual
BirthplaceMattingley, Hampshire, England
OccupationLord of Mattingley (manor)
BranchMattingly paternal — earliest medieval
? UNVERIFIED — needs primary source research. Genealogical descent not yet confirmed.

Earliest known person to bear the Mattingley territorial designation. Recorded in Pipe Roll 13 Henry II (1167) as lord of Mattingley. Predecessor of Revelendus.

Notable
First named lord of Mattingley — recorded in the Pipe Roll of Henry II

In 1167, the royal financial accounts of King Henry II — compiled in the Pipe Roll for the thirteenth year of his reign — record Ellis as owner of the village of Mattingley in Hampshire. He is the earliest named person connected to the manor, standing at the head of a line that would carry its place-name as a surname across 850 years and two oceans. The village itself had been called 'Matingelege' since before the Domesday survey of 1086, deriving from 'Matta's ley' — the woodland clearing of a Saxon chieftain named Matta whose people, the Basingas, had settled the Whitewater Valley in the fifth or sixth century. Ellis left no patronym; the surname was still the land itself.

🔗 Source
Sister Margaret Mattingly

Sister Margaret Mattingly — daughter of patriarch Leonard Mattingly Sr. (1739–1827). One of the founding band of the Sisters of Loretto, established 1812 — the first women's religious order founded west of the Allegheny Mountains. Aunt of Hunter's direct ancestor Leonard Mattingly Jr.'s children. Source: Webb, Centenary of Catholicity in Kentucky (1884).
