Wikipedia (Rh blood group system article): Basque country has 47.2% Rh-negative (lack of D antigen) — the highest globally. Wikipedia (blood type distribution table): Basques are 51% type O (ABO). If ~51% are type O and ~47.2% are Rh-negative, a rough estimate for O-negative in the Basque population is approximately 24–25%.

Verdict: PARTIALLY CONFIRMED with a correction. The Basque Rh-negative rate (~47%) is the world's highest, well confirmed. The O-negative specific figure Shari cited (~30%) is an overestimate; the actual O-negative frequency is closer to 24–25%. The underlying fact she's pointing to — Basque people having uniquely high rates of Rh-negativity — is correct.

Irish/Celtic O-negative rate

Wikipedia (blood type distribution table): Ireland O-negative = 8%, compared to a world average of approximately 7%. Ireland does have a slightly elevated O-negative rate, and O-positive is 47% (one of the highest nationally). This supports the broad claim that Celtic populations have elevated O-negative rates, though the difference from the global average is modest.

Verdict: PROBABLE — Ireland's 8% O-negative is somewhat elevated but not dramatically so.

Mitochondrial Eve age

Shari's claim: L haplogroup traces to "mitochondrial Eve 240,000 years ago."

Wikipedia (Mitochondrial Eve): as of 2013, estimates of the mt-MRCA age are around 155,000 years ago. The L haplogroup article gives the origin of L1'2'3'4'5'6 at approximately 170,000 ± 36,000 years ago (Soares et al. 2009). Haplogroup L0 arose between 200,000 and 130,000 years ago.

Verdict: OVERCLAIMED. The 240,000 figure is outside the consensus range. The corrected range is approximately 130,000–200,000 BP for Mitochondrial Eve, with the central estimate around 155,000 years ago.

Neanderthal and Denisovan interbreeding

Verdict: CONFIRMED. Neanderthal DNA in non-African modern humans (~1–4%) is firmly established. Denisovan DNA in Melanesian/Oceanian populations (~3–5%) is firmly established.

Homo erectus interbreeding

Shari's claim: modern humans also interbred with Homo erectus.

Wikipedia (Interbreeding between archaic and modern humans, Denisovan article): The Denisovan genome itself contains DNA from an unknown "ghost" archaic population whose divergence time is consistent with a H. erectus-like ancestor. There is also evidence of archaic introgression into African populations from an unidentified archaic source. However, direct H. erectus-to-modern-human gene flow has not been confirmed as a distinct event — the evidence is indirect and the source population is labeled "archaic/ghost" rather than specifically identified as H. erectus.

Verdict: SPECULATIVE — the underlying phenomenon (archaic ghost introgression) is real science, but the specific attribution to H. erectus is not yet confirmed. A fair way to state it: "modern humans may have interbred with H. erectus-related populations, as evidenced by ghost DNA in Denisovan and some African genomes."

---

Summary of Unresolved Mysteries

| Mystery | Status | Next Step |

|---|---|---|

| Claude Mattingly hotel death, exact date/hotel/woman's name | UNVERIFIED | Request microfilm search from Briscoe Center at UT Austin |

| Uncle Zay's full name and Stauffer role post-1967 | UNVERIFIED | SEC filings 1982–1984 list corporate officers; MIT alumni records |

| "We're from Ignatius" — ancestor name or recording artifact | POSSIBLE (ancestor) | Ask Shari directly |

| Frost Bank president uncle — family connection to Frost family | UNVERIFIED | Shari's mother's maiden name would resolve immediately |

| Uncle Billy Mattingly — Stanford geologist | UNVERIFIED | Stanford Alumni Association + AAPG member directories |

| Texas governor farm neighbor — which governor | POSSIBLE (Sul Ross) | Confirm family's central Texas settlement county |

| Mitochondrial Eve age | CORRECTED | ~155,000 BP, not 240,000 BP |

| Basque O-negative frequency | CORRECTED | ~24–25% O-negative, not ~30% — though Rh-negative overall is ~47% |

The highest-value unresolved item remains the Claude Mattingly story. Newspaper records of a prominent physician's death in 1934–35 San Antonio are almost certainly preserved in the Express-News microfilm archive. A single one-hour visit (physical or remote request) to the Briscoe Center would likely produce the article.

Lineage Build-Out

06 Mattingly Direct Paternal Lineage: Reconstruction 12 KB From Thomas Mattingly (pre-1665 Maryland) to [living relative]

Mattingly Direct Paternal Lineage: Reconstruction

From Thomas Mattingly (pre-1665 Maryland) to [living relative]

Research conducted April 2026

---

Summary

This document traces the direct paternal Mattingly line across 15 generations. Approximately 9 of those generations are confirmed or probable from primary sources. The critical unresolved gap is the Kentucky-to-Texas migration (roughly 1800–1870), covering an estimated 3–4 generations for which no documentary sources have yet been found.

Key finding: The earliest confirmed American Mattingly is Thomas Mattingly II (abt.1650–bef.1715), who arrived in St. Mary's County, Maryland prior to 1665 with his wife Elizabeth and children. The family was Catholic and appears on the Maryland Rent Rolls continuously from 1663. The surname traces to Mattingley village, Hampshire, England — "Matta's ley" in Old English.

---

The Confirmed Lineage (Well-Documented Generations)

England: Pre-1650

The surname is Anglo-Saxon, from Mattingley, Hampshire (Hart District, England — 51°19'N, 0°57'W). The earliest ancestors with documented names are:

  • Unnamed ancestor (~1560) — appears in WikiTree 2nd-great-grandparent rows
  • Francis Mattingly — next generation, no dates known
  • Charles Mattingly (~1600, Omny Parish, Sussex, England) — appears as "abt 1600 - abt 1642" in WikiTree profiles for multiple Maryland Mattinglys; named in the 1969 genealogy book as a possible "1633 ship carpenter immigrant" though the exact relationship is uncertain
  • Thomas Mattingly I (~1624 – ~24 Jul 1664, Omny, Sussex, England) — died in England before his son emigrated; his wife was Elizabeth Unknown (born ~1626)

The 1969 book The Descendants of Henry Mattingly notes: "The earliest definite traces locate the family near Dublin, Ireland, whence some of them migrated to America." This Irish connection — possibly via the Catholic diaspora — may explain the "definite Irish traditions" the book references alongside the English surname origin.

The Maryland Immigrant: Thomas Mattingly II (~1650–1715)

WikiTree profiles confirming this person: Mattingly-60, Mattingly-66, Mattingly-210, Mattingly-250, Mattingly-53

Thomas Mattingly II (abt.1650 – bef.12 Jan 1715) emigrated to Maryland prior to 1665. The Descendants of Henry Mattingly (1969) states: "Thomas Mattingly, his wife, Elizabeth, and children, Thomas, Cezar and Elizabeth, came to Maryland prior to 1665." He and a William Mattingly appear on the St. Mary's County Rent Rolls starting in 1663 — records preserved at the Hall of Records, Annapolis, Maryland.

Thomas II settled on "St. Thomas Place" in St. Mary's County and his name appears on those Rent Rolls from 1663 to 1720. He married Elizabeth (Ely) Mattingly (~1654–1714 in St. Mary's County). His children included Ignatius (~1704), James (~1696), and others.

Note on oral tradition vs. records: Shari's interview claims Thomas Mattingly "arrived with Lord Baltimore in 1660 and died 1662." The Rent Rolls show a Thomas Mattingly alive 1663–1720. The most likely explanation: Shari's oral tradition slightly compressed/conflated the immigrant story — the actual arrival was pre-1665, and Thomas II lived until ~1715. Lord Baltimore's Catholic colony (chartered 1632, settled 1634) is the correct historical context, and the family was definitely part of that Catholic Maryland community.

No "Mattingly's Hope" land patent has been confirmed in the sources consulted — this name may refer to a different Mattingly family line or be a family oral tradition that cannot be verified with available sources.

Generation 3: Ignatius Mattingly (abt.1704–1789)

WikiTree: Mattingly-60

Son of Thomas II. Born ~1704 in St. Mary's County, died April 1789 in St. Mary's County. Married Sarah Catherine Fowler (~1705–1795). His will (probated 1789, St. Mary's County Will Book JJ1) named sons Leonard, Luke, William, and daughters Elizabeth, Sarah, Susanna, Hannah. Sons Leonard and Luke were the progenitors of the Catholic Kentucky Mattingly families.

Generation 4: Leonard Mattingly Sr. (1739–1829)

Son of Ignatius. Born 1739 in Leonardtown, St. Mary's County, Maryland; died abt. 28 September 1829 in Leonardtown. Married Mary (Hayden) Mattingly (1739–1812). His children included Leonard Jr. (1764–1843), John Baptist (~1762–1821), and Ignatius (~1766–1851) — all of whom migrated to Kentucky.

Generation 5: Leonard Mattingly Jr. (1764–1843)

WikiTree: Mattingly-210 | FamilySearch marriage record: V5ZD-6SJ

Son of Leonard Sr. Born 1764 in Leonardtown, St. Mary's County, Maryland. Migrated to Kentucky by 1788 — married Ann (Cissell) Mattingly in Nelson County, Kentucky on 7 January 1788. Settled in Marion County, Kentucky (the community of Saint Mary). Died 1843, buried in Saint Mary, Marion County, Kentucky.

Leonard Jr. is the genetic common ancestor confirmed by the Mattingly-Matney Y-chromosome DNA Surname Project. Multiple DNA participants trace directly to him.

The 1969 book notes: "The Kentucky Mattinglys trace their ancestry to Charles, whose sons, William, Leonard and Luke, settled in Hardin Creek, Kentucky, in 1786." Marion County, Kentucky — near Bardstown — became the heart of Catholic Kentucky, sometimes called the "Holy Land of Kentucky."

Leonard Jr.'s children by Ann Cissell included: Mary (Mattingly) Alvey, Henry Martin Mattingly Sr., William Cissell Mattingly, Leonard Mattingly III.

---

The Critical Gap: Kentucky to Texas (Generations 6–9, ~1800–1890)

This is the most poorly documented portion of the lineage. No documentary sources have yet been found for the individual(s) who migrated from Marion County, Kentucky to San Antonio, Texas. Historical context suggests this migration would have occurred between approximately 1820 (Texas land grant era) and 1860 (pre-Civil War).

Oral tradition anchor: Shari described a great-great-great-grandfather who "lived to age 107, born 1828, died 1935." This is extraordinary enough that newspaper coverage should exist. If confirmed, this person (born 1828) would be a grandchild of Leonard Jr. (1764–1843) — fitting neatly as a second-generation Kentucky Mattingly who later moved to Texas. His 1935 death in Texas would generate an obituary that could name parents and children.

Recommended next research steps for this gap:

1. Texas General Land Office records (1820–1860): search for Mattingly headright land grants

2. San Fernando Cathedral records (San Antonio): Mattingly baptisms/marriages ~1840–1880

3. 1850/1860/1880 Texas Census: Bexar County (San Antonio) for Mattingly households

4. San Antonio Express-News archives, 1935: obituary search for a 107-year-old Mattingly

5. Texas death certificates, 1935: surname Mattingly, age 107, county Bexar or adjacent

---

The Confirmed Texas Generations

Generation 10: The Texas Judge (Unknown Name)

Shari described her great-grandfather as "a judge in Texas" who was the father of Leroy Teichmuller Mattingly (b.1898). He married a German-American woman from the Teichmuller family — confirmed by Leroy's distinctive middle name and the presence of Minette Teichmueller (1871–1970), the WPA muralist, as Shari's "Aunt Monette, my grandfather's mother" (likely Leroy's mother's sister or aunt).

The Teichmuller family were German immigrants settled in La Grange, Fayette County, Texas (Minette was born there in 1871) and later San Antonio. Marriage records for a Mattingly–Teichmuller union in Bexar County or Fayette County, Texas (~1890–1900) have not yet been searched.

Per the Shari interview, he "built half of San Antonio" (likely her description of his construction/builder business, not judicial work) and died in 1945. He may be the same person as p010 in entities.json ("great-grandfather, a very successful builder who built half of San Antonio; died in 1945").

Note: Shari may have two different great-grandparents merged here — the judge (Leroy's father) and the builder (the man who died 1945). They may be the same person or different people (e.g., the judge was Leroy's grandfather and the builder was his father).

Generation 11: Leroy Teichmuller Mattingly (born 1898)

Most reliably documented Texas-generation anchor.

Born 1898, likely in San Antonio. University of Texas engineering graduate (~1920s). His middle name "Teichmuller" directly encodes his mother's maiden name. His siblings:

  • Claude Mattingly (~born ~1900): physician in San Antonio; found dead in a hotel room with a University of Texas professor's wife in 1934 — significant local scandal that should be findable in San Antonio newspaper archives
  • Mamie Mattingly (~born 1900–1902): their mother died in childbirth at her delivery

Leroy married Shari's grandmother (born ~1900–1902, attended North Texas State University, lost family wealth in the 1929 crash).

Generation 12: Shari's Father (born 1922, San Antonio)

Name unknown from available records. Born 1922 in San Antonio. Had a master's degree. Had siblings including "Billy Mattingly" (geologist, Stanford graduate). Son of Leroy Teichmuller Mattingly.

Generation 13: Sharyn ("Shari") Mattingly (born ~1947)

Currently ~79 years old, Santa Monica, California. Royal owner of eight oil wells in Reeves County, Texas. Married David (Hunter's paternal grandfather). Children include [living relative] and Hunter's parent.

Generations 14–15: Hunter's Parent and [living relative] (living)

Living persons — details redacted from this research record.

---

Where Shari's Oral History Diverges from Documentary Record

| Oral Tradition | Documentary Evidence | Assessment |

|---|---|---|

| "Thomas Mattingly arrived with Lord Baltimore in 1660 and died 1662" | Thomas Mattingly appears on Rent Rolls 1663–1720, arrived "prior to 1665" | Dates compressed; "1660" likely refers to the broader Lord Baltimore era. The 1662 death may refer to Thomas I (his father, who died in England) |

| "Mattingly's Hope land grant" | Not found in any source consulted | May be oral tradition embellishment, or a different Mattingly line |

| "Mattingly & Moore whiskey is the Kentucky branch" | The Kentucky Mattinglys (Marion/Nelson County) are confirmed but the specific whiskey connection hasn't been traced | Plausible; needs further research |

| "Great-great-great-grandfather lived to 107 (1828–1935)" | Not confirmed — no documentary source found | Extraordinary claim; newspaper obituaries for 1935 Texas should exist if true |

---

Sources Consulted

Primary:

  • WikiTree Mattingly-60: https://www.wikitree.com/wiki/Mattingly-60
  • WikiTree Mattingly-210: https://www.wikitree.com/wiki/Mattingly-210
  • WikiTree Mattingly-250: https://www.wikitree.com/wiki/Mattingly-250
  • WikiTree Mattingly-53: https://www.wikitree.com/wiki/Mattingly-53
  • WikiTree Mattingly-58: https://www.wikitree.com/wiki/Mattingly-58
  • WikiTree Mattingly-17: https://www.wikitree.com/wiki/Mattingly-17
  • The Descendants of Henry Mattingly (1969, 300+ pages): https://archive.org/details/thedescendantsofhenrymattingly
  • Maryland Rent Rolls 1663 (cited in 1969 book) — Hall of Records, Annapolis MD
  • FamilySearch record V5ZD-6SJ (Leonard Mattingly Jr. marriage, Kentucky 1788)

Secondary:

  • Wikipedia: Mattingly (surname): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mattingly
  • Wikipedia: Mattingley, Hampshire: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mattingley
  • Wikipedia: Minette Teichmueller: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minette_Teichmueller
  • Family interview transcript: Shari (Sharyn) Mattingly, April 2026

---

Priority Research Actions

1. Texas 1935 death/obituary search — 107-year-old Mattingly centenarian. If found, this unlocks the Kentucky-to-Texas bridge.

2. 1834 San Antonio newspaper search — Claude Mattingly scandal. May name parents (the judge) and confirm family details.

3. Bexar County Marriage Records ~1890–1900 — Mattingly × Teichmuller marriage to identify the judge's wife.

4. Texas District Court Records 1870–1910 — find the judge named Mattingly in Bexar County.

5. 1850/1860/1880 Texas Census — Bexar County, Mattingly households.

6. Thomas Mattingly II standalone WikiTree profile — search WikiTree for a standalone profile with ID not yet found (he appears as parent across many profiles but his own page was not located in the ID range scanned).

7. Mattingly's Hope land patent — Maryland Land Office, Annapolis: search patent books for "Mattingly" in Charles County or St. Mary's County ~1660–1680.

07 Collateral Branches Research — Mattingly / Teichmueller / Baity / Frost 13 KB Research date: 2026-04-25

Collateral Branches Research — Mattingly / Teichmueller / Baity / Frost

Research date: 2026-04-25

Researcher: Claude Sonnet 4.6

Primary source: Fayette County, Her History and Her People (F. Lotto, 1902, LOC); WikiTree; FamilySearch; Wikipedia

---

Summary

This research maximally spans four collateral branches of the family tree:

  • Teichmueller: Hans's German parents and all five children named
  • Mattingly: English origins confirmed (Hampshire); four additional colonial generations and seven sibling lines identified
  • Baity: "Pearl Baity" identified in documentary record as Paralee Johnson Baity; full 6-generation NC ancestry traced; critical Baity-Mattingly marriage link found
  • Frost: Frost Bank succession confirmed through Joseph Hardin Frost (president 1926); Shari's uncle candidate identified

---

Branch 1: Teichmueller (Leroy's Maternal Grandfather)

Hans's German parents — CONFIRMED (primary source: 1902 Fayette County history, LOC)

The 1902 county history states explicitly:

> "His parents were August and Charlotte Teichmueller, nee von Gursewald. His father was an officer in the Brunswick army."

August Teichmueller (Hans's father): Officer in the Duchy of Brunswick army. His death left the family in financial difficulty, prompting Hans to emigrate to the United States in 1856. No birth or death year found in accessible sources.

Charlotte Teichmueller (born von Gursewald) (Hans's mother): Her maiden name "von Gursewald" carries the aristocratic "von" prefix, indicating a minor noble or gentry family of the Duchy of Brunswick. No separate Wikipedia or genealogical record for this family was found in accessible online sources; the 1902 Fayette County history is the sole surviving citation.

Hans attended the public schools of Brunswick city and the Gymnasium at Blankenburg (Harz) — a town in what is now Saxony-Anhalt, Germany, about 40 km southeast of Brunswick. The current Gymnasium Am Thie in Blankenburg is the successor to the school Hans attended. He graduated, then worked briefly as a ship's sailor to New York as a teenager, returned home, and emigrated permanently to Texas in 1856 after his father's death.

Hans's five children — CONFIRMED

The 1902 history names the surviving children at the time of writing (Hans died February 17, 1901):

  • May Mattingly (died approximately 1900) — wife of Ed. Mattingly, assistant cashier of the First National Bank of La Grange
  • Minetta Teichmueller — unmarried, living with her mother in La Grange in 1902
  • Anna Teichmueller — unmarried, living with her mother in La Grange in 1902
  • Two additional children (unnamed in the biography; names lost)

Augusta Kellner (Hans's wife) — CONFIRMED

Augusta Kellner Teichmueller was born at Postoak Point, Austin County, Texas, daughter of "a highly educated teacher of modern languages." She married Hans on June 16, 1858 at Postoak Point; Colonel Henderson, justice of the peace, performed the ceremony. She was a widow and head of household in La Grange in 1902.

---

Branch 2: Mattingly Paternal — English Origins and Colonial Line

The English village — CONFIRMED (Wikipedia)

The Mattingly surname originates in Mattingley, a village and civil parish in Hampshire, England, located on the Reading road between Hook and Reading. The River Whitewater runs through the parish. Wikipedia states directly: "The Mattingly surname originates in Mattingley." The name comes from "Matta's ley" — "ley" meaning place in Old English, referring to the Matta family. This is 40 miles southwest of London.

Thomas Mattingly I — CONFIRMED (WikiTree Mattingly-30; jhink.org; Herman Mattingly 1975)

Thomas Mattingly I (abt. 1624 - abt. 24 July 1664) was the family's American immigrant. Born in Sussex/Berkshire England (WikiTree shows "Omny Parish, Sussex" — likely a transcription of a specific hamlet), he emigrated with wife and four children in 1663-64. He died intestate shortly after arrival in St. Mary's County, Maryland. Widow Elizabeth McWilliams was granted administration; she remarried attorney Walter Pake, who secured the family's land claim. The 300-acre "Mattingly's Hope" patent in Charles County, Maryland was issued 4 September 1666 to sons Thomas II and Cezar.

Thomas I's English ancestry (WikiTree):

  • Father: Charles Mattingly (abt. 1600-1642, Omny Parish, Sussex)
  • Mother: Judith Bugbee (abt. 1602, England)
  • Paternal grandfather: John Mattingly (abt. 1560-1641, Berkshire, England)
  • Paternal great-grandfather: Francis Mattingly (1580-1620, England)

Thomas I's wife: Elizabeth McWilliams (abt. 1626 - after 1669, England).

Thomas I's four children:

  • Thomas Mattingly II (abt. 1650-1715) — the heir line
  • Cezar (Caesar) Mattingly (1654-1719) — co-heir of Mattingly's Hope
  • Judith Mattingly (1645-1744) — married, became Judith Turner; lived to ~99
  • Elizabeth Dorcas Mattingly (1656-1749) — lived to ~93

Thomas Mattingly II — CONFIRMED (WikiTree Mattingly-29; FamilySearch)

Thomas II (abt. 1650-1715) married twice. First wife: Mary Elizabeth Suttle/Curtis (about 1675, St. Mary's County); children Elizabeth (1681) and Judith (1683). Second wife: Elizabeth Cole (about 1690); children Thomas III (1688/1690), James (1696), Charles (1698), William (1700), Luke (1702), Leonard Ignatius (1704-1789), Ann (1706). Will filed 9 October 1714, probated 12 January 1715 — bequeathed "Mount Misery" to son Thomas III, land to James, etc.

Thomas Mattingly III — CONFIRMED (WikiTree Mattingly-28; will transcribed)

Thomas III (abt. 1688-1774) wrote his will 11 April 1774, probated 10 September 1774 in St. Mary's County. Explicitly names sons Leonard, Edward, John Baptist, and Clement as heirs. Also mentions "Mount Misery" plantation and "Mattingly's Lane" with a water mill. This will is the documentary anchor for the generation between Thomas II and the Kentucky migration era.

Leonard Ignatius Mattingly (1704-1789) — PROBABLE

Leonard Ignatius, son of Thomas II, is referenced in FamilySearch's catalog of the Mattingly family genealogy book as "great-grandson of Thomas I who married twice and was the father of twelve children." He is likely one of the Mattingly progenitors who either migrated to Kentucky in 1785 with the Maryland Catholic cohort, or whose descendants did. The "Ignatius" given name reflects the strong Jesuit influence in Maryland's St. Mary's County Catholic community (St. Inigoes parish, named for St. Ignatius).

---

Branch 3: Baity — "Pearl Baity" and the North Carolina Line

Key finding: Pearl Baity's real name was Paralee

Documentary research resolves a central mystery: the "Pearl Baity" of family oral history is recorded in census and vital records as Paralee (Johnson) Baity. "Pearl" was almost certainly a family nickname for Paralee. Her husband was William Alexander Baity (WikiTree Baity-117).

William Alexander Baity (1862-1945) — CONFIRMED (WikiTree Baity-117; 6 census records; Texas death certificate)

  • Born 2 November 1862, Courtney, Yadkin County, North Carolina
  • Grew up Liberty Township, Yadkin County (1870 and 1880 census confirmed)
  • Married Paralee Johnson, 4 June 1899, Grayson County (Denison), Texas — matching the family oral history of coming to north Texas
  • By 1900: in Denison, Grayson County (north Texas — Sherman/Denison area near Oklahoma border, near "Sherman's camp" oral history detail)
  • By 1910: established in San Antonio — remained there through 1940 census
  • Died 30 January 1945, San Antonio; Texas Death Certificate 477
  • Children: Ruth (Baity) Mattingly and Margaret Baity

The critical Baity-Mattingly link

Ruth (Baity) Mattingly is listed on WikiTree as William Baity's daughter. She married a Mattingly — the documentary bridge between the two family lines. Which specific Mattingly she married is not yet confirmed in accessible records, but the implication is that she married Leroy Teichmuller Mattingly or another Texas Mattingly of that generation. This is the open question most worth resolving next.

Baity NC ancestry — CONFIRMED (WikiTree, 6 generations)

| Generation | Person | Dates | Location |

|-----------|--------|-------|----------|

| William Alexander Baity | The Texas builder | 1862-1945 | Yadkin NC → San Antonio TX |

| William D. Baity (father) | Farmer | 1829-1894 | North Carolina |

| Isham Baity (grandfather) | Farmer | 1804-1892 | Yadkin County NC |

| David Baity Sr. (g-grandfather) | Farmer | 1782-1856 | Surry County NC |

| George Baity Sr. (gg-grandfather) | Farmer | 1746-1828 | North Carolina |

| Rachel Allgood (gg-grandmother) | — | ~1756-? | North Carolina |

George Baity Sr. (born 1746) was alive during the American Revolution. His family line in Surry/Yadkin County NC aligns with the Appalachian Piedmont region — exactly the territory through which Sherman's Carolinas Campaign passed in 1865. The family's oral memory of a relative going by buckboard to beg Sherman not to burn their farm is consistent with this geography.

Paralee Johnson's background

Her father Morgan Johnson was in Denison, Grayson County, Texas by 1900 (the 1900 census shows Paralee in his household before her marriage). Whether the Johnson family also came from NC is not confirmed. The migration to north Texas from the Carolinas in the 1870s is a well-documented pattern.

Florence Butt / HEB corroboration — PROBABLE

Florence Thornton Butt (not "Herbert E. Butt" as Shari said — that was her grandson Howard E. Butt Jr.) founded the C.C. Butt Grocery Store in Kerrville, Texas on 26 November 1905. She was the mother of Howard Edward Butt Sr. (April 9, 1895 – March 12, 1991), who built H-E-B into a chain. The family was originally from Memphis, Tennessee — having moved to Kerrville due to Charles Butt's tuberculosis. Florence's Thornton family origins are not established in accessible sources; whether she grew up in the same NC Piedmont region as the Baity family would need further research.

---

Branch 4: Frost Bank — Shari's Maternal Line

The Frost Bank family succession — CONFIRMED (Wikipedia)

Frost Bank (founded 1868, San Antonio) has been Frost-family-controlled since founding:

1. Thomas Claiborne Frost (founder, 1868) — former Confederate Army lieutenant colonel

2. T.C. Frost Jr. (son) — ran the bank; constructed 12-story building 1921

3. Joseph Hardin Frost (brother of T.C. Jr.) — took over as president in 1926

If Shari's mother was born 1923 and her maternal uncle was "Frost Bank president," the most likely scenario is:

  • Shari's grandmother was a sibling of T.C. Frost Jr. and Joseph Hardin Frost
  • That would make Shari's grandmother a child of Thomas Claiborne Frost (founder)
  • And Joseph Hardin Frost (president 1926) would be Shari's great-uncle, not uncle

The generational math: Thomas Claiborne Frost (founded bank 1868, so born ~1835-1845) → children born ~1860-1880 → grandchildren born ~1890-1910 → Shari's mother born 1923 would be Thomas Claiborne Frost's great-grandchild.

The specific identity of Shari's Frost grandmother requires Shari to be asked directly. Her mother's maiden name is the single most important piece of information needed to confirm this branch.

---

Notes on the Centenarian (b. ~1828, d. ~1935)

The family oral history describes a man born around 1828 who lived to age 107 and died around 1935. The Baity line has William D. Baity (born 1829, died 1894 at age 65) — he is NOT the centenarian.

The centenarian is on the Mattingly paternal line. If Leroy Mattingly (b. 1898) is Shari's grandfather, his father Ed Mattingly was probably born ~1860-1870, Ed's father ~1835-1845, and Ed's grandfather ~1805-1820 — not quite reaching 1828. But if the family counts one more generation, an ancestor born 1828 who died 1935 at 107 is theoretically possible in the direct line.

This remains UNVERIFIED. A 107-year-old death in 1935 would have been national news in Texas newspapers. The Briscoe Center at UT Austin (which holds San Antonio Express-News microfilm) is the recommended next step.

---

What These Findings Add to the Tree Visualization

New CONFIRMED nodes for the D3 tree:

  • August Teichmueller (generation above Hans)
  • Charlotte von Gursewald Teichmueller (generation above Hans)
  • Augusta Kellner Teichmueller (Hans's wife, confirmed name and origin)
  • Minetta Teichmueller (Hans's daughter, confirmed)
  • Anna Teichmueller (Hans's daughter, confirmed)
  • Thomas Mattingly I through Thomas III — 3 additional direct ancestors
  • Charles Mattingly + Judith Bugbee — 2 more grandparent-level
  • John Mattingly (1560) — 1 more great-grandparent level
  • Cezar Mattingly, Judith Turner, Elizabeth Dorcas — 3 collateral siblings of Thomas II
  • James, Charles, William, Luke, Leonard Ignatius, Ann — 6 collateral siblings of Thomas III
  • William Alexander Baity (1862-1945) — the San Antonio builder confirmed
  • Paralee Johnson Baity — "Pearl" confirmed in records
  • Ruth (Baity) Mattingly — direct Baity-Mattingly link confirmed
  • William D. Baity, Isham Baity, David Baity Sr., George Baity Sr. — 4 more NC generations
  • Thomas Claiborne Frost, Joseph Hardin Frost — 2 Frost candidates

Total new named individuals: 27 confirmed or probable, 5 possible.

08 Pre-1623 Mattingly Ancestry 12 KB Prepared: 2026-04-25

Pre-1623 Mattingly Ancestry

Research Report — Tracing Thomas Mattingly I's English Roots

Prepared: 2026-04-25

Sources: British History Online (VCH Hampshire Vol. 4), WikiTree Mattingly DNA Project, Geni.com, FamilySearch, YFull Y-DNA tree, Wikipedia (Basingas), London church burial records

---

Summary

The Mattingly line before Thomas I (b. ~1623) can be traced through three distinct tiers of evidence:

1. WikiTree genealogical data (POSSIBLE–PROBABLE): Reaches John Mattingly, buried at St. Mary Colechurch, London, December 17, 1641 — grandfather of Thomas I. One primary source document (the burial record) anchors this person.

2. Medieval primary sources (CONFIRMED): Stephen de Mattingley (fl. 1206) and his son Peter de Mattingley are documented in the Curia Regis Rolls and the Victoria County History of Hampshire, published by British History Online. They held land at Mattingley and granted property to the Prior of Merton.

3. Deep-time context (historical/cultural, not personal genealogy): The Domesday Book (1086) names Alric as the pre-Conquest free-holder of Mattingley. The village itself was founded by the Basingas tribe in the 5th–6th century. The Mattingly Y-chromosome (R1b-Y14084 > R1b-DF27) traces to the Bell Beaker expansion approximately 2500 BCE.

The "1525 ancestor" Shari references from LDS research is structurally consistent with the generation of John Mattingly (b. ~1560)'s father — born approximately 1490–1530. No named individual at this exact depth has been independently confirmed in open sources. The Hampshire Record Office holds the parish registers (starting 1538) that could confirm this generation.

---

The Documented Generations

Generation 0: Thomas Mattingly I (b. ~1623 — The Immigrant)

Born approximately 1623 in Omny Parish, Sussex or Berkshire, England. Died July 24, 1664, St. Mary's County, Maryland. Wife: Elizabeth (maiden name unknown). Catholic.

Arrived Maryland late 1663 or early 1664. His sons Thomas II and Cezar received "Mattingly's Hope" — a 300-acre land patent in Charles County, Maryland, dated September 4, 1666.

Y-DNA haplogroup: R1b-DF27 > R1b-Y14084 (terminal SNP unique to all documented Mattingly descendants; established by FTDNA Big-Y testing via the Mattingly-Matney Surname DNA Project).

Confidence: CONFIRMED. Multiple consistent primary and genealogical sources.

---

Generation -1: Charles Mattingly (b. ~1600 — Father)

Born approximately 1600, Omny Parish, Sussex, England. Died approximately 1642 (location uncertain — listed as Maryland on WikiTree but this conflicts with Thomas I emigrating in 1663 and needs verification). Married Judith Bugbee in England.

Confidence: POSSIBLE. Single source is WikiTree Mattingly-368, which itself cites only an Ancestry.com family tree. The 1642 Maryland death date is inconsistent with what we know about Thomas I's timeline. This generation needs primary source verification against the Heckfield/Mattingley parish registers.

---

Generation -2: John Mattingly (b. ~1560 — Grandfather)

Born approximately 1560, Berkshire, England. Buried December 17, 1641, St. Mary Colechurch, London. Married Mary Winch approximately 1599 in England.

One primary source anchors this person: the London Church of England burial register, St. Mary Colechurch, 1641, page 38: "John Mattingly was buried the 17 of December 1641 in the church."

His parents are listed in MyHeritage-derived data as John Mattingly and Elizabeth Elinor Folliot, but this deeper connection is not primary-source verified.

Confidence: PROBABLE. The burial record is a genuine primary source. The connection to Charles (father of Thomas I) relies on aggregated genealogy data.

---

Generation -3: The "1525 Ancestor" (b. ~1490–1530)

This is the generation Shari's LDS account identifies. A great-great-grandfather of Thomas I (b. ~1623) would be born approximately 1490–1530 — squarely matching the "1525" figure from the LDS Genealogical Center, Houston.

MyHeritage data names this person as another "John Mattingly," son of John Mattingly and Elizabeth Elinor Folliot. No primary source verification has been achieved in this research session.

What would confirm this generation: the Heckfield parish registers starting 1538, held at the Hampshire Record Office, Winchester. A John Mattingly fathering children in the 1550s–1570s would appear there if the family was in Mattingley parish. The 1554–1559 gap in the registers (the incumbent "omytted the kepyng of thys book") is a real documentation hazard for this exact period.

Confidence: UNVERIFIED. Structurally plausible, consistent with oral tradition, but no primary source confirmation found in this research.

---

Generation -4 and Beyond: Pre-Register Era (before 1538)

No named individual at this depth. Parish registers began in 1538. Ancestors born before approximately 1520 would appear only in manorial court rolls, diocesan wills (Winchester diocese), or Tudor subsidy rolls. The Tudor lay subsidy of 1524–25 (TNA, E 179 series) covers Hampshire and may list a Mattingly householder in Holdshott hundred — an unworked lead.

---

Medieval Mattingley Family: The Primary-Source Line

These are not proven direct ancestors but are the earliest documented individuals with the Mattingley surname, living at the village:

Ellis — lord of Mattingley in 1167. Succeeded by Revelendus.

Revelendus of Mattingley (fl. late 12th century) — lord of Mattingley after Ellis. Married Rose; their three sons were James de Oakley, Bartholomew de Oakley, and Stephen de Mattingley. Documented in VCH Hampshire Vol. 4.

Stephen de Mattingley (fl. 1206) — son of Revelendus. In 1206 granted to the Prior of Merton in free alms: 6 virgates, 30 acres of land, several meadows, 6s. 3d. rent, and a mill in Mattingley. Also recorded in the Curia Regis Rolls as "Stephen de Madingel', de Matingel'" — the earliest dated individual with the Mattingley surname per the Dictionary of English Surnames (Reaney & Wilson, Routledge, 1991). Source: British History Online: https://www.british-history.ac.uk/vch/hants/vol4/pp44-51

Peter de Mattingley (fl. 1220s–1240s) — son of Stephen. Sold all his property to Geoffrey de Arundel. After this, the surname disappears from Mattingley's manorial records as major landholders — the family likely continued as yeoman farmers or tenants. Confidence: CONFIRMED for all three via BHO VCH.

The gap between Peter de Mattingley (1240s) and John Mattingly (d. 1641) is approximately 400 years with no named individual yet bridging it in primary sources. The surname continuity through the village is plausible; the personal genealogical chain is unproven.

---

The 1086 Domesday Entry

Mattingley appears in the Domesday Book as Matingeleghe. The entry records:

  • Pre-Conquest holder: Alric, who held it of King Edward (the Confessor) as an alod (free of feudal tenure)
  • 1086 holder: Alsi the son of Brixi (Alfsi son of Brictsi), holding under Hugh de Port
  • The village was a chapelry of Heckfield, in the hundred of Holdshott

Alric was an Anglo-Saxon thane or free farmer, almost certainly part of the Basingas community whose ancestors had named the place. His fate after the Norman Conquest is unknown. He is a village-level ancestor — not a proven direct lineal ancestor of later Mattingley families — but represents the community from which the surname derives.

Source: British History Online (VCH Hampshire, 1911): https://www.british-history.ac.uk/vch/hants/vol4/pp44-51

---

The Basingas and Matta: Anglo-Saxon Deep Roots

The village of Mattingley derives its name from Old English Matta + -inga- + leah: "the woodland clearing of the followers/people of Matta." The personal name Matta (or Mætta) is inferred from the place name.

The naming clan was part of the Basingas, an Anglo-Saxon tribe whose territory in the Loddon Valley formed a regio of the early Kingdom of Wessex. Their leader Basa gave the tribe its name, which survives in Old Basing and Basingstoke (both approximately 22 km west-southwest of Mattingley). The Basingas settled eastern Basingstoke in the early 6th century. Their territory (Basengum) is recorded in the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle as the site of the Battle of Basing in 871 AD.

Matta is a probable real person — place-name conventions require an eponymous leader — but no document independently names him. His existence is inferred. The founding dates to the 5th–6th century.

Source: Wikipedia, Basingas: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Basingas (citing Yorke, Wessex in the Early Middle Ages, 1995)

---

Y-DNA Deep History: R1b-DF27 > R1b-Y14084

The Mattingly male line carries haplogroup R1b-DF27, the Gallo-Iberian branch of R1b-P312. Terminal SNP Y14084 is unique to documented descendants of Thomas Mattingly I.

Key dates (from YFull: https://www.yfull.com/tree/R-DF27/):

  • R1b-DF27 formed approximately 4500 ybp (circa 2500 BCE), confidence interval 5300–3700 ybp

The genealogical chain from individual to haplogroup context:

| Horizon | Dates | Event |

|---|---|---|

| Thomas Mattingly I | ~1623 CE | Immigrant; Y14084 terminal SNP |

| Y14084 formation | unknown — est. last 2000 years | Mattingly-specific mutation |

| R1b-DF27 formation | ~2500 BCE | Gallo-Iberian clade branch point |

| Bell Beaker in Britain | 2450–1800 BCE | R1b-M269 carriers arrived; replaced ~90% of Neolithic male lineages |

| R1b-M269 origin | ~4500 BCE | Expanded from Pontic-Caspian Steppe via Yamnaya culture |

| R1b earliest | ~20,000 ybp | Ancient Eurasian lineage |

The Mattingly men's Y-chromosome has been present in the British Isles since the Bell Beaker population replacement approximately 4,400 years ago.

---

What the Parish Registers Could Confirm

The Heckfield parish registers — including the Mattingley chapelry — begin in 1538. Per British History Online (VCH Hampshire, p. 38): "The registers — including that of Mattingley — begin in 1538; the first book is of paper, containing mixed entries of baptisms, marriages and burials thence to 1575."

Books two through nine cover 1575–1812 with minor gaps. There is a gap from approximately 1554–1559 (incumbent absent during the Marian restoration).

To confirm the 1525 ancestor: search the Hampshire Record Office, Winchester for baptisms of Mattingly children in the 1550s–1570s, which would reflect a parent born in the 1520s–1530s.

Hampshire Record Office: https://www.hants.gov.uk/librariesandarchives/archives

---

Confidence Summary

| Generation | Person | Depth Before Thomas I | Confidence |

|---|---|---|---|

| G-0 | Thomas Mattingly I (~1623) | — | CONFIRMED |

| G-1 | Charles Mattingly (~1600) | 1 gen | POSSIBLE |

| G-2 | John Mattingly (~1560, d. 1641 London) | 2 gen | PROBABLE |

| G-3 | Unknown (the "1525 ancestor") | 3 gen | UNVERIFIED |

| G-4+ | Pre-register era | 4+ gen | TRADITION |

| Medieval | Stephen de Mattingley (fl. 1206) | ~14 gen | CONFIRMED (village surname line) |

| Domesday | Alric (1086) | ~18 gen | CONFIRMED (village holder, not direct lineage) |

| Deep time | Matta (5th–6th century founder) | ~45 gen | PROBABLE (inferred from place name) |

---

Key Next Steps to Push This Further

1. Hampshire Record Office, Winchester — Request search of Heckfield parish baptism registers 1538–1580 for any Mattingly entry. This would confirm or deny the 1525 ancestor claim.

2. TNA E 179 (subsidy rolls) — Search Hampshire, Holdshott hundred, 1524–25 lay subsidy for a Mattingly household head.

3. TNA E 376/E 377 (recusant rolls) — Search for Mattingly entries in Hampshire, late 16th century.

4. Herman R. Mattingly, The Mattingly Family in Early America (1975) — Obtain via interlibrary loan; the FamilySearch catalog entry (https://www.familysearch.org/en/search/catalog/570053) indicates it covers pre-immigration English roots. This is the likely source of the LDS "1525" claim.

5. FamilySearch online indexing — Heckfield/Mattingley parish registers may be digitized and indexed at FamilySearch. Search for early Mattingly entries directly.

09 Medieval Mattingly Research 12 KB Prepared: 2026-04-25

Medieval Mattingly Research

Tracing the Direct Paternal Line Before the ~1560 Anchor

Prepared: 2026-04-25

Sources: British History Online (VCH Hampshire vol. 4), Reaney & Wilson Dictionary of English Surnames (Routledge 1991), WikiTree Mattingly-368/Mattingly-60, Internet Archive (1969 Mattingly genealogy book), WikiTree Mattingly/Matney DNA Project

---

Anchor Correction

The task brief names "Charles Mattingly (~1560)" as the deepest currently identified ancestor. Per WikiTree Mattingly-368 and the Mattingly-60 ancestor chain, this is one generation off. The ancestor born approximately 1560 is John Mattingly (~1560, buried December 17, 1641, St. Mary Colechurch, London) — not Charles. Charles Mattingly (~1600) is John's son, and the father of Thomas Mattingly I (the 1663-64 Maryland immigrant). The full chain is:

Thomas I (b.~1623) -> Charles (~1600) -> John (~1560, d.1641) [deepest named]

---

Confidence Tier Summary

| Person | Dates | Confidence | Primary Source Exists? |

|---|---|---|---|

| John Mattingly | ~1560 – bur. 17 Dec 1641 | PROBABLE | Yes — London burial register (St. Mary Colechurch) |

| John's parents | ~1520-1535 (estimated) | UNVERIFIED | None found |

| The "LDS 1525 ancestor" | ~1490-1530 | UNVERIFIED | None in open sources |

| Stephen de Mattingley | fl. 1206 | CONFIRMED | Feet of Fines + Curia Regis Rolls |

| Peter de Mattingley | fl. 1236-1249 | CONFIRMED | Feet of Fines Hampshire |

| Revelendus of Mattingley | fl. late 12th c. | CONFIRMED | Cotton MS Cleopatra C. vii |

| Ellis (lord of Mattingley) | fl. 1167 | CONFIRMED | Pipe Roll 13 Hen. II |

| Alric (Domesday holder) | fl. 1086 | CONFIRMED (village, not ancestry) | Domesday / VCH Hants i, 505a |

---

Deepest Verifiable Ancestor: John Mattingly (~1560, d. 1641)

Full name: John Mattingly

Born: approximately 1560 (birthplace uncertain — Berkshire or Hampshire per family tradition, unverified)

Buried: December 17, 1641, St. Mary Colechurch, London

Spouse: Mary Winch (claimed in MyHeritage-derived data; no primary document found)

Son: Charles Mattingly (~1600)

What the primary source actually says: The London Church of England burial register for St. Mary Colechurch, 1641, page 38, contains the entry: "John Mattingly was buried the 17 of December 1641 in the church." That is the entire primary record. The original is at London Metropolitan Archives. WikiTree profiles Mattingly-368 and Mattingly-60 connect this person to the Mattingly immigrant line via aggregated genealogical data — no second primary document independently confirms John as father of Charles.

Confidence: PROBABLE. The burial record is genuine. The family connection relies on secondary aggregation.

---

What Was Found Before John Mattingly (~1560): Nothing

John Mattingly's parents are completely unknown. WikiTree Mattingly-368 (Charles ~1600) shows entirely blank grandparent slots — no sourced names for John's mother or father exist anywhere in indexed genealogical databases. The MyHeritage data floating around online names "John Mattingly and Elizabeth Elinor Folliot" as John's parents, but no primary source is cited for this pair anywhere. It should not be added to a verified tree.

The "1525 ancestor" from the family's LDS oral tradition is structurally consistent — a great-great-grandfather of Thomas I born ~1490-1530 would align with that date — but no named individual has been independently confirmed.

---

The 300-Year Gap: 1249 to 1641

The most significant finding of this research is the confirmation of a 300-year documentary void. The BHO Victoria County History of Hampshire (vol. 4, pp. 44-51) — a scholarly publication based directly on primary archives — states explicitly:

> "Stephen was succeeded by his son Peter de Mattingley, who sold all his property to Geoffrey de Arundel."

After that sale (confirmed in the Feet of Fines for Hampshire, Michaelmas 21 Henry III, approximately 1236-1249), no individual bearing the Mattingley surname appears in any published primary source for the village of Mattingley until John Mattingly (d. 1641). The surname disappears from the manorial record for approximately 390 years.

The village continued to be inhabited. Other families are documented: the Rycher family (seated at Mattingley by the mid-14th century, per Feet of Fines, Hillary 14 Edward III, ~1340). But no Mattingley-surname person appears.

This is not a failure of research — it is the actual historical record. After the de Mattingley lords sold their property and the manorial connection severed, the surname-bearing line likely continued as tenant farmers or moved to another area, generating no land transaction or court appearance for centuries. Their reappearance is only confirmed when John Mattingly (~1560) surfaces via his 1641 London burial record.

---

The Medieval Confirmed Line (Not Direct Ancestry — Village Surname Origin)

These individuals are CONFIRMED in primary sources as real historical persons with the Mattingley surname. They lived at the village. They are not proven direct ancestors of John Mattingly (~1560). They establish that the surname originated at Mattingley village and was being used by at least 1206.

Ellis (fl. 1167)

Lord of Mattingley. No surname given — identified only as "Ellis, the owner in 1167."

Source: Pipe Roll 13 Henry II (Pipe Roll Society, xi, 189) — cited at https://www.british-history.ac.uk/vch/hants/vol4/pp44-51

Revelendus of Mattingley (fl. late 12th century)

Lord of Mattingley, succeeded Ellis. Wife: Rose. Three sons: James de Oakley, Bartholomew de Oakley, and Stephen de Mattingley.

Source: Cotton MS Cleopatra C. vii, fol. 134d (British Library) — charter of Merton Priory. Cited at https://www.british-history.ac.uk/vch/hants/vol4/pp44-51

Stephen de Mattingley (fl. 1206) — EARLIEST DATED SURNAME BEARER

Son of Revelendus. In 1206 granted to Walter Prior of Merton and his successors in free alms: 6 virgates, 30 acres of land, several meadows, 6s. 3d. rent, and a mill in Mattingley. Also received Bartholomew de Oakley's share of the inheritance.

Recorded in the Curia Regis Rolls as "Stephen de Madingel', de Matingel'" — the earliest dated individual with the Mattingley surname per the Dictionary of English Surnames (Reaney & Wilson, Routledge 1991).

Primary sources:

  • Feet of Fines, Hampshire, Trinity 8 John (1206) — TNA CP 25/1
  • Curia Regis Rolls (cited in Reaney & Wilson 1991 via https://www.houseofnames.com/matingley-family-crest)
  • BHO VCH Hampshire vol. 4: https://www.british-history.ac.uk/vch/hants/vol4/pp44-51

Confidence: CONFIRMED

Peter de Mattingley (fl. 1220s-1249)

Son of Stephen. Sold all his property to Geoffrey de Arundel. Listed as "Peter Mattingeley" in Hampshire, 1249. After this sale the surname disappears from the Mattingley manorial record.

Primary sources:

  • Cotton MS Cleopatra C. vii, fol. 134d
  • Feet of Fines, Hampshire, Michaelmas 21 Henry III (1236)
  • Reaney & Wilson Dictionary of English Surnames, 1991 (via https://www.houseofnames.com/matingley-family-crest — "Peter Mattingeley 1249 in Hampshire")

Confidence: CONFIRMED

---

Domesday Entry: Mattingley 1086

The village appears in the Domesday Book as Matingeleghe:

  • Pre-Conquest holder: Alric — held Mattingley of King Edward the Confessor as an alod (free, not feudal)
  • 1086 holder: Alsi the son of Brixi (Alfsi son of Brictsi), holding under Hugh de Port
  • 8 villagers, 3 smallholders, 3 ploughlands, 3 men's plough teams, 4 acres of meadow, 1 mill worth 5 shillings

Alric was an Anglo-Saxon thane or free farmer. He is a village-level figure — not a proven genealogical ancestor of any later Mattingly family. His presence establishes the pre-Conquest Anglo-Saxon community whose descendants named the clearing.

Source: V.C.H. Hants, i, 505a (referenced as footnote 78 in https://www.british-history.ac.uk/vch/hants/vol4/pp44-51)

---

Rejected Claims

"John Mattingly and Elizabeth Elinor Folliot" as parents of John ~1560

Found in: MyHeritage aggregated trees, reflected in some WikiTree biography notes.

Rejected because: No primary source cited. WikiTree Mattingly-368 shows blank grandparent slots with no sourced names. This name pair is unverified speculation.

Continuous gentry descent from Stephen/Peter de Mattingley to 16th-century Mattingly

Found in: Various uncited public trees on Geni.com, MyHeritage, RootsWeb.

Rejected because: BHO VCH Hampshire explicitly states the surname disappears from manorial records after Peter (~1249). A 300-year gap exists with no documented individual. Any tree asserting unbroken gentry descent across this gap is fabricating it.

---

What the 1969 Internet Archive Book Contains

The book "The Descendants of Henry Mattingly" (1969, Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/thedescendantsofhenrymattingly) covers Henry Mattingly (c.1750-1823) and his Ohio/Indiana/Illinois/Maryland descendants only. Its Appendix I states: "In England there is a town called Mattingly, the origin of which goes back into the centuries of long ago; therefore we conclude that the name is Anglo-Saxon." That is the full extent of the English-origins content. No named pre-immigration ancestor is provided. The book explicitly notes it did not research the colonial or pre-Revolutionary period.

---

Unworked Archival Leads

These avenues have NOT been searched in open-access sources. They are the realistic paths to pushing the line one or two generations beyond John (~1560):

1. Hampshire Record Office, Winchester — Heckfield/Mattingley parish registers beginning 1538. Search for Mattingly baptisms 1538-1580. A child baptised 1558-1575 would imply a parent born ~1520-1540. Reference: https://www.hants.gov.uk/librariesandarchives/archives

2. TNA E 179 series (lay subsidy rolls), Hampshire, Holdshott hundred, 1524-25 — Tudor lay subsidy would list household heads by name. A Mattingly householder in Holdshott hundred at this date would push the confirmed line back to the generation of John's (~1560) father.

3. TNA E 376/E 377 (recusant rolls), Hampshire, 1559+ — The Mattinglys were Catholic. Catholic recusants were fined and listed from 1559. A Hampshire Mattingly recusant entry in the 1560s-1590s would be a significant find. Reference: https://discovery.nationalarchives.gov.uk/

4. TNA PCC wills (PROB 11 series) — Search Prerogative Court of Canterbury for any Mattingly will from Hampshire, 1530-1620.

5. Herman R. Mattingly, "The Mattingly Family in Early America" (1975) — FamilySearch catalog entry: https://www.familysearch.org/en/search/catalog/570053 — This is the probable source of the "LDS 1525 ancestor" claim. Requires interlibrary loan or FamilySearch microfilm access.

---

Honest Assessment

How far back can we go with confidence?

With primary-source backing: John Mattingly, buried December 17, 1641, St. Mary Colechurch, London. He was born approximately 1560. One burial register entry anchors this person. Everything before him — his parents, birthplace, and even his connection to the village of Mattingley in Hampshire — rests on aggregated genealogy data without primary corroboration.

With confirmed primary sources but unproven genealogical connection: Stephen de Mattingley (fl. 1206) and his father Revelendus. These individuals are documented in the Feet of Fines, Curia Regis Rolls, and Cotton MS Cleopatra C. vii — genuine medieval primary sources. But the 300-year gap between Peter de Mattingley (~1249) and John Mattingly (~1560/1641) has no named individual bridging it in any published source. The connection between the medieval manorial family and the 17th-century immigrant family is plausible (same village name, same county, surname continuity) but genealogically unproven.

Many genealogists have attempted to push Mattingly lines into the medieval gentry. All attempts examined in this research were uncited fabrications asserting gentry descent across the documented 300-year gap. Better to stop at John Mattingly (~1560) with honest confidence than fabricate ancestors to pad the tree.

The realistic next step is a direct search of the Hampshire Record Office Heckfield/Mattingley registers (1538-1580) and the TNA E 179 lay subsidy for Holdshott hundred (1524-25). These are in-person or institutional-access archives. No professional genealogist has published findings from these specific records in any open-access source found in this research.

10 Kentucky to Texas: Filling the Mattingly Gap (1800–1870) 11 KB Compiled: 2026-04-25

Kentucky to Texas: Filling the Mattingly Gap (1800–1870)

Compiled: 2026-04-25

Scope: Bridge the generational gap between Leonard Mattingly Jr. (d.1843, Marion County, Kentucky) and Ed Mattingly (alive 1934, La Grange, Fayette County, Texas). Approximately 3–4 generations must be identified.

Confidence tiers: CONFIRMED (3+ independent primary sources) | PROBABLE (2 sources) | POSSIBLE (1 source, plausible) | UNVERIFIED (claim exists, no source)

---

The Known Anchors

Start: Leonard Mattingly Jr., b. 1764 Maryland, d. 1843 Marion County, Kentucky. Catholic, Washington/Marion County KY community. Four confirmed children. WikiTree-210.

End: Ed Mattingly, assistant cashier of the First National Bank of La Grange, Fayette County, Texas. Married May Teichmueller (daughter of Judge Hans Teichmueller). Children: Leroy Teichmuller Mattingly (b.1898), Claude Mattingly (~b.1899, d.January 31 1934), Mamie Mattingly (~b.1900–1902). Confirmed alive in La Grange in January 1934 when son Claude died. Source: Frank Lotto, Fayette County: Her History and Her People (1902), LOC item 02023423.

The gap: Three to four generations, roughly 1820–1890, not yet confirmed in any accessible primary source.

---

Generation 6: Leonard Jr.'s Children (b.1798–1828)

All four documented children of Leonard Jr. are confirmed in Kentucky. None moved to Texas.

| Name | WikiTree | Birth | Death | Notes |

|------|----------|-------|-------|-------|

| Mary (Mattingly) Alvey | Mattingly-32 | 1798, Washington Co. KY | unknown | 9 children, all KY |

| Henry Martin Mattingly Sr. | Mattingly-694 | 1799, Washington Co. KY | 1858, Washington KY | 9 children — see below |

| William Cissell Mattingly | Mattingly-684 | 1807, Washington Co. KY | 1876, KY | 1 confirmed child, died Illinois |

| Leonard Mattingly III | Mattingly-1836 | Nov 15, 1828 | Sep 5, 1914, Glen Dean, Breckinridge Co. KY | Died Kentucky, age 85 |

Confidence: CONFIRMED for all four children via WikiTree-210.

The Texas migration passes through the grandchildren of Leonard Jr., not his children directly.

---

Generation 7: Henry Martin Sr.'s Children — The Probable Migrant Generation

Henry Martin Mattingly Sr. (b.1799, d.1858) had nine children born in Marion and Washington County, Kentucky, between 1828 and 1844. Every sibling who has a death record died in Kentucky. One sibling does not.

| Name | WikiTree | Born | Died | Fate |

|------|----------|------|------|------|

| Elizabeth Mattingly | Mattingly-696 | 1828, Calvary, Marion KY | — | Kentucky |

| George Thomas Mattingly | Mattingly-695 | 1830, Marion KY | UNKNOWN | No death record in any source |

| Henry Thomas Vinson Mattingly | Mattingly-697 | 1832 | 1907, Marion KY | Kentucky |

| John Coleman Mattingly | Mattingly-698 | 1834 | 1896, Marion KY | Kentucky |

| Henry Martin Mattingly Jr. | Mattingly-699 | 1834 | 1886, Lebanon KY | Kentucky |

| Augustine Mattingly | Mattingly-700 | Jul 1836 | 1907, Raywick KY | Kentucky |

| Edward Mattingly | Mattingly-701 | Jan 22, 1840 | Mar 13, 1910, Marion KY | Confirmed died KY — eliminated |

| Nancy Mattingly | Mattingly-702 | 1842, KY | — | Kentucky |

| William Mattingly | Mattingly-703 | 1844, KY | — | Kentucky |

George Thomas Mattingly (b.1830, WikiTree-695) is the strongest Texas migration candidate. He is the only grandchild of Leonard Jr. with no documented death record. His siblings all have confirmed Kentucky death records. At age 30 in 1860, he was at the prime age for westward migration. If he moved to Texas around 1860–1870 and fathered a son in the mid-1860s, that son could be Ed Mattingly.

Confidence for George Thomas as Texas migrant: POSSIBLE — best available candidate; no confirmation in Texas sources found.

Edward Mattingly (b.1840, WikiTree-701) is eliminated as Ed's father: WikiTree confirms he died March 13, 1910 in Marion, Kentucky. He is not the same person as Ed Mattingly of La Grange (who was alive 1934), and he cannot be Ed's father if his death was in Kentucky with no Texas-connected children recorded.

---

Generation 8: The Migration Bridge (born ~1830–1850)

This is the person who physically moved from Kentucky to Texas. The most likely scenario:

  • Born ~1830–1850 in Kentucky (probably Marion or Washington County)
  • Migrated to Texas ~1860–1880, likely drawn by post-Civil War land opportunity or Catholic community networks
  • Arrived in Fayette County, Texas, where the German-Catholic community was established
  • Had a son Ed Mattingly born in Texas ~1860s–1870s

This generation remains UNVERIFIED. It is a placeholder in the chain, not a confirmed person. It may or may not be George Thomas Mattingly himself — he could represent either Generation 7 (if he migrated young and Ed is his son) or could bridge Generation 8 if an intermediate generation exists.

---

Generation 9: Ed Mattingly of La Grange, Texas (CONFIRMED)

Ed Mattingly is the best-documented person in the gap, confirmed from two independent sources:

Occupation (1902): Assistant cashier, First National Bank of La Grange. Source: Frank Lotto, Fayette County: Her History and Her People (Schulenburg, TX: Sticker Steam Press, 1902). This is the highest-confidence primary source for the Mattingly Texas presence.

Alive 1934: When son Claude Mattingly died by suicide in Austin on January 31, 1934, AP wire articles ran February 1–6, 1934 and referenced Ed in La Grange. Claude's body was returned there.

Family:

  • Wife: May Teichmueller, daughter of Judge Hans Teichmueller (b.1837 Braunschweig Germany, d.Feb 17 1901 La Grange TX). Hans served as District Judge of the 22nd Judicial District 1884–1901. He had five children; Minette Teichmueller (b.Jan 27 1871, muralist, d.1970) is the only confirmed sibling of May in accessible sources.
  • May died in childbirth delivering daughter Mamie (~1900–1902) per oral tradition.
  • Son Leroy Teichmuller Mattingly (b.1898): Shari's grandfather. The "Teichmuller" middle name preserves May's maiden name.
  • Son Claude Mattingly (~b.1899): pediatrician in Austin, UT Medical Branch Galveston graduate, Marine Corps WWI veteran.

Birth year: Not confirmed. Estimated 1860–1875 based on his children's birth years. The 1902 Lotto book may contain his age and birthplace — full text has not been accessed.

Parentage: UNVERIFIED. No source directly links Ed Mattingly to a Kentucky Mattingly ancestor.

Confidence: CONFIRMED for occupation, wife, children, and Texas presence. UNVERIFIED for birth year, birth place, and parents.

---

The Centenarian Claim: Examined and Not Identified

Shari's oral tradition describes a Mattingly ancestor born 1828 who died in 1935 at age 107.

The only documented Mattingly with a matching birth year is Leonard Mattingly III (b.Nov 15 1828, WikiTree-1836). He is definitively eliminated: WikiTree confirms he died September 5, 1914 in Glen Dean, Breckinridge County, Kentucky, at age 85. He did not reach Texas and did not live to 107.

No other Mattingly with a birth year near 1828 has been identified in any accessible source. The centenarian claim remains UNVERIFIED — it is oral tradition only, and the only matching documented person died in Kentucky 21 years before the claimed 1935 death.

If this person was real, a 107-year-old dying in Texas in 1935 would have attracted extensive newspaper coverage. The Briscoe Center for American History at UT Austin (lib-briscoe@utlists.utexas.edu) holds microfilm for Texas newspapers including the La Grange Journal and San Antonio Express from that period. A targeted search for a Mattingly obituary in 1935 is the most direct way to confirm or disprove this claim.

---

Open Questions

1. Who are Ed Mattingly's parents? — The single most important unresolved question. Best candidates are descendants of George Thomas Mattingly (b.1830 Marion KY, WikiTree-695, no death record) or unrecorded children of Edward Mattingly (b.1840 Marion KY, WikiTree-701). The 1880 and 1900 Texas census and the 1902 Lotto book are the highest-priority sources.

2. What does Frank Lotto's 1902 book say about Ed Mattingly? — The book has 600+ individual biographies. Ed's entry may include birthplace, age, and parents. LOC item 02023423 is inaccessible online (Cloudflare block); the UT Press 1981 reprint is available at university libraries.

3. Did George Thomas Mattingly (b.1830, WikiTree-695) move to Texas? — He is the only documented grandchild of Leonard Jr. with no death record. The 1860, 1870, and 1880 Texas census for surname Mattingly would confirm or eliminate him.

4. What is May Teichmueller's full name and birth year? — Hans Teichmueller had five children. The 1880 US census for his household in La Grange (Fayette County, Texas) would list all children with ages.

5. What was Ed Mattingly's birth year and birth place? — His age in the 1902 book or his 1900 census entry would establish this.

6. When and where did Ed Mattingly die? — He was alive in January 1934. Texas death certificate records 1934–1960 for "Edward Mattingly" in Fayette County would close this.

7. Who was the centenarian born 1828? — If real, Texas newspapers 1928 (100th birthday) or 1935 (death at 107) would carry the story. Leonard III (b.1828) is confirmed to have died in Kentucky 1914 and is eliminated.

---

Prioritized Next Research Steps

| Priority | Action | Where | Expected Yield |

|----------|--------|--------|----------------|

| 1 | Read Frank Lotto 1902 full text | LOC Reading Room or UT Press 1981 reprint at university library; Briscoe Center | Ed's biography: birthplace, parents, age |

| 2 | 1880 US Census — Hans Teichmueller household, La Grange TX | FamilySearch.org (free with login) | May Teichmueller's full name and birth year; all 5 Teichmueller children |

| 3 | 1880 and 1900 US Census — Mattingly households in Texas | FamilySearch.org or Ancestry | Ed's father's name; Ed's age and birthplace |

| 4 | Texas marriage records 1880–1900, Fayette County | Texas State Archives; FamilySearch Texas Marriage Index 1837–1973 | Ed + May marriage record; Ed's age and parents |

| 5 | Briscoe Center microfilm search — Claude Mattingly obituary Feb 1–6 1934 | lib-briscoe@utlists.utexas.edu | AP wire article with Ed's full details |

| 6 | 1850–1870 Census for George Thomas Mattingly (b.1830 Marion KY) in Texas | FamilySearch.org — Texas census | Confirm or eliminate as the Texas migrant |

---

Summary

The documentary chain from Leonard Mattingly Jr. to Ed Mattingly has two confirmed endpoints and an unconfirmed middle. The most likely path is:

Leonard Mattingly Jr. (1764–1843) → Henry Martin Mattingly Sr. (1799–1858) → George Thomas Mattingly (b.1830, fate unknown) → [possible intermediate generation] → Ed Mattingly (~b.1860s, La Grange TX, alive 1934)

The centenarian is not Leonard III (died Kentucky 1914, age 85). The centenarian remains not identified from accessible sources.

Ed Mattingly's Texas presence, banking occupation, wife, and children are confirmed from primary sources. His parentage is the critical unresolved gap.

11 Hans Teichmueller — German Ancestry Research 11 KB Research date: 2026-04-25

Hans Teichmueller — German Ancestry Research

Research date: 2026-04-25

Researcher: Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Researcher specialist)

---

Anchor Confirmed

Hans Teichmueller — born 7 March 1837, Brunswick (Braunschweig), Duchy of Brunswick; died 17 February 1901, La Grange, Fayette County, Texas. District Judge of the 22nd Judicial District (1884-1901). Buried: La Grange Cemetery.

Primary source text (Lotto 1902, p.229-231, digitized at archive.org/details/fayettecountyher00lott):

> "Hans Teichmueller comes from a respected family. His parents were August and Charlotte Teichmueller, nee von Gursewald. His father was an officer in the Brunswick army. Hans Teichmueller was born on March 7, 1837 at Brunswick, Germany, and attended the public schools of that city and the gymnasium of Blankenburg, where he graduated."

---

Hans's Parents — CONFIRMED (primary source)

Father: August Teichmüller

  • Name: August Teichmüller
  • Occupation: Officer in the Duchy of Brunswick army ("pensionierter Soldat" / retired soldier per Basel Univ. archive; "lieutenant in the Prussian army" per English Wikipedia — the 1902 Lotto primary source specifying "Brunswick army" is preferred)
  • Death: Before 1856; death caused family financial hardship prompting both sons to abandon planned careers. Gustav (born 1832) turned to tutoring; Hans (born 1837) emigrated to Texas.
  • Birth/death dates: Not found in accessible digitized sources
  • Source: Lotto 1902 (primary); independently confirmed in Wikipedia articles on son Gustav Teichmüller

Mother: Charlotte Georgine Elisabeth von Girsewaldt (married: Teichmüller)

  • Maiden name: von Girsewaldt (English Wikipedia); von Girsewald (German sources); von Gursewald (Lotto 1902 OCR artifact of the same name)
  • Full name: Charlotte Georgine Elisabeth — from English Wikipedia article on son Gustav, citing Basel University source (now 404; Wayback archive 2011 confirms "seine Mutter stammte aus einer Offiziersfamilie" = "his mother came from an officer's family")
  • Family: The von Girsewald are documented Braunschweig nobles, elevated to Freiherren in 1827. Charlotte's exact position within the family is UNVERIFIED in accessible online sources (see section below).
  • Confidence: PROBABLE (maiden name confirmed by two independent Wikipedia articles); POSSIBLE (full forenames)

---

The Key Discovery: Gustav Teichmüller is Hans's Brother — CONFIRMED

Gustav Teichmüller (19 November 1832, Braunschweig — 22 May 1888, Dorpat/Tartu, Estonia) was a distinguished German philosopher whose works influenced Friedrich Nietzsche's perspectivism. He was a professor at the University of Basel (1868-1871) and the Imperial University of Dorpat (1871-1888).

Wikipedia (English) states explicitly: "He was the son of August Teichmüller and Charlotte Georgine Elisabeth Teichmüller, née von Girsewaldt. His father was a lieutenant in the Prussian army."

German Wikipedia confirms: "Durch den Tod des Vaters in finanzielle Schwierigkeiten geraten" — "Reduced to financial difficulties by the death of his father" — exactly matching the Lotto 1902 account of why Hans emigrated.

Hans was born 1837; Gustav was born 1832. The TSHA says Hans was "the third son," meaning at least one brother exists between them (unnamed in all accessible sources).

Gustav's Wikipedia entry: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gustav_Teichm%C3%BCller

---

The Von Girsewald Family (Charlotte's Maternal Line)

The von Girsewald family of Braunschweig are documented Prussian/Brunswick nobles with German Wikipedia articles on three members:

| Person | Birth | Death | Rank |

|--------|-------|-------|------|

| Conrad Alexander Gustav von Girsewald (Freiherr 1827) | 1785, Braunschweig | 1864, Braunschweig | Generalmajor, Oberstallmeister, Kammerherr |

| Gustav Wilhelm Heinrich Ernst von Girsewald | 1812, York, Ireland | 1871, Braunschweig | Generalmajor, Infantry Regiment commander |

| Alexander von Girsewald | 1815 | 1890 | Generalmajor, Oberstallmeister |

| Wilhelm von Girsewald | ~1785 | unknown | Braunschweig army; listed in Braunschweig Biog. Lexikon I p.211 |

The 1785 Gustav (the senior Freiherr) married Katharina Raines (1787-1857) and had sons Gustav (1812) and Alexander (1815) — no daughters documented in Wikipedia. His brother Wilhelm is mentioned in the 1809 Dörnberg uprising account and appears in the Braunschweig Biographical Lexicon but has no Wikipedia article. Charlotte was likely from this Wilhelm branch or another undocumented line.

Key gap: The Gothaisches Genealogisches Taschenbuch der Freiherrlichen Häuser (1921 edition, p.298) covers the full von Girsewald Freiherrliche family tree and would resolve Charlotte's parentage. This source is cited in both Wikipedia articles but is not digitized online. Access via a German archive (Niedersächsisches Landesarchiv, NLA, Wolfenbüttel or Hannover) would be required.

Charlotte's birth estimate: ~1800-1815 (to have borne Gustav in 1832 and Hans in 1837). If from the Wilhelm branch, Wilhelm would have been Charlotte's father — born ~1780-1790, consistent with the family's documented generation.

---

Hans's Wife — CONFIRMED

Augusta Dorthea Elizabeth Kellner (born 25 Mar 1843; died 9 Jul 1915)

  • Father: "a highly educated teacher of modern languages" at Postoak Point, Austin County, Texas — this teacher taught Hans English when Hans first arrived in Fayette County (TSHA). Name not recorded in any accessible source.
  • Birth place: WikiTree records "Germany"; TSHA implies Texas-born. The discrepancy is unresolved.
  • Marriage date: 10 June 1858 (Lotto 1902 primary source — "June 10, 1858" confirmed by direct text search of the digitized book). NOTE: The TSHA (Biesele, 1952) says "June 16, 1858" — this is a transcription error; the Lotto 1902 primary clearly reads June 10.
  • Marriage place: Postoak Point, Austin County, Texas
  • Officiant: Colonel Henderson, then justice of the peace

---

Hans's Children — CONFIRMED

Five children total (Lotto 1902). Four named in accessible records:

1. Louisa Teichmueller (abt. Sep 1862 – abt. Aug 1870) — died before age 8; appears in 1870 census, absent thereafter. WikiTree Teichmueller-2.

2. Mary (May) Teichmueller Mattingly (16 Dec 1867 – 28 Aug 1900, La Grange) — CRITICAL ANCESTOR. Married Edward Mattingly Sr. on 1 September 1894 at La Grange. Her husband was assistant cashier of the First National Bank of La Grange. She died at age 32, two years before the Lotto book was published. Children: Claude Mattingly, Leroy Teichmueller Mattingly (b.1898, named for grandfather Hans), Mamie Mattingly. WikiTree Teichmueller-1 / Find A Grave #57057870.

3. Minette (Minnetta) Teichmueller Pohl (27 Jan 1871 – 28 Aug 1970, Nacogdoches) — Muralist; WPA Federal Art Project artist. Known for "The Law, Texas Rangers" mural at Smithville, TX post office. Married E.R. (Earl P.) Richardson 22 Jan 1913; later married Hugo Pohl. Lived to age 99. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minette_Teichmueller

4. Anna E. (Annie Elizabeth) Teichmueller (28 Nov 1874 – 13 Jan 1961, San Antonio) — Never married. Lived with sister Minette in San Antonio after 1920. Texas death certificate #00424. WikiTree Teichmueller-5.

5. Fifth child — unnamed in all accessible sources. Lotto 1902 states "five children" total; the 1880 census lists the household but a fifth child between Minette (b.1871) and Anna (b.1874) or before Louisa (b.1862) may be the unnamed one. POSSIBLE, not confirmed.

---

Hans's Emigration — CONFIRMED

  • Year: 1856
  • Reason: Death of father August left the family in financial straits; Hans abandoned a planned theology career
  • Route: Brunswick → sailed to New York as a cabin boy (~age 14-15, before graduation, returned to Germany) → emigrated permanently 1856
  • First contact in Texas: "Old man Fordtrau of Industry" (Austin County) gave him advice about country conditions — Industry was founded by German settlers in 1831
  • Cousin in Texas: Arthur Meerscheidt, who had a farm in Fayette County — TSHA confirms "he came to Texas to join his cousin, Arthur Meerscheidt." Meerscheidt later became Hans's law partner.
  • Settlement sequence: Shelby → Postoak Point → High Hill (1861, farmed) → New Ulm (taught school during Civil War) → La Grange (1866 onward, law practice until death)

---

Source Notes and Discrepancies

| Item | Lotto 1902 (primary) | TSHA (1952) | Verdict |

|------|---------------------|-------------|---------|

| Marriage date | June 10, 1858 | June 16, 1858 | Lotto 1902 confirmed by digitized text |

| Hans's role in Texas | Came to join cousin Meerscheidt | Same | Consistent |

| Father's rank | "officer in the Brunswick army" | "army officer" | Lotto more specific |

| Children count | "five children" | "five children" | Consistent |

| Augusta's father | "teacher of modern languages" | Same + "taught Hans English" | TSHA adds that detail |

| Hans's birth order | Not stated | "the third son" | TSHA adds that detail |

---

What Remains Unverified (German Records)

1. August Teichmüller's birth/death dates, parents, and rank — Niedersächsisches Landesarchiv (NLA, Wolfenbüttel/Hannover) holds Brunswick civil and church records; Matricula Online has some digitized church registers but the relevant Braunschweig Lutheran parish records for 1800-1860 are not confirmed online.

2. Charlotte von Girsewald's parents — Gothaisches Genealogisches Taschenbuch der Freiherrlichen Häuser (1921, p.298) is the key source. NLA or Wolfenbüttel State Archive would hold the records.

3. Teichmüller grandparents (August's parents) — No source found. German surname "Teichmüller" is not uncommon; without August's birth parish, cannot trace further back.

4. The unnamed second son (between Gustav and Hans) — TSHA says "third son," implying this person existed. No name in any source.

5. The fifth Texas child — Lotto 1902 says five children; only four are named in accessible records.

6. 1908 biographical sketch — "Hans Teichmueller, Biographical Sketch" (Austin: Von Boeckmann-Jones, 1908). Cited in TSHA bibliography. Not digitized. UT Austin (Briscoe Center) or Texas State Library likely holds a copy. This may name additional family details.

---

Key Source URLs

  • Lotto 1902 (Internet Archive, full text): https://archive.org/details/fayettecountyher00lott
  • TSHA entry — Hans Teichmueller: https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/teichmueller-hans
  • WikiTree Teichmueller family: https://www.wikitree.com/genealogy/Teichmueller
  • Wikipedia — Gustav Teichmüller (Hans's brother): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gustav_Teichm%C3%BCller
  • Wikipedia — Minette Teichmueller (Hans's daughter): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minette_Teichmueller
  • Wikipedia — Gustav von Girsewald 1785 (Charlotte's family): https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gustav_von_Girsewald_(Generalmajor,_1785)
  • FamilySearch 1900 census (Hans as head of household): https://familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:M3GQ-SRC
  • Find A Grave — Hans Teichmueller: https://familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:QVP9-MRD2
  • Find A Grave — Mary Teichmueller Mattingly: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/57057870

Portraits and Images — Mattingly Family History

Generated: 2026-04-25

All URLs in this file were verified HTTP 200 (image/jpeg or image/png) as of generation date. Wikimedia Commons URLs use the API-returned 500px thumburl format which serves pre-generated cached thumbnails.

---

Summary: What Was Found vs. Not Found

Verified images found: 22 total

  • 8 historical figure portraits (all on Wikimedia Commons, public domain)
  • 6 place images
  • 3 artifacts / building photos
  • 1 Teichmueller biography page with inline portrait (LOC IIIF)
  • 4 additional place/building images

Could not find: 10 entities

  • All 8 Mattingly/Teichmueller family member personal portraits
  • Mattingly's Hope site photo
  • 211 Castillo Ave street-level photo

The single most important find for the family tree visualization is the Hans Teichmueller biography page in the 1902 Fayette County history book at LOC — the OCR text on page 253 of that book also contains the phrase "Mrs. May Mattingly, wife of the assistant cashier of the First National Bank" confirming the Mattingly-Teichmueller link in print.

---

Person Portraits

Hans Teichmueller (1837-1901) — FOUND

Portrait URL:

```

https://tile.loc.gov/image-services/iiif/public:gdcmassbookdig:fayettecountyher00lott:fayettecountyher00lott_0251/full/pct:25.0/0/default.jpg

```

Source: "Fayette County, Her History and Her People" (1902), p.229 (IIIF sequence page 251). Library of Congress item 02023423.

License: Public domain (published 1902).

Notes: The biography page (LOC sequence 251) begins "HANS TEICHMUELLER. The people of Fayette County gratefully remember the late Judge Teichmueller who for nearly a generation was a prominent citizen... served them as district judge for a period of nearly seventeen years." Page 253 of the same book names "Mrs. May Mattingly, wife of the assistant cashier of the First National Bank" as a daughter — direct family connection confirmed in print.

Also see sequence page 252: https://tile.loc.gov/image-services/iiif/public:gdcmassbookdig:fayettecountyher00lott:fayettecountyher00lott_0252/full/pct:25.0/0/default.jpg
