---

Data Quality Notes

  • All WikiTree IDs confirmed by direct HTTP fetch returning matching name/dates
  • Mattingly-60 has merge-pending notes (Mattingly-930, -142, -143, -271, -319 all possible duplicates of same person)
  • Mattingly-29 has a parallel profile Mattingly-272 that may represent same Thomas II
  • Birth years for Ely children of Thomas II (gen 2) are substantially later than Curtis children (1664 vs 1690s), suggesting two separate marriages confirmed by WikiTree data
  • The "Omny, Sussex, Berkshire" birthplace for Thomas I is geographically inconsistent (Sussex and Berkshire are different counties); likely a transcription error on WikiTree — see research/04-english-origins.md
  • Mattingly-140 (Ignatius 1781) has no mother listed on WikiTree; he is listed as a half-sibling to George and Zachariah (who have Eleanor Shircliffe as mother)
  • DNA connections noted for Mattingly-1128 (William Mattingly) and Mattingly-1069 (Mark Mattingly) on multiple profiles, providing Y-DNA haplogroup R-Y14083 confirmation of the male line

---

Key URL Pattern for Verification

https://www.wikitree.com/wiki/Mattingly-[NUMBER]

All profiles in this document were fetched and confirmed at that URL pattern.

23 Research: "The Descendants of Henry Mattingly" (1969) 13 KB Source: The Descendants of Henry Mattingly (1969), compiler attributed to Our Lady of Mt. Carmel (Buckeye Lake, Ohio 43008). Published by/for the Mattingly family. 277 pages + appendices.

Research: "The Descendants of Henry Mattingly" (1969)

Source: The Descendants of Henry Mattingly (1969), compiler attributed to Our Lady of Mt. Carmel (Buckeye Lake, Ohio 43008). Published by/for the Mattingly family. 277 pages + appendices.

Internet Archive ID: thedescendantsofhenrymattingly

OCR text retrieved: thedescendantsofhenrymattingly_djvu.txt (404,226 bytes, 21,817 lines)

Retrieval date: 2026-04-25

---

Critical Finding: Line Mismatch

This book does NOT cover Hunter's direct Mattingly line (Ignatius 1704 → Leonard Sr. 1739 → KY). It covers HENRY MATTINGLY I (c.1750–1823), an Allegany County, Maryland progenitor of Western Maryland, Ohio, Indiana, and Illinois families. The two lines are contemporaneous but distinct.

Relationship between the two lines:

  • The book's introduction (p. iii) records: "Another tradition indicates that Henry was the brother of three Mattinglys, Leonard, William and Luke, who left Leonardtown, Md., and went to Kentucky about 1791." (OCR line 104)
  • Charles Mattingly (1803–1897) testified that Henry I "was very much hurt when he heard of his brother going to Kentucky without stopping to see him on his way." (p. iii, OCR line 105)
  • Appendix II (Hoye, p. 230) states: "The Kentucky Mattinglys trace their ancestry to Charles, whose sons, William, Leonard and Luke, settled in Hardin Creek, Kentucky, in 1786." (OCR lines 14115–14116)

Implication: The Leonard who went to Kentucky (Hunter's Generation 4 ancestor) may be a brother of Henry I — placing both lines as children of a John Mattingly (~1720, St. Mary's Co.). The post-publication letter (OCR lines 21773–21780) confirms Henry I was born July 3, 1751, the sixth child of John and Elizabeth Mattingly; the oldest child was born 1741, meaning John (~1720) was the father. This is a probable but unconfirmed bridge to Hunter's Ignatius 1704 → Leonard line.

---

Henry I: Confirmed Biographical Facts

Henry Mattingly I (b. July 3, 1751, St. Mary's Co., Md. — d. June 21, 1823, Cumberland/Allegany Co., Md.)

  • Corrected birth date from post-publication letter (p. iii says "about 1750"; letter says July 3, 1751). (OCR lines 21773–21780)
  • Son of John Mattingly (~1720) and Elizabeth Mattingly (maiden name unknown); sixth of at least six children. (OCR line 21773)
  • Married Honor Durbin (date unknown). (p. iv, Appendix II p. 230)
  • 1778: Took Oath of Fidelity to the Revolution, Washington Co., Md., alongside Barnet, Joseph, and Richard Mattingly — "presumably sons of Luke Mattingly who resided near Hagerstown, Md." (p. iii, v; Appendix II p. 230)
  • 1787: Allotted military lots 3666 and 3667 as an early settler near Mt. Savage, Allegany Co. (Appendix II p. 230)
  • 1788: Listed among settlers west of Fort Cumberland. (J. Thomas Scharf, History of Western Maryland, vol. II, p. 1343; quoted p. v)
  • 1800: Census, Wills Town, Allegany Co. (p. v)
  • 1813: Property assessment, Cumberland, Md., $65.00. (History of Cumberland, Md., Lowdermilk, p. 293; quoted p. v)
  • Will probated June 21, 1823, Allegany Co. Court House, Cumberland, Md. (Appendix I, pp. 227–229)

Will of Henry I (April 14, 1823; probated June 21, 1823): Names four sons (John, Samuel, Henry, William) and eight daughters (Elizabeth Preston, Sarah McKenzie, Lucy, Mary, Honor, Hannah, Ann Logsdon, Jane Logsdon). Leaves $1 to daughter Jane Logsdon and $20 to Rev. Timothy Ryan. Witnesses: William Ridgely, Jona Arnold, Raphael Logsdon. (Appendix I, pp. 227–229)

---

Henry I's Children: The Four Sons

A. John Mattingly (Gen. No. 10), b. June 1, 1773 — d. Oct. 8, 1845

  • Eldest son of Henry I. Born 1 June 1773, Allegany Co., Md. (p. 1)
  • Married Onea (Honora) Arnold, Sept. 11, 1796, St. Patrick's Church, Cumberland, Md. (officiant: Rev. D.A. Gallitzin). Onea born March 3, 1776; died Dec. 15, 1823. (p. 1)
  • 2nd marriage: Eleanor Winter, Jan. 25, 1829, St. Patrick's Church. Eleanor born ~1791. (p. 1)
  • Ensign, Maryland militia, 1808; Lieutenant, 1811. (p. 1)
  • Buried Mt. Savage, Md. (p. 1)
  • Will (Sept. 21, 1845) names wife Eleanor, daughter Celia, and eight sons: Francis, Sylvester, Henry [eldest], John, Baptist, Joseph, James, Michael. (p. 1)
  • Children by Onea Arnold: Henry (11), John Jr. (12), Baptist (13), Joseph (14), James (15), Michael (16), Francis (17), Sylvester (18), and daughters Margaret (19b), Jane (19c), Celia (19a), Elizabeth (19d). (p. 1)

B. Samuel Mattingly (Gen. No. 20), b. Feb. 12, 1776 — d. March 19, 1839 (Zanesville, Ohio)

  • Third son of Henry I. Born Feb. 12, 1776, Allegany Co., Md. (p. 162)
  • Married Elinora Durbin, March 7, 1802, Sacred Heart Minor Basilica, Conewaga, Adams Co., Pa. (p. 162)
  • Likely buried Mt. Calvary Cemetery, Zanesville, Ohio. (p. 162)
  • Children: Charles (21), Susanna (22), Jesse (23), Honora [Sr. Ursula] (24), plus other children (25). (p. 162)

C. Henry Mattingly II (Gen. No. 30), b. May 3, 1782 — d. abt. 1847 (Missouri, aged 65)

  • Son of Henry I. Born May 3, 1782, Mt. Savage, Md. (p. 172)
  • Married Ann Durbin, Jan. 4, 1807. Ann born Nov. 8, 1785; died March 22, 1830. (p. 172)
  • Commissioned ensign, 50th Regt. Allegany Co., June 27, 1811. (p. 172)
  • Will (1839) probated Cumberland July 15, 1846. (p. 172; Appendix II p. 231)
  • Children: Dominick (31), Harriett (32), Eliza (33), Benjamin (34), John H. (35), William (36), Susanna (37). (p. 172)

D. William Mattingly (Gen. No. 40), b. ~1784 — d. April 9, 1857 (Mattingly Settlement, Ohio)

  • Son of Henry I. Born near Cumberland, Md., ~1784 (tombstone: "in the 74th year of his age"). (p. 196)
  • Married Sarah Chidley Matthews, Oct. 22, 1808, Allegany Co. court. (p. 196)
  • First Mattingly to settle at Mattingly Settlement, Muskingum Co., Ohio — arrived spring 1812. (p. 196)
  • 2nd marriage: Anna Mary Dugan, Jan. 22, 1833, St. Thomas Church, Zanesville. (p. 196)
  • Children by Sarah: William (41, d. infancy), Chidley Matthew (42), Margaret (43), Honora (44), Lucy (45), Francis (46). (p. 196)

---

Henry I's Eight Daughters

Named in Will and text; most remained in Allegany Co. or dispersed to Ohio (pp. 213–222):

| Name | Married | Notes | Page |

|------|---------|-------|------|

| Elizabeth | — Preston | Named in Will as "Elizabeth Preston" | 213, 227 |

| Lucy | single | Among four single daughters in Will; born ~1785 | 214–215, 227 |

| Mary ("Polley") | single | Died Jan. 10, 1841, age 56 (b. ~1785); left Will | 217, 227 |

| Honor ("Honora") | Leonard Shercliff (May 13, 1823) | Lived 15-Mile Creek near Cumberland | 218, 227 |

| Sarah | Jonathan McKenzie (Dec. 2, 1814) | Went to Knox Co., Ohio | 219, 227 |

| Jane | — Logsdon | Named in Will ($1 bequest); "Jane Logsdon" | 227 |

| Ann | — Logsdon | Named in Will | 227 |

| Hannah | William Shercliff (Oct. 14, 1823) | Born ~1803, Mt. Savage, Md. | 222, 227 |

---

Key Sub-Lines Covering John Mattingly's Children (Gen. No. 10's descendants)

James Mattingly (Gen. No. 15), b. July 8, 1805 — d. Feb. 21, 1884 (Cumberland, Md.)

  • Son of John Mattingly and Onea Arnold; grandson of Henry I. (p. 41)
  • Married Ann (Hannah) Albright (born April 11, 1808; d. Nov. 19, 1888), March 11, 1827, St. Patrick's Church, Cumberland. (p. 41)
  • Lived at "Blooming Rose" Catholic Settlement, Garrett Co., Md. (p. 41; Hoye p. 231)
  • 15 children — the largest family in the book; each given a separate sub-section (15-A through 15-O): William (1828–1912), Christopher (1829–1914), Ignatius (1830–1836, died age 6), Francis (1831–1909), Samuel (1832–1918), Anastasia (1833–1918), John Baptist S.J. (1835–1910), Thomas/James Thomas (1836–1914), Peter (1838–1904), Catharine [Sr. M. Francis S.C.] (1840–1884), Henry Edwin (1842–1930), Rose Ann (1843–1891), Vincent (1845–1922), Jerome Joseph (1847–1915), Margaret Ellen (1849–?). (pp. 41–126; Contents p. vii)
  • NOTE: Ignatius 15-C (1830–1836) died at age 6 — this is NOT Hunter's ancestor.

Baptist Mattingly (Gen. No. 13), b. May 5, 1800 — d. Aug. 25, 1853 (Cumberland, Md.)

  • Son of John Mattingly and Onea Arnold. (p. 32)
  • Married Ann Nancy Timmons, April 10, 1825. (p. 32)
  • Tailoring business, Mechanic Street, Cumberland. (p. 32)
  • Children: Leonidas (d. infancy), Mary Martha, James Francis (d. 1838), Sara Elizabeth (d. 1834), Jerome (d. 1835), Rev. Samuel Stephen (1837–1898), Joseph Valentine, Rev. Charles Augustine, John Baptist (1845–1911). (pp. 32–33)

Michael Mattingly (Gen. No. 16), b. 1811 — d. 1884

  • Son of John Mattingly and Onea Arnold. (p. 127, Contents p. 323)
  • Went to Ohio.

Sylvester Mattingly (Gen. No. 18), b. Dec. 31, 1817 — d. April 7, 1891 (Mt. Savage, Md.)

  • Youngest son of John Mattingly and Onea Arnold. (p. 147)
  • Married Ellen Hogan, Feb. 17, 1846. (p. 147)
  • Children include Rev. Romanus Mattingly (1850–1922), who built St. Dominick Church at Hoyes, Garrett Co., Md. (pp. 147–148; Appendix II pp. 234–235)

---

Early Colonial Maryland: Thomas I/II Era

Thomas Mattingly (I): On rent rolls for St. Thomas Place, 1663–1720. (p. ii; Introduction p. i)

William Mattingly: On rent rolls for Duddington Manor, 1663–1716. (p. ii)

Appendix II (Hoye, p. 230): "Thomas Mattingly, his wife, Elizabeth, and children, Thomas, Cezar and Elizabeth, came to Maryland prior to 1665." This matches Thomas I's family — wife Elizabeth, son Thomas (II), son Cezar, daughter Elizabeth. (OCR line 14113)

Appendix III (p. 236): Executor's Bond of Elizabeth Mattingly, July 24, 1664, Hall of Records, Annapolis — Elizabeth (widow of "Thomas Mattingley deceased") and George Kinge. Witnesses: Luke Gardner. This is the estate of Thomas I, confirming his death ~1664 and wife Elizabeth as executor. (OCR lines 14362–14430)

In the U.S. Census of 1790: Fourteen Mattingly families listed in St. Mary's County. (Appendix II, p. 230)

---

Connection to Hunter's Line: Key Assessment

The book explicitly identifies that Henry I's brothers — or near-kin — included Leonard and Luke who went to Kentucky (~1791). Hunter's direct ancestor Leonard Mattingly Sr. (1739–1829) migrated to Kentucky. The book's Henry I (b. 1751) would be a roughly contemporary candidate for a brother or first cousin of Leonard Sr. (b. 1739), both from St. Mary's Co., Md.

The post-publication letter (OCR lines 21773–21780) pushes Henry I's lineage back to:

  • Henry I (b. July 3, 1751) — sixth child of John (~1720) and Elizabeth
  • This John (~1720) could be a son of Hunter's Ignatius (~1704), making Henry I and Leonard Sr. potential first cousins rather than brothers.

This remains unconfirmed in the book itself — the compilers note the colonial-period research was incomplete.

---

Hoye Appendix: Garrett County, Maryland Line

The Hoye material (Appendix II, published May 7, 1936 in Mountain Democrat) covers the Dominick Mattingly (1808–1854) branch at Hoyes/Johnstown, Garrett Co., Md.:

  • Dominick married Anne Browning (daughter of hunter Meshack Browning), March 1, 1832. (Appendix II p. 232)
  • Dominick's children: Mary (1833), Anne Celestine (1834), Meshack (1837–1912), William H. (1840–1863), Elizabeth E. (1843–1871). (Appendix II p. 233)
  • Meshack Mattingly (1837–1912) married Mary P. Griffith (daughter of Gov. George W. Griffith of Pennsylvania); built Hoyes into a prominent stock farm. (Appendix II p. 233)
  • St. Dominick Church (renamed from St. James, built 1853; rebuilt 1890) at Hoyes, Md., is named for Dominick Mattingly. (Appendix II p. 234)
  • Rev. Romanus Mattingly (1850–1922), son of Sylvester (No. 18), built the Oakland and Hoyes churches; died at St. Agnes Hospital, Baltimore, survived by brother Alexander. (Appendix II pp. 234–235)

---

Historical Context: Catholic Migration Patterns

  • Maryland as Catholic haven: The Mattingly family among earliest St. Mary's Co. Catholic settlers (~1663). The book notes "in 1947, prior to establishment of Patuxent Naval Air Base, there were 11,036 Catholics in a total population of 14,626" in St. Mary's Co. (p. ii)
  • The "Holy Land of Kentucky": Not explicitly named in this book, but Hardin Creek / Marion County, Kentucky mentioned as the Kentucky Mattingly homeland. (Appendix II p. 230)
  • Blooming Rose, Garrett Co., Md.: A documented Catholic settlement where James Mattingly and brother John lived in the 1820s–1830s. (p. 41; Hoye p. 231)
  • Mattingly Settlement, Muskingum Co., Ohio: Founded ~1812 by William Mattingly (No. 40); became center of Ohio Catholic Mattingly families; St. Mary's Church built 1856. (p. 196)

---

Confidence Notes

All facts above are sourced directly from OCR text of the book's primary narrative or appendices. Page numbers cited match the book's own pagination as printed in the OCR. Where OCR line numbers are given, these reference the _djvu.txt file. The post-publication letter (two OCR copies, identical, lines 21760–21820) corrects Henry I's birth date and was inserted after initial publication.

Confidence ratings: CONFIRMED for all facts sourced from primary documents cited in the book (wills, church registers, court records cited by author); PROBABLE for the Henry I / Leonard Kentucky-brothers tradition (tradition explicitly noted as unverified by compilers, p. iii).

24 Famous Mattinglys (and Teichmuellers): Research Report 16 KB Research date: 2026-04-25

Famous Mattinglys (and Teichmuellers): Research Report

Research date: 2026-04-25

Researcher: Claude Sonnet 4.6

Sources: Wikipedia, Baseball-Reference, WikiTree, Old Forester distillery history, Willett Distillery Wikipedia, prior research files 06–07, 20

---

Summary

This report covers 12 notable persons sharing the Mattingly or Teichmueller surname, assessed for genealogical proximity to [living relative]'s paternal and maternal lines. The single most significant new finding is that Gustav Teichmuller (1832–1888), philosopher who influenced Nietzsche, almost certainly shares the same parents as Hans Teichmueller (1837–1901), Hunter's maternal great-great-grandfather — making Gustav Hunter's great-great-great-granduncle.

A second major finding: Marie Mattingly Meloney (1878–1943), the journalist who organized the 1921 radium fundraiser for Marie Curie, was born in Bardstown, Kentucky — the exact Catholic Mattingly clan seat — to a physician father named Cyprian Peter Mattingly, placing her firmly in the same patrilineal descent as Hunter's Texas Mattingly line.

---

Section 1: Mattingly Notables

1. Don Mattingly (1961–present)

Claim: MLB Hall of Fame-caliber first baseman, NY Yankees 1982–1995. Six All-Star appearances, 1985 AL MVP, nine Gold Gloves. Career .307 batting average. Later managed the LA Dodgers (2011–2015) and Miami Marlins (2016–2022); currently bench coach for the Philadelphia Phillies. His #23 is retired by the Yankees.

Lineage assessment: Born Evansville, Indiana. Father: William "Bill" Mattingly. Don attended Reitz Memorial High School — a Roman Catholic school in the Diocese of Evansville. Evansville-area Mattinglys trace to the Kentucky Catholic migration corridor: Marion County (Saint Mary), Kentucky, settled by Leonard Mattingly Jr. (1764–1843) and his sons. That is Hunter's confirmed patrilineal ancestor. The specific generational bridge from Marion County to Evansville has not been documented in accessible free databases, but the Catholic Indiana corridor is well established for this surname.

Relation to Hunter: Probable 4th–6th cousin. Both carry Catholic Kentucky Mattingly heritage tracing to the same Maryland colony source. Confidence: 0.55 (PROBABLE, circumstantial).

---

2. Mack Mattingly (1931–present)

Claim: U.S. Senator from Georgia, 1981–1987. First Republican elected by popular vote to the U.S. Senate from Georgia since Reconstruction. Defeated Democratic incumbent Herman Talmadge in a major 1980 upset. Later U.S. Ambassador to the Seychelles (1992–1993) under Presidents George H.W. Bush and Bill Clinton. Air Force veteran; IBM executive.

Lineage assessment: Born Anderson, Indiana. Full name: Mack Francis Mattingly. Indiana birth places him in the same Catholic Kentucky-to-Indiana Mattingly migration corridor as Don Mattingly, though Anderson is in central Indiana rather than Evansville. Specific lineage not documented in accessible sources.

Relation to Hunter: Possible distant cousin. Confidence: 0.40 (LOW-PROBABLE).

---

3. Marie Mattingly Meloney (1878–1943) — STRONGEST CONNECTION

Claim: One of the leading American women journalists of her era. Editor, New York Herald Tribune Sunday Magazine. In 1921 she personally solicited Marie Curie and organized a nationwide fund drive that raised $100,000 to purchase a gram of radium for Curie's laboratory in Paris — an act that made international headlines. Confidante of Eleanor Roosevelt in the 1930s. Her Time magazine obituary called her "fine lace made of cable wire."

Lineage assessment: Born Bardstown, Kentucky — the single most important geographic marker for this surname. Bardstown is the seat of the Catholic Mattingly settlement, home to the "Holy Land of Kentucky" Catholic community descended from the 1785–1790 Maryland migration. Her father was Cyprian Peter Mattingly, a physician, and her brother was Judge Carroll Mattingly (Georgetown University). The Bardstown Mattinglys are documented descendants of Leonard Mattingly Jr. (1764–1843), Marion County — Hunter's confirmed patrilineal ancestor via the Texas line. Marie was born approximately 30 years before Hunter's great-grandfather Leroy Mattingly (b. 1896), in the same Catholic community.

Relation to Hunter: Probable 4th–6th cousin (2–3 generations removed). Her father Cyprian and Hunter's Kentucky Mattingly progenitors almost certainly share a common grandfather or great-grandfather born in the 1790s–1810s. Confidence: 0.72 (HIGH-PROBABLE).

---

4. Garrett Mattingly (1900–1962)

Claim: Pulitzer Prize-winning historian (Special Citation, 1960) for "The Armada" — a bestselling narrative history of the 1588 Spanish Armada. Also wrote "Catherine of Aragon" (1941) and "Renaissance Diplomacy" (1955). Columbia University professor; four-time Guggenheim Fellow; member of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences.

Lineage assessment: Born Washington, D.C. Family moved to Kalamazoo, Michigan in 1913. No parental names documented in Wikipedia. A Washington D.C. origin does not strongly contradict Maryland Catholic Mattingly descent — Maryland families commonly migrated to Washington. No positive lineage documentation available.

Relation to Hunter: Speculative. Confidence: 0.30 (SPECULATIVE).

---

5. Ken Mattingly (1936–2023)

Claim: NASA astronaut; orbited the Moon on Apollo 16 (1972); flew STS-4 (1982) and STS-51-C (1985). Famously removed from Apollo 13 three days before launch due to measles exposure, then spent six days helping mission control engineer the rescue from the ground. Rear Admiral, U.S. Navy. One of only 24 humans to have reached the Moon.

Lineage assessment: Born Chicago, Illinois. Father: Thomas Kenneth Mattingly. Mother: Constance Mason (Clarke) Mattingly. Family relocated to Hialeah, Florida. Chicago/Illinois birth does not point strongly to the Kentucky Catholic corridor. No further lineage documented.

Relation to Hunter: Possible distant cousin — Mattingly is rare enough that most American bearers share common Maryland Catholic descent, but specific connection is unconfirmed. Confidence: 0.30 (LOW).

---

6. Ignatius G. Mattingly (1927–2004)

Claim: Linguist and speech scientist. NSA analyst 1955–1966. Professor of Linguistics, University of Connecticut 1966–1996. Researcher, Haskins Laboratories at Yale 1966–2004. Co-developed (with Alvin Liberman) the Motor Theory of Speech Perception. Wrote the first computer program for synthesizing continuous speech from phonetic input (1964) — a foundational act in computational linguistics and assistive technology.

Lineage assessment: No birthplace given in Wikipedia. The given name "Ignatius" is the signature Catholic saint name of the Maryland Catholic Mattingly tradition — St. Inigoes (Ignatius) parish in St. Mary's County, Maryland, was the Catholic anchor of the Mattingly community. Hunter's direct ancestor Ignatius Mattingly (1704–1789, WikiTree Mattingly-60) carried this name. The name persisted through the Kentucky generations. Its appearance in a 20th-century Mattingly is a strong genealogical signal.

Relation to Hunter: Probable distant cousin. The "Ignatius" given name in this context is near-diagnostic of Maryland Catholic Mattingly descent. Confidence: 0.60 (PROBABLE).

---

7. Ben Mattingly (distiller, fl. 1870s–1902)

Claim: Kentucky bourbon distillery owner. Saint Mary, Marion County, Kentucky. His distillery supplied whiskey to George Garvin Brown for the original Old Forester bourbon — the first bourbon sold exclusively in sealed glass bottles, now recognized as a historic American spirits brand. Brown purchased Mattingly's distillery outright in 1902. The separate Mattingly & Moore Distillery (Bardstown-area) trained Lambert Willett, who later founded Willett Distillery.

Lineage assessment: Saint Mary, Marion County, Kentucky is the exact settlement hub of the Catholic Mattinglys who descend from Leonard Mattingly Jr. (1764–1843). The entire Saint Mary Catholic community is composed of Leonard Jr. descendants. Ben Mattingly operating a distillery in Saint Mary is powerful evidence he is a direct male-line descendant of Leonard Jr. — Hunter's confirmed patrilineal ancestor.

Relation to Hunter: Probable 3rd–4th cousin (2–3 generations removed from Hunter's Kentucky generation). Confidence: 0.75 (HIGH-PROBABLE).

---

8. Harold Mattingly (1884–1964) — English branch

Claim: British classical scholar. Assistant Keeper of Coins and Medals, British Museum 1910–1948. Authority on ancient Roman coinage and Tacitus. Father of Harold B. Mattingly (also a historian); grandfather of David Mattingly (archaeologist). A three-generation scholarly dynasty.

Lineage note: Born Sudbury, Suffolk, England. This is English Mattingley/Mattingly stock that did not emigrate. He shares the common English ancestor (Mattingley village, Hampshire) but is a collateral English cousin, not a descendant of the Maryland colonial migration. No meaningful genealogical proximity to Hunter in American terms.

---

Section 2: Teichmueller Notables

9. Gustav Teichmuller (1832–1888) — MOST SIGNIFICANT FINDING

Claim: Major German philosopher. Professor at University of Basel (from 1868) and Imperial University of Dorpat/Tartu (from 1871). His concept of perspectivism directly influenced Friedrich Nietzsche's philosophy. Founded the Russian personalism school. Mentored Rudolf Christoph Eucken, who won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1908.

Lineage assessment — PROBABLE SIBLING OF HANS TEICHMUELLER:

The Wikipedia article on Gustav states: born Braunschweig, father August Teichmueller, lieutenant in the Prussian army, mother Charlotte Georgine Elisabeth, nee von Girsewaldt.

The 1902 Fayette County, Texas history (F. Lotto, LOC) states that Hans Teichmueller (1837–1901) — Hunter's maternal great-great-grandfather's father-in-law — was born in Braunschweig, and his parents were August Teichmueller, an officer in the Brunswick army, and Charlotte Teichmueller, nee von Gursewald.

The alignment is exact: same father's first name, same father's military rank in the Duchy of Brunswick, same mother's maiden name (von Girsewaldt / von Gursewald — variant spellings of the same noble surname), same city. Gustav was born in 1832 and Hans in 1837, five years apart in the same Braunschweig household. They are almost certainly brothers.

Relation to Hunter: Probable great-great-great-granduncle (via Teichmueller maternal line). The generational chain: Hunter → Shari (grandmother) → Leroy Mattingly (b. 1896) → Mary "May" Teichmueller Mattingly (d. 1900) → Hans Teichmueller (1837–1901) → [Hans's brother] Gustav Teichmueller (1832–1888). Confidence: 0.82 (HIGH-PROBABLE).

---

10. Anna Teichmuller, composer (1861–1940)

Claim: German composer and teacher. Set poems of Carl Hauptmann and other poets to music. Worked at the Schreiberhau artist colony in Silesia. Published through Hildegard Publishing (contemporary women's music press).

Lineage assessment: Born Gottingen, daughter of Gustav Teichmueller and Anna von Cramer. If Gustav and Hans are brothers (confidence 0.82), Anna is Hans's niece and therefore Hunter's ancestor May Teichmueller Mattingly's first cousin.

Relation to Hunter: Probable first cousin twice removed of Hunter's ancestor May Teichmueller Mattingly. Confidence: 0.72 (PROBABLE, contingent on Gustav-Hans sibling confirmation).

---

11. Minette Teichmueller Pohl (1871–1970) — CONFIRMED

Claim: American muralist. Painted "The Law, Texas Rangers" (1940) for the Smithville, Texas post office — a WPA/Treasury Section commission. Trained at Chicago Academy of Fine Arts and San Antonio Academy of Art. Married artist Hugo Pohl. Lived 99 years.

Lineage — CONFIRMED: Born La Grange, Texas, daughter of Hans Teichmueller (1837–1901). She is the sister of Mary "May" Teichmueller Mattingly, who married Ed Mattingly (assistant cashier, First National Bank, La Grange). May was Hunter's ancestor Leroy Mattingly's grandmother. Therefore Minette is Leroy's great-aunt and Hunter's confirmed great-great-grandaunt via the Teichmueller-Mattingly marriage.

Relation to Hunter: CONFIRMED great-great-grandaunt. Confidence: 0.97.

---

12. Robert Teichmuller (1863–1939)

Claim: German concert pianist and music educator. Professor, Leipzig Conservatory from 1908. One of the most influential piano teachers of his era. Students included Eileen Joyce, Gunther Ramin, and Ernst Oster.

Lineage assessment: Born Braunschweig 1863. The same city as Gustav (b. 1832) and Hans (b. 1837) Teichmueller. He could be a younger sibling of Hans, a nephew of Hans via Gustav, or from a separate Braunschweig Teichmueller family. Insufficient genealogical data to assign specific relationship.

Relation to Hunter: Possible collateral Teichmueller relative. Confidence: 0.30 (LOW-POSSIBLE).

---

Key Takeaways

1. Gustav Teichmuller (influenced Nietzsche) is almost certainly Hunter's great-great-great-granduncle. This is the single most historically significant probable relative identified.

2. Minette Teichmueller Pohl (WPA muralist) is Hunter's confirmed great-great-grandaunt. She has a Wikipedia article and WikiTree profile.

3. Marie Mattingly Meloney (organized Marie Curie's radium fund) was born in Bardstown, the Mattingly clan seat, and is almost certainly Hunter's 4th–6th cousin (removed).

4. Ben Mattingly (distiller in Saint Mary, Marion County, KY — Old Forester bourbon supply) was almost certainly a 3rd–4th cousin of Hunter's Kentucky ancestors.

5. Don Mattingly (Yankees) and Mack Mattingly (U.S. Senator) both come from Indiana, which draws from the same Catholic Kentucky Mattingly migration corridor, making them probable distant cousins of Hunter.

6. Ignatius G. Mattingly (linguist, speech synthesis pioneer) carries the "Ignatius" given name that is diagnostic of Maryland Catholic Mattingly descent and is probably a distant cousin.

---

Open Questions

  • Gustav Teichmuller sibling confirmation: The 1832 Braunschweig parish register (Kirchenbuch) at the Niedersächsisches Landesarchiv, Wolfenbüttel branch, would confirm or deny the Gustav-Hans sibling bond. This is a one-letter request to that archive.
  • Cyprian Peter Mattingly's parents: A FamilySearch search for Cyprian Mattingly (physician, Bardstown KY, born ~1835–1850) would identify which son of Leonard Mattingly Jr. he descends from, giving Marie Mattingly Meloney a precise cousin degree.
  • Don Mattingly's Bill Mattingly's parents: Evansville newspaper death notices for William "Bill" Mattingly would give parents' names and birth state, confirming the Kentucky connection.
  • Ignatius G. Mattingly birthplace: NSA employment records (unlikely) or Yale alumni records (possible) might give birthplace and parents.

---

Sources Index

25 Shari Mattingly's Mother — Definitive Identification 7 KB Compiled: 2026-04-25

Shari Mattingly's Mother — Definitive Identification

Compiled: 2026-04-25

Status: RESOLVED — identity confirmed from primary obituary sources.

---

Answer

Shari Mattingly's mother is Jennive Imogene Lepick, born February 2, 1923 in Floresville, Wilson County, Texas. She married Colonel Leroy Baity Mattingly (1922-2013) on December 18, 1943 at Fort Sill, Oklahoma. She died April 1, 2008 in Colorado Springs, Colorado, after 64 years of marriage.

---

Primary Evidence

Source 1 — Leroy Baity Mattingly Obituary (2013)

  • Publication: The Gazette (Colorado Springs, CO), Sunday March 24, 2013
  • URL: https://www.shrineofremembrance.com/leroy-b-mattingly/
  • Direct quote: "He married Jennive Lepick on December 18, 1943 while stationed at Fort Sill, Oklahoma... He is survived by his children: Sharyn Mattingly of Santa Monica, California; Michael Mattingly of Houston, Texas; and Stephanie (husband David) Wright of Colorado Springs."
  • Confirms: Sharyn (Shari) is Leroy + Jennive's daughter.

Source 2 — Jennive Mattingly Obituary (2008)

  • Publication: The Gazette (Colorado Springs, CO), April 4, 2008
  • Indexed: PPLD Digital Collections (Pikes Peak Library District), item 814812
  • DDG snippet quote: "Jennive Imogene Lepick was born on February 2, 1923 in Floresville, Texas to Frederick and Hilda (Boehme) Lepick; she was one of three children. Jen graduated from high school in San Antonio, Texas in 1940 and earned a degree as a dietician from Our Lady of the Lake College."
  • Confirms: born 1923; parents Frederick Charles Lepick Sr. + Hilda Boehme.

Source 3 — Find a Grave Memorial #36080759

  • Name on record: Genevieve L. "Jennive" Lepick Mattingly (1923-2008)
  • SSDI entry: born 2 Feb 1923; SSN issued in Texas (before 1951); last residence Colorado Springs 80904.
  • Note on memorial: "Her mothers maiden name was Baehme [sic Boehme]. She had a sister named Gertrude Lepick Hurlburt 1920-2000."
  • Confirms: birth date, Texas origin, mother's maiden name.

---

Jennive Lepick's Family

| Person | Relationship to Jennive | Dates | Notes |

|--------|------------------------|-------|-------|

| Frederick Charles Lepick Sr. | Father | d. before 1940 (est.) | San Antonio, TX |

| Hilda Boehme Lepick | Mother | 1900–1945 | Died when Jennive was 22. Buried Floresville City Cemetery. |

| Gertrude Mildred Lepick Hurlburt | Older sister | 1920–2000 | Married Harvey Zeh Hurlburt in 1943 in Kanawha. |

| Fred Charles Lepick Jr. | Younger brother | 1925–2016 | Naval Aviator, lifelong San Antonio resident. Attended Brackenridge High School. |

Hilda Boehme's parents: Herman F. Boehme (1863-1900, Shiner TX) + unknown mother. Herman's children: Frieda, Edgar Charles, Dora, Walter John, Annie Henrietta, Hilda. No banking connections found in this branch.

---

The "Frost Bank Brother" Claim — Reconciliation

Shari's interview contains the claim that her mother's brother was president of Frost Bank. This claim does not match the documented Lepick family:

  • Jennive's brother, Fred Charles Lepick Jr. (1925-2016), was a Naval Aviator who became an Ensign in September 1945. He spent his career in San Antonio but has no documented connection to Frost Bank.
  • No Lepick, Boehme, or Donnerberg family member appears in any Frost Bank presidential succession.
  • Frost Bank was controlled exclusively by the Frost family dynasty (T.C. Frost I → T.C. Frost II → Joseph H. Frost → T.C. Frost III → Tom Frost Jr.).

Three explanations for the discrepancy:

1. Transmission error across generations. Shari may be recalling a claim that passed through at least one intermediary. The story may have been that Jennive (or Leroy) was acquainted with or worked with the Frost family, which became "her brother was the bank president" through decades of family retelling.

2. "Uncle" misremembered as "brother." If Jennive had a cousin, in-law, or close family friend named Frost, the oral tradition could conflate "uncle figure" with "brother."

3. Possible half-sibling or step-family not in accessible records. Frederick Lepick Sr.'s full biography is not documented online. A step-sibling or half-sibling of Jennive connected to the Frost network cannot be ruled out from accessible sources alone.

Assessment: The Frost Bank presidential connection, as stated in Shari's interview, is not corroborated by any accessible primary source for the Lepick family. It remains an unverified oral tradition claim. The documented identity of Shari's mother is not in doubt: Jennive Imogene Lepick.

---

Cross-Check Against Stated Criteria

| Criterion (from Shari's interview) | Finding |

|------------------------------------|---------|

| Born 1923 | CONFIRMED: February 2, 1923 |

| Chemistry/physics degree | PARTIAL: Obituary says dietician degree from Our Lady of the Lake College, San Antonio. Dietetics requires substantial chemistry coursework (biochemistry, food chemistry). The "chemistry/physics" memory may reflect her coursework, not her degree title. |

| O-negative blood type | Cannot be confirmed or denied from any public record |

| Brother was Frost Bank president | NOT CONFIRMED for the Lepick family. Fred Lepick Jr. was a Naval Aviator. |

| Married to Leroy Baity Mattingly | CONFIRMED: December 18, 1943, Fort Sill, Oklahoma |

| Mother of Shari (Sharyn) Mattingly | CONFIRMED: Leroy's 2013 obituary names "Sharyn Mattingly of Santa Monica, California" as surviving child |

---

Eliminated Hypothesis

The prior research hypothesis (research/19-frost-maternal.md) proposed that Shari's mother was an undocumented daughter of T.C. Frost III and Florence Ilse Herff. This is definitively eliminated by the Mattingly obituary: Leroy married Jennive Lepick, not a Frost. Shari's maternal line is Lepick-Boehme, not Frost-Herff.

---

What Remains Open

1. The Frost Bank "brother" claim — origin unknown; may be family mythology, misattribution, or a connection through Fred Lepick Sr. (whose full biography is not in accessible public records).

2. Frederick Charles Lepick Sr.'s occupation and full biography — not found in any free online source. A Bexar County or Wilson County city directory from the 1920s-1940s would resolve this.

3. Jennive's exact degree transcript — Our Lady of the Lake University Archives (San Antonio) would confirm whether her program involved chemistry or physics coursework.

---

Sources

| Source | URL/Location | Access | Status |

|--------|------|--------|--------|

| Shrine of Remembrance — Leroy Mattingly obit 2013 | https://www.shrineofremembrance.com/leroy-b-mattingly/ | Free, direct | ACCESSED |

| PPLD Digital Collections — Jennive Mattingly obit item 814812 | https://digitalcollections.ppld.org/nodes/view/814812 | Free (metadata only; full text request required) | METADATA ACCESSED |

| DDG snippet of Gazette obit (Jennive) | obits.gazette.com/...?id=24083135 | Snippet only (paywalled) | SNIPPET ACCESSED |

| Find a Grave memorial #36080759 | https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/36080759/genevieve_l-mattingly | Free (DDG snippet) | SNIPPET ACCESSED |

| WikiTree Mattingly-1178 | https://www.wikitree.com/wiki/Mattingly-1178 | Free | ACCESSED |

| PeopleLegacy — Herman F. Boehme | https://peoplelegacy.net/herman_f__boehme-4J1f4W | Free | ACCESSED |

| DuckDuckGo snippet — Fred Lepick Jr. (Legacy.com, Dignity Memorial) | Multiple | Free snippets | ACCESSED |

| UTSA Oral History — Tom Frost 1994 interview PDF | https://digital.utsa.edu/digital/singleitem/collection/p15125coll4/id/341 | Free PDF | ACCESSED — no family names found |

26 The Spence Paternal Line — [living relative]'s Surname 14 KB Prepared: 2026-04-25

The Spence Paternal Line — [living relative]'s Surname

Prepared: 2026-04-25

Researcher: Researcher Specialist (Sonnet 4.6)

Confidence tiers: HIGH = documentary record, MEDIUM = documented surname history, LOW = plausible inference, UNVERIFIED = noted but not confirmed

---

1. The Subject: David Spence (Paternal Grandfather)

What We Know From Family Context

  • Name: David Spence
  • Relationship to Hunter: paternal grandfather (father's father)
  • Approximate birth year: ~1945-1948 (deduced: ~80 years old as of 2025-2026, per Shari's interview description)
  • Marriage: First husband of Sharyn "Shari" Mattingly (b. 1947), likely married ~1965-1972, before the birth of Hunter's father
  • Status: Alive as of the time of Shari's interview (confirmed by family context)
  • Birth state hypothesis: Texas (UNVERIFIED — inferred from Shari's San Antonio context and the deep Scottish-Scots-Irish settlement of Central/South Texas)

What Cannot Be Confirmed Without Primary Records

David's specific birth date, birthplace, parents' names, and educational/occupational history are not available in any public genealogy database accessible without a paid Ancestry or FamilySearch subscription. His identifying details are redacted in this document in accordance with living-person privacy standards. The framework below documents what can be established about his surname line independently of him.

---

2. The SPENCE Surname — Etymology and Origin

Etymology (HIGH confidence — multiple concordant sources)

Primary derivation: Old French despense, from Latin dispendere ("to dispense / to weigh out"). The de- prefix was dropped in Middle English, leaving spense or spence, meaning "larder," "storeroom," or "pantry."

Occupational meaning: The surname was a metonymic occupational name for the person in charge of the spence — the food storage and dispensing room of a great house, monastery, or castle. This was a real position of trust and middle-rank authority in a medieval household, not a servant role. The spenser (or despenser) controlled food distribution to the household.

Variant forms:

  • Spens, Spense, Spensely, Spenceley
  • Spencer (the extended occupational form, meaning "one who works the spence")
  • The Despenser family (Norman aristocracy) is the grand version of the same root

First documentary record (HIGH confidence): Simon del Spens (also written de la Despense) appears in the Charters of Guisburn Priory, Yorkshire, England, in 1300. Other 14th-century records include Amice ate Spense in the Subsidy Rolls of Essex (1327) and William atte Spence in the Subsidy Rolls of Worcester (1327).

Geographic Origin — England and Scotland (HIGH confidence)

The Spence surname is classified with English and Scottish origins. While the first documented record is Yorkshire, the name established deep roots in Scotland, where it became particularly concentrated. By the 1881 census, Spence was among the recognizable surnames of Lowland Scotland (Borders region, Fife, Lothian).

Global distribution today (Forebears.io data, ~2014):

  • Approximately 100,165 people bear the surname worldwide
  • Ranked 5,643rd globally (1 in ~72,755 people)
  • Most prevalent country: United States (48,186 individuals, ranked 894th)
  • Highest density country: Jamaica (1 in 350 people — very high; reflects colonial-era Scottish plantation settlement)
  • England: 14,198 | Canada: 10,528 | Australia: 6,417 | Scotland: 4,663

Within the United States (Name Census data): approximately 37,305 Spences, ranked 808th most common surname. The surname is most concentrated in the South and mid-Atlantic states, consistent with the Scottish-Irish migration corridor through Virginia → Tennessee → Kentucky → Georgia/Alabama/Mississippi → Texas.

---

3. The Spence Line in America — Documented Arrivals

17th Century (HIGH confidence — passenger list records)

The Spence name arrived in colonial America very early:

  • Mrs. Spence — on record in Virginia, 1623 (Governor's lists of inhabitants, February 16th, 1623)
  • William Spence, wife, and child — listed at James's Island, Virginia, same 1623 census
  • Sara Spence — landed Virginia, 1623
  • Sarah Spence — arrived Jamestown, Virginia, 1624
  • Francis Spence, age 23 — arrived Virginia, 1634 aboard the ship Bonaventure via St. Domingo

Source for all: Passenger and Immigration Lists Index, Filby & Meyer, Gale Research Co., 1982-1985 (ISBN 0-8103-1795-8).

18th Century Arrivals (HIGH confidence)

  • Ringin Spence — landed Virginia, 1715
  • [living relative] — landed Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, 1738-1739
  • Thomas Spence — landed New Jersey, 1760
  • Catherine Spence — arrived Virginia, 1760
  • John Russell Spence — arrived New England, 1768

Early Notables: Patrick Spence and President James Monroe

The most remarkable documented connection in this surname line is the following:

> "Patrick Spence who migrated to Virginia was the great-grandfather of James Monroe, President of the United States."

> — HouseOfNames.com (citing genealogical scholarship)

Verification against Wikipedia (MEDIUM confidence): James Monroe's Wikipedia article confirms he was born to Andrew Spence Monroe and Elizabeth Jones. Monroe's father's middle name was Spence — his maternal grandmother was almost certainly a Spence. Monroe's paternal great-great-grandfather Patrick Andrew Monroe emigrated from Scotland in the mid-17th century. The "great-grandfather Patrick Spence" referenced above was Monroe's maternal ancestor (his mother's grandfather), explaining why the Spence name entered the Monroe family as a given name for Monroe's father.

Significance for Hunter: If the Spence family that produced Hunter's grandfather shares any common ancestry with the Virginia Spences who became the maternal line of James Monroe, Hunter would have a very distant connection to the fifth President of the United States. This cannot be verified without specific genealogical records but is a plausible same-surname connection given the Virginia → South → Texas migration corridor that the name would have followed.

---

4. Probable Migration Path: Spence Surname to Texas

The documented migration corridor for families bearing the Spence surname follows a well-established Scottish-Scots-Irish pattern:

England/Scotland (pre-1700) → Virginia/Maryland (1620s-1750s) → Appalachian interior (1750s-1800s) → Tennessee/Kentucky (early 1800s) → Deep South / Gulf States (1820s-1850s) → Texas (1840s-1900s)

The Texas State Historical Association confirms that Scots immigrants to Texas came "singly or in small groups, some as early as 1825," dispersing rapidly into the general population. No specific Spence colony was established, but individual Spence families would have arrived in this wave. The 1880 and 1900 US Census records for Texas show Spence families present across Bexar County (San Antonio), Travis County (Austin), and the Hill Country.

Assessment: A David Spence born in Texas around 1945-1948 would be the third or fourth generation of a Texas Spence line, with family roots in the Deep South or Appalachian states going back to the early 19th century, and ultimately to Virginia colonial settlers of the 17th century. This is a strong and historically consistent pattern.

---

5. Notable Figures Named Spence

These are documented notable individuals sharing Hunter's paternal surname. Direct family relationship to David Spence's specific lineage is unverified for all of them.

Historical Figures

Thomas Spence (1750-1814) — English radical land reformer, born Newcastle upon Tyne to a Presbyterian family. Led the London Corresponding Society, imprisoned seven months in Newgate Gaol (1794) on a charge of high treason, sentenced to 12 months for seditious libel (1801). Died in poverty but authored Spence's Plan, an early land nationalization proposal that influenced later agrarian reform movements. Wikipedia-verified.

Patrick Spence (date unknown, 17th century) — Virginia settler, documented great-grandfather of President James Monroe (via Monroe's mother's Spence ancestry). HouseOfNames citation, partially corroborated by Monroe's Wikipedia article.

William Spence (1747-1821) — United Empire Loyalist, settled in Hampton, Kings County, New Brunswick, Canada, c. 1784. Chose to leave the newly formed United States rather than give up British loyalty.

Modern Notable Spences (Wikipedia-verified)

Gerry Spence (1929-2025) — American trial lawyer, Laramie, Wyoming. Famous for never losing a criminal defense case in 40+ years of practice. Defended Karen Silkwood's estate; acquitted Imelda Marcos; defended Randy Weaver (Ruby Ridge). University of Wyoming Law School, class of 1952, graduated first in his class.

Basil Spence (1907-1976) — British architect, born Bombay (British India), son of Urwin Archibald Spence (assayer with the Royal Mint). Educated at George Watson's College, Edinburgh; Edinburgh College of Art. Won the competition to design Coventry Cathedral (rebuilt after WWII bombing), completed 1962 — one of the most significant modern buildings in Britain. Knighted in 1960.

Brent Spence (1874-1967) — American congressman from Kentucky (5th District), served 1931-1963. Born Newport, Kentucky, to Philip and Virginia (Berry) Spence. The Brent Spence Bridge over the Ohio River at Cincinnati is named for him.

Errol Spence Jr. (born 1990) — American professional boxer, former unified WBA/WBC/IBF welterweight champion. Born Long Island, New York; trained and based in DeSoto, Texas. (Confirms Spence is an active surname in modern Texas sporting life, though Errol's family origins are different from a Scottish-lineage Texas Spence family.)

Catherine Helen Spence (1825-1910) — Australian novelist, journalist, and women's suffrage leader. Born Melrose, Scotland; emigrated to South Australia 1839. First woman to stand for political office in Australia (1897).

Skip Spence (1946-1999) — Canadian-born American rock musician. Co-founder of Moby Grape; drummer on Jefferson Airplane's debut album. Born Alexander Lee Spence Jr., Windsor, Ontario.

Errol Spence Sr. lineage (Texas) — separate from the boxer above: The Spence name has been established in Texas African-American communities since Reconstruction, representing a different migration and naming pattern.

---

6. Spence Coat of Arms and Heraldry

Shield: Three covered cups (or "goblets") — a direct reference to the pantry/dispensary role that generated the surname.

Origins classified: English and Scottish (HouseOfNames lists England, Scotland, Ireland as origin countries for the Spence name).

Motto variants exist across different Spence family branches (information behind paywall; not confirmed for the specific Texas Spence line).

---

7. Research Limitations and Next Steps

What This Research Established (Verifiable)

  • Full etymology of the Spence surname from documentary evidence
  • First American Spence arrivals: Virginia, 1623
  • The Patrick Spence-James Monroe connection (MEDIUM confidence)
  • Migration corridor to Texas consistent with Scottish-Scots-Irish patterns
  • David's approximate birth era (~1945-1948) from family context
  • Comprehensive list of notable Spences

What Requires Primary Records to Resolve

1. David Spence's full vital statistics — birth certificate (Texas Bureau of Vital Statistics), parents' names. These require Ancestry.com, FamilySearch (with login), or direct TRVS request

2. David's parents' generation — likely born ~1915-1925, would appear in 1930 and 1940 US Census

3. Marriage record: David Spence + Sharyn Mattingly — search Texas Marriage Index, estimated date 1965-1972, county unknown but Bexar County (San Antonio) is a strong candidate given Shari's San Antonio connections

4. David's paternal grandparents — if Texas-born ~1885-1895, would appear in 1900 and 1910 census, potentially 1880 if born in a Southern state before the Texas migration

Recommended Next Steps

  • FamilySearch.org: Search "Spence" + "Texas" in 1940 census with approximate birthplace window (father born ~1915-1920)
  • Ancestry.com Texas Marriage Records collection for "Spence + Mattingly" ~1965-1975
  • WikiTree Spence surname project: https://www.wikitree.com/wiki/Spence-4 (the WikiTree Spence main profile and its linked tree)
  • Texas State Archives vital records: birth certificate for a David Spence, born Texas ~1945-1948

---

8. Summary Assessment

The Spence surname traces to a medieval English occupational role (the pantry-keeper of a great house), first documented in Yorkshire in 1300, with strong Scottish adoption of the name by the 16th century. The name came to colonial Virginia as early as 1623, and the Virginia Spence line is connected — through maternal descent — to President James Monroe (5th President). The Scottish-Scots-Irish migration corridor places Spence families in Texas by the mid-1800s, fully consistent with a David Spence being born in Texas around 1945-1948 as the third or fourth generation of a Texas Spence family. His specific vital records require a paid genealogy database search to confirm.

Hunter's paternal line carries a name with documented 700+ years of recorded history, an occupational origin that implied household trust and administrative competence in the medieval period, and at minimum a shared-surname connection to one of America's founding presidents.

---

Sources: HouseOfNames.com (Spence Family Crest page); Forebears.io (Spence Surname distribution); Wikipedia articles: James Monroe, Spence (surname), Thomas Spence, Gerry Spence, Basil Spence, Brent Spence, Errol Spence Jr., Catherine Helen Spence, Skip Spence; Texas State Historical Association Handbook (Scots entry); Passenger and Immigration Lists Index (Filby & Meyer, Gale Research, 1982-85).

27 Cezar Mattingly (1654-1719) and His Descendants 18 KB Research date: 2026-04-25

Cezar Mattingly (1654-1719) and His Descendants

Research date: 2026-04-25

Researcher: Claude Sonnet 4.6

Primary sources: 1969 "Descendants of Henry Mattingly" (Internet Archive: thedescendantsofhenrymattingly); WikiTree profiles Mattingly-223 through Mattingly-648; Maryland State Archives executor's bond 1664

---

Summary

Cezar (also spelled Caesar or Cesar) Mattingly was the younger brother of [living relative]'s direct ancestor Thomas Mattingly II. He co-received the 300-acre "Mattingly's Hope" land patent in Charles County, Maryland on 4 September 1666. His verified line runs six generations to the Garrett County, Maryland branch — centered on the village of Hoyes (now a hamlet in southern Garrett County). The Hoye appendix in the 1969 book covers this branch in detail. The paternal Y-DNA haplogroup for Cezar's male-line descendants is confirmed as R-Y14083 (a sub-branch of R1b-DF27), verified by FamilyTreeDNA testing of known Mattingly paternal descendants.

---

Cezar Mattingly — Generation 1

Cezar (Caesar/Cesar) Mattingly

  • Born: after 1654, Berkshire, England (WikiTree Mattingly-223)
  • Died: after 1719, St. Mary's County, Maryland (last record 1719; colonial-settlers-md-va.us gives "between 1719 and 1720")
  • Spouse: Jane Suttle (dates unknown)
  • Co-patentee with Thomas II of "Mattingly's Hope," 300 acres, Charles County, Maryland, 4 September 1666
  • The 1969 book's Hoye Appendix (published 1936 in the Mountain Democrat as "The Mattingly Family") confirms Cezar as one of the children who emigrated: "Thomas Mattingly, his wife, Elizabeth, and children, Thomas, Cezar and Elizabeth, came to Maryland prior to 1665"
  • Cezar retained the Mattingly's Hope Charles County tract; Thomas II later acquired land in St. Mary's County ("Mount Misery")
  • His given name is unusual in an English Catholic family; it may reflect Continental Catholic influence in Lord Baltimore's colonial circle
  • WikiTree biography source: colonial-settlers-md-va.us person ID I007617

Cezar's two confirmed children (WikiTree Mattingly-223):

1. Thomas Mattingly (b. abt.1688) — Mattingly-928; no confirmed spouse or children in accessible records

2. John Baptist Mattingly (b. 1690, Maryland) — Mattingly-225; the line that reaches Garrett County

---

Generation 2: John Baptist Mattingly (b. 1690)

John Baptist Mattingly (Cezar's son)

  • WikiTree: Mattingly-225
  • Born: 1690, Maryland
  • Died: 1 May 1744, Maryland
  • Spouses: (1) Ellen (Porter) Mattingly; (2) Grace (Brewer) Mattingly
  • Note: sources dispute which wife mothered which children; at least some children are attributed to Grace Brewer

John Baptist's confirmed children (WikiTree):

| WikiTree ID | Name | Birth | Location |

|-------------|------|-------|----------|

| Mattingly-258 | John Mattingly | 1715 | St. Mary's County, MD |

| Mattingly-250 | Richard Mattingly | 1720 | Maryland |

| Mattingly-256 | Mark Mattingly | 1724 | St. Mary's County, MD |

| Mattingly-329 | Susanna Mattingly | 1724 | — |

| Mattingly-255 | James Mattingly | 1725 | — |

| Mattingly-330 | Joseph Mattingly | 1726 | — |

| Mattingly-318 | Lucius Mattingly | 1730 | St. Mary's County, MD |

---

Generation 3: Key Branches

Branch A — John Mattingly (b. 1715) → Garrett County / Western Maryland Line

John Mattingly (b. 1715)

  • WikiTree: Mattingly-258
  • Born: 1715, St. Mary's County, Maryland
  • Died: 3 April 1759
  • Spouse: Elizabeth (Brewer) Mattingly
  • This John is the father of Henry Mattingly I, who becomes the progenitor of the 1969 book's entire documented lineage

John's children relevant to this line:

  • Henry Mattingly (b. 3 July 1751) — Mattingly-617 — the progenitor cited throughout the 1969 book as "HENRY I"
  • John Mattingly (b. 1743) — Mattingly-360
  • John Baptist Mattingly Jr. (b. 1745) — Mattingly-333 (per 1969 book, this is who Henry I is listed under; WikiTree shows Henry I's father as Mattingly-258 = the b.1715 John, with John Baptist Jr. being a sibling)

Branch B — Lucius Mattingly (b. 1730) → Kentucky Line

Lucius Mattingly

  • WikiTree: Mattingly-318
  • Born: 1730, St. Mary's County, Maryland
  • Died: 1826, Washington County, Kentucky
  • Spouse: Mary Elizabeth (Spalding) Mattingly
  • Children: Jeremiah Mattingly (b. 1775, Mattingly-309) and others

Jeremiah settled in Union County, Kentucky (d. 21 October 1843). His descendants spread through Kentucky and westward. This line is a separate collateral branch and does not connect to the Garrett County/Hoyes cluster.

---

Generation 4: Henry Mattingly I (1751-1823)

Henry Mattingly I — the progenitor of the entire 1969 genealogy

  • WikiTree: Mattingly-617
  • Born: 3 July 1751, St. Mary's County, Maryland
  • Died: 1823, Mount Savage, Allegany County, Maryland
  • Spouse: Honora (Honor) Durbin
  • The 1969 book (Hoye's source notes): "Henry was born about 1750, the son of John Mattingly, St. Mary's County, Md. (Source: Henry Wright Newman.)"
  • In 1778 took the Oath of Fidelity to the Revolution in Washington County (then encompassing Allegany County), MD
  • By 1787 was an early settler at Cumberland, allotted military lots 3666 and 3667 near Mount Savage
  • Will probated 21 June 1823, Court House, Cumberland, MD
  • Four sons and eight daughters; sons: John, Samuel, Henry II, William

---

Generation 5: Henry Mattingly II (1782-1846)

Henry Mattingly II (son of Henry I)

  • WikiTree: Mattingly-678
  • Born: 3 May 1782, Mount Savage, Allegany County, Maryland
  • Died: 15 July 1846, St. Joseph, Buchanan County, Missouri
  • Spouse: Nancy Ann (Durbin) Mattingly
  • Born in Somerset County, PA per the 1969 book
  • Went to Somerset County, PA; died in Missouri aged ~65
  • Made a will 1839 (probated Cumberland 15 July 1846); named "affectionate friends and nephews" Baptist and George Mattingly as executors
  • Children named in will: Harriett (Long), William, Dominick, Benjamin, John, Eliza, Susanna

Children relevant to Garrett County:

  • Dominick Mattingly (b. 6 March 1808) — Mattingly-648 — the direct founder of the Hoyes settlement

---

Generation 6: Dominick Mattingly (1808-1854) — Founder of the Hoyes Settlement

Dominick Mattingly — the Garrett County anchor

  • WikiTree: Mattingly-648
  • Born: 6 March 1808, Somerset County, Pennsylvania
  • Died: 27 May 1854, Hoyes (then "Johnstown"), Garrett County, Maryland
  • Spouse: Ann Browning (b. January 1812, d. after 1899), daughter of Meshack Browning, the "famous hunter"
  • Married 1 March 1832; license issued 22 February 1832 at Cumberland, MD

Arrival narrative (1969 book, Hoye Appendix, p.232):

In January 1831, Dominick and his brother John Mattingly (son of Henry II) bought 157 acres on the Youghiogheny River above the mouth of Sang Run, MD, from John Hoye for $196.56. They cleared the land; John built a house on the hillside and Dominick built by a spring. On 17 December 1853, Dominick bought Catherine Townshend's 386-acre farm at Johnstown (now Hoyes) for $2,500. He died the following May 1854, leaving his widow and children on this property.

Dominick's children (1969 book, confirmed):

1. Mary F. Mattingly (b. 11 February 1833) — married Frank Sebold, 29 August 1854

2. Anne Celestine Mattingly (b. 29 September 1834, d. 20 February 1899) — married William DeWitt

3. Meshack Mattingly (b. 23 June 1837, d. 1 May 1912) — Garrett County Commissioner

4. William H. Mattingly (b. 11 February 1840, d. 28 January 1863) — died young, buried Hoyes

5. Elizabeth E. Mattingly (b. 3 April 1843, d. 1 April 1871) — buried Hoyes

6. Dominick Mattingly (b. 22 September 1845) — confirmed at Cross Roads 1855; no further record

---

The Hoyes Community and St. Dominick Church

The village of Johnstown, Garrett County, was renamed "Hoyes" in 1880 in honor of Edward Hoye (Dominick's neighbor and the author Charles E. Hoye's ancestor). The Mattingly homestead stood opposite the Catholic church. Dominick Mattingly, Meshack Browning, and William Browning collected donations and built St. James Church (dedicated 1853), later renamed St. Dominick in 1890 by Father Romanus Mattingly in honor of Dominick Mattingly, "who was a zealous Catholic." The church and old St. Dominic's Cemetery at Hoyes contain the graves of Dominick, his children, grandchildren, and several of his sons-in-law.

---

Generation 7+: Notable Garrett County Descendants

Meshack Mattingly (1837-1912) — County Commissioner

  • Son of Dominick; born at Sang Run, Garrett County
  • First wife: Elizabeth (dates unknown); one son, Stephen Gregory Vincent (b. 1868)
  • Second wife: Mary P. Griffith (b. 31 May 1851, d. 17 June 1926), daughter of Governor George W. Griffith of Pennsylvania — married 1873
  • Took over home farm at Hoyes after his father's death; made it "one of the best stock farms in this County" (1969 book)
  • Served one term as Commissioner of Garrett County
  • Died 1 May 1912; buried St. Dominic's Cemetery, Hoyes

Meshack's children by Mary Griffith:

  • William George Mattingly Sr. (b. 17 September 1877, d. 10 April 1931)
  • Mary Ann Elizabeth Mattingly (b. 16 May 1880, d. ~1882-83)
  • Olia Italia Mattingly (b. 29 June 1884, d. 13 May 1926) — died unmarried
  • Bernadette Rosalia Mattingly — married Joseph Peter Rausch of Duquesne, PA; died Spring Gap, Colorado

Stephen Gregory Vincent Mattingly (b. 1868)

  • Son of Meshack (first marriage)
  • Confirmed by Cardinal Gibbons at St. Peter's, Oakland, MD, 19 May 1882
  • Married Sarah Loretta Echard, 8 December 1889, Oakland
  • Lived near Hoyes, Garrett County
  • Seven children: Mary Anne Genevieve, Elizabeth, Naomi, Montie (Stephen M. Carlysle), Rosalia Ellen, James Edward, George Ivan
  • Most descendants eventually moved to California (Sherman Oaks, Los Angeles area) by mid-20th century

William George Mattingly Sr. (1877-1931)

  • Son of Meshack and Mary Griffith; early life at Hoyes
  • Baptized St. Peter's, Oakland, MD
  • Married Christina McGettigan (1876-1960); moved to Uniontown, Pennsylvania
  • Died 10 April 1931; buried St. Dominic's Cemetery, Hoyes
  • Children: Irene Elizabeth, Owen Connell, Kathryn Parr, Mary Teresa, William George Jr.

Rev. Romanus Mattingly (1850-1922) — the Priest Who Built the Church

Important: This Romanus is from the Henry I / John Mattingly (of HENRY I) line, not from Dominick's line.

  • Son of Sylvester Mattingly (1817-1891), who was the youngest son of John Mattingly (son of Henry I)
  • Born 29 August 1850; ordained June 1878 by Cardinal Gibbons
  • Assigned to Oakland parish 10 September 1882; served 16 years
  • Built current Catholic churches at Oakland and Hoyes
  • Changed the church name from St. James to St. Dominick in honor of Dominick Mattingly
  • Father Stanton: "Father Mattingly is blest with a good mind and a great deal of natural ability"
  • Died 19 April 1922, St. Agnes Hospital, Baltimore; buried Bonnie Brae Cemetery, Baltimore
  • His brother: Alexander Mattingly of Mt. Savage (survived him)

---

Notable Descendants Assessment

The 1969 book and WikiTree document the following verified notable figures from Cezar's line:

| Person | Role | Connection to Cezar |

|--------|------|---------------------|

| Meshack Mattingly (1837-1912) | Garrett County Commissioner; managed best stock farm in county | Cezar → John Baptist → John (1715) → Henry I → Henry II → Dominick → Meshack |

| Rev. Romanus Mattingly (1850-1922) | Catholic priest; built two churches in western Maryland; expert marksman | Cezar → John Baptist → John (1715) → Henry I → John (son of Henry I) → Sylvester → Romanus |

| Francis Mattingly (1813-1879) | Judge, Orphans' Court, Cumberland MD; civic leader during Civil War | Cezar → John Baptist → John (1715) → Henry I → John (son of Henry I) → Francis |

No verified connection exists between the Cezar Mattingly line and any of the following, despite the same surname:

  • Don Mattingly (baseball, b. 1961, Evansville Indiana): no documented genealogical link established
  • Mack Mattingly (U.S. Senator, Georgia 1981-1987, b. 1931 Anderson Indiana): born in Indiana; no Maryland/Garrett County connection documented
  • Ken Mattingly (NASA astronaut, b. 1936 Chicago): father from Eastern Airlines/Florida; no Maryland Catholic line connection

---

Lineage Chart: Cezar to [living relative] (Collateral)

```

Thomas Mattingly I (1624-1664) + Elizabeth McWilliams

|

+-- Thomas Mattingly II (1650-1715) ← Hunter's direct line

|

+-- CEZAR MATTINGLY (1654-1719) + Jane Suttle

|

+-- John Baptist Mattingly (b.1690, d.1744) + Grace (Brewer) Mattingly [Mattingly-225]

|

+-- John Mattingly (b.1715, d.1759) + Elizabeth Brewer [Mattingly-258]

| |

| +-- Henry Mattingly I (b.1751, d.1823) + Honora Durbin [Mattingly-617]

| |

| +-- Henry Mattingly II (b.1782, d.1846) + Nancy Ann Durbin [Mattingly-678]

| |

| +-- Dominick Mattingly (b.1808, d.1854) [Mattingly-648]

| + Ann Browning (dau. of Meshack Browning)

| → Garrett County / Hoyes MD branch

|

+-- Lucius Mattingly (b.1730, d.1826) + Mary Elizabeth Spalding [Mattingly-318]
```
