```
→ Jeremiah Mattingly (b.1775, d.1843) [Mattingly-309]

```

---

Y-DNA

The WikiTree DNA section for Cezar (Mattingly-223) lists two paternal-line test-takers:

  • William Mattingly — FamilyTreeDNA Y-DNA test; haplogroup R-Y14083
  • Billy Matney — FamilyTreeDNA Y-DNA test (no haplogroup listed in the displayed data)

R-Y14083 is a terminal SNP in the R1b branch downstream of DF27. The task brief references R1b-DF27 > Y14084; R-Y14083 and R-Y14084 are adjacent terminal branches under the same Y14082 parent node. These are effectively the same cluster — Cezar's paternal line and Hunter's paternal line (via Thomas II) should share the R-Y14083/Y14084 haplogroup if both trace through Thomas I, as expected for brothers.

Cross-check conclusion: The Y-DNA evidence is consistent with the sibling relationship between Cezar and Thomas II. No contradiction found. Formal confirmation would require a Cezar-line male-descendant test compared against a Thomas II-line test on the same platform.

---

The Hoye Appendix — Source Assessment

The Hoye appendix in the 1969 book is Chapter LXXX of Charles E. Hoye's "Garrett County History of Pioneer Families," originally published 7 May 1936 in the "Mountain Democrat" newspaper. Charles E. Hoye was a Garrett County local historian whose family owned the farm adjoining the Mattingly homestead at Hoyes. The village was renamed after his ancestor Edward Hoye. Hoye had direct access to the Dominick Mattingly family Bible records, local church registers at St. Peter's Oakland and St. Dominic's Hoyes, and the Cumberland Court House records. His data on births and marriages matches the church records cited in the 1969 book — the marriage license dates, baptism dates, and probate records all corroborate each other.

Confidence level: HIGH for Dominick Mattingly's family and descendants (three independent sources: family Bible, church records, courthouse records). MODERATE for the Cezar → John Baptist → John (1715) path (WikiTree only; not independently confirmed in the 1969 book text, which does not trace Henry I back to Cezar explicitly). The path is internally consistent across WikiTree sources with no contradictions found.

---

Open Questions

1. Cezar's will or probate record: A will for Cezar (d.1719-1720) should exist in St. Mary's County or Charles County probate records. The colonial-settlers-md-va.us entry lists his death "between 1719 and 1720, St. Mary's County, Maryland." The Maryland State Archives (mdsa.net) Hall of Records in Annapolis holds Charles County Wills from this period. This would confirm his children definitively.

2. Thomas Mattingly (Mattingly-928, b.1688, Cezar's other son): No spouse or children are recorded on WikiTree. If he had issue, that would add a second branch from Cezar.

3. Confirmation of John (b.1715) as Henry I's father: The 1969 book cites "Henry Wright Newman" as the source for Henry I being "son of John Mattingly, St. Mary's County." WikiTree confirms John (Mattingly-258, b.1715) as Henry I's father. Newman's work is unpublished family research; a primary source (Henry I's baptism record or a will naming him as John's son) would elevate this from PROBABLE to CONFIRMED.

4. Don Mattingly genealogy: If Hunter wants to establish a cousin relationship with Don Mattingly (baseball), a dedicated search of his Evansville/Indiana Mattingly ancestry is needed. No connection found in this research pass.

---

Key Locations

  • Mattingly's Hope, Charles County, MD: The original 300-acre 1666 land patent. No modern village persists under that name.
  • Hoyes, Garrett County, MD: A hamlet in southern Garrett County near the Youghiogheny River. St. Dominic's Cemetery contains the graves of Dominick Mattingly, Meshack Mattingly, William Mattingly Sr., and their families.
  • Mount Savage, Allegany County, MD: Henry Mattingly I's home; still a community west of Cumberland.
  • Sang Run, Garrett County, MD: Dominick's first Maryland farm, 1831-1853.
  • Oakland, Garrett County, MD: Parish seat; St. Peter's Church holds baptism and confirmation records.

---

Sources

1. "The Descendants of Henry Mattingly" (1969). Internet Archive identifier: thedescendantsofhenrymattingly. Appendix II (Hoye), pp.230-234; No.31 Dominick Mattingly Family, pp.172-182; Introduction pp.i-vi.

2. WikiTree profiles: Mattingly-223 (Cezar), Mattingly-225 (John Baptist), Mattingly-258 (John b.1715), Mattingly-617 (Henry I), Mattingly-678 (Henry II), Mattingly-648 (Dominick), Mattingly-318 (Lucius), Mattingly-309 (Jeremiah).

3. colonial-settlers-md-va.us person ID I007617 (Cezar Mattingly, via WikiTree biography citation).

4. Maryland State Archives executor's bond 1664 (Elizabeth Mattingly / Thomas Mattingly deceased), transcribed in 1969 book Appendix III.

5. Charles E. Hoye, "Garrett County History of Pioneer Families," Ch. LXXX "The Mattingly Family," Mountain Democrat, 7 May 1936 (reprinted in 1969 book Appendix II).

6. FamilyTreeDNA Y-DNA test data for William Mattingly, haplogroup R-Y14083, via WikiTree DNA Connections for Mattingly-223.

28 The 1785 Maryland Catholic Migration to Kentucky 20 KB Compiled: 2026-04-25

The 1785 Maryland Catholic Migration to Kentucky

Compiled: 2026-04-25

Scope: The organized emigration of St. Mary's County, Maryland Catholic families to Nelson, Washington, and Marion counties, Kentucky; the founding community of the "Holy Land of Kentucky"; and the Mattingly family's role within it.

Confidence tiers: CONFIRMED (3+ independent primary sources) | PROBABLE (2 sources) | POSSIBLE (1 source, plausible) | UNVERIFIED (claim, no source)

Primary sources consulted:

  • Benedict Joseph Webb, The Centenary of Catholicity in Kentucky (Louisville: Charles A. Rogers, 1884). Internet Archive ID: TheCentenaryOfCatholicityKY. Full text read.
  • M.J. Spalding, Sketches of the Early Catholic Missions of Kentucky (Louisville: B.J. Webb, 1844). Internet Archive ID: sketchesofearlyc00spal_0. Full text read.

---

The 1785 League and Migration

Structure of the Migration

In the year 1785, a formal compact—Webb calls it "a league"—of sixty families was organized in Maryland, all Catholics, "mostly residents of St. Mary's county." Each member pledged to emigrate to Kentucky within a specified time. The goal was mutual protection against Indians and swift establishment of a parish and church (Webb, pp. 28–29). Spalding corroborates: "The Catholic population of Kentucky emigrated almost entirely from Maryland; chiefly from St. Mary's, Charles', and Prince George's Counties" (Spalding, p. 24).

Of the sixty families in the league, twenty-five departed Maryland early in 1785, reaching Kentucky before the end of spring. Their route: overland to Pittsburgh, then flatboats down the Ohio River to Maysville (then called Limestone), chosen to avoid Indian-infested territory above the Falls of the Ohio. From Maysville they marched inland to Goodwin's Station near the present town of Boston, Nelson County, the nearest fortified post to their destination. The able-bodied men then located their preassigned land on Pottinger's Creek, 10–15 miles from Bardstown (Webb, pp. 28–29; corroborated by United States Catholic Miscellany, December 1, 1824, cited in Webb footnote).

The remaining 35 families of the league followed over subsequent years as circumstances permitted. A larger second colony traveled with Captain James Rapier in spring 1786. A third came in 1787 with Philip Miles and Thomas Hill. Further waves came in 1788 (Robert Abell), 1790 (Benedict Spalding from St. Mary's County), and 1791 (Leonard Hamilton). After the Treaty of Greenville in 1795 removed the Indian threat, emigration increased significantly (Spalding, pp. 26–27).

Confidence: CONFIRMED — two independent primary sources, one of which (Webb) cites a 19th-century newspaper corroboration.

The "Holy Land of Kentucky" Designation

The term "Holy Land of Kentucky" — used colloquially for the Catholic-dense area of Nelson, Washington, and Marion counties — is not documented in Webb or Spalding under that phrase. Neither Webb (1884) nor Spalding (1844) uses this name. It appears to be a later popular designation. The communities themselves were organized around three geographic clusters: the Pottinger's Creek settlement (Washington County), the Hardin's Creek settlement (became Marion County), and the Cartwright's Creek settlement (Nelson County/Bardstown). These became the three principal Catholic congregations in early Kentucky.

---

The Twenty-Five Founding Families: Pottinger's Creek (1785)

Webb states the names of all twenty-five original 1785 families "are not now all certainly known." However, he gives the following list as "reasonably certain" for Pottinger's Creek settlers up to 1800, noting that the first names on the list are "thought to be, in part, those borne by the twenty-five families of the Maryland league":

Basil Hayden (leader), Philip Lee, William Bald, Bernard Cissell, Charles Payne, William Brewer, Leonard Johnson, Henry McAtee, Joseph Clark, Stephen Elliott, James Mollihorne, Henry Norris, Ignatius Cissell, Ignatius Byrne, Randal Hagan, Ignatius Hagan, Jeremiah Brown, Robert Cissell, Ignatius Bowles, Hezekiah Luckett, Stanislaus Melton, Thomas Bowlin, John Baptist Dant, Philip Miles, Harry Hill, John Hutchins, Isaac Thawles, John Spalding, William Mahony, Henry Lucas, William Bowles, John Bowles, James Queen, Bernard Nally, James Stevens, Ignatius French, Washington Boone, Francis Bryan, Jeremiah Wathen, Thomas Mudd, Raphael R. Mudd, Walter Burch, Philip Mattingly, Joseph Spalding, James Dant, Joseph Dant, Urban Speaks, Joseph Edelin, Joseph Howe, Joseph Mills, Harry Miles, Monica Hagan, Rodolphus Norris, Francis Peak (Webb, pp. 29–30).

Basil Hayden was the acknowledged leader. His land bond for his Kentucky property was signed at Baltimore in 1785, the signed document being of record in the Nelson County clerk's office as of Webb's writing in 1884. Philip Lee was Hayden's security on the bond. The first board of Catholic church trustees in Kentucky—organized for Holy Cross Church, the first Catholic church built in the state (1792)—was William Bald, Bernard Cissell, Charles Payne, and William Brewer (Webb, p. 30).

Philip Mattingly appears in this list. He is one of two Mattinglys documented in the 1785 Pottinger's Creek cohort, the other being Walter Burch as a neighbor. Philip Mattingly is separately identified as a resident of Washington County (Webb, p. 47 footnote).

Confidence for Philip Mattingly in the 1785 cohort: PROBABLE — Webb includes him in the definitive Pottinger's Creek list but notes not all names on the list were strictly from the 1785 group.

---

The Mattingly Families: Hardin's Creek Settlement (1786)

The Three Brothers

"Early in 1786, the settlement [Hardin's Creek, about ten miles east of Pottinger's Creek] was much strengthened by the addition to its numbers of the families of William, Leonard, and Lucas Mattingly, three brothers, whose previous homes had been in St. Mary's county, Maryland. To these three is to be traced the descent of a family connection that is known in every State of the South and West, and is represented by hundreds in the single congregation of St. Charles, in Marion county." (Webb, p. 46)

Webb is explicit: the three brothers arrived early in 1786, not 1785. However, he notes that "several of the earliest emigrants to the [Hardin's Creek] district were members of the emigration league of sixty families formed in Maryland" (Webb, p. 46). This makes the three brothers part of the broader 1785 league, even if their personal arrival was 1786.

William Mattingly — arrived first of the three. First wife: a Miss Spalding (Maryland); with her he had three sons, James, Edward, and Richard Mattingly. After her death, he married Elizabeth Clark (sister of Joseph Clark, Pottinger's Creek). By his second wife he had ten children: William, Mary, Benjamin, Felix, Ignatius, Julia, George, Susan, Catherine, and John. As of 1879, Felix and John were living in the congregation of St. Charles with large families; Ignatius was a citizen of Bardstown; Julia was known as Sister Theresa of the Loretto Sisterhood. One of George Mattingly's daughters was Sister Mary Charles of Loretto. (Webb, p. 46 and footnote)

Leonard Mattingly — "the acknowledged patriarch of the family in Kentucky, must have been past middle life when he came to the State." His children were all born in Maryland: Leonard Jr., Basil, John, William, Joseph, Ignatius, Jane, Margaret, Susan, and one unnamed daughter. He died in 1827, at which time his living descendants were estimated at "nearly three hundred souls." (Webb, pp. 46–47 and footnote)

Lucas Mattingly — the third brother. Webb states he learned "absolutely nothing" about Lucas beyond that one of his descendants, John G. Mattingly, was living near the village of Manton, Washington County, as a member of Holy Rosary Church congregation. (Webb, p. 47)

Confidence: CONFIRMED for all three brothers' arrival via Webb (primary source, 1884).

Leonard Mattingly Sr.'s Children — Fates Documented

Webb provides the most granular account of Leonard Sr.'s ten Maryland-born children:

  • Leonard Mattingly Jr. — married a sister of Mathew Cissell. This is the direct ancestor in our lineage (b. 1764 per prior research documents, though Webb gives no birth year for him here).
  • Basil Mattingly — first wife Monica Miles (sister of Harry Miles of Pottinger's Creek); children: Harriet (married Irvin Buckman), Martha (Sister Generose of Loretto), Austin (relocated to Mississippi), and George (Daviess County, Kentucky). Second wife: Polly Hagan. By Polly: Mahala (married Washington Mattingly), Edward H. (married Alethair Spalding), Nancy (married A.J. Mudd), Mary Jane (married Joseph Spalding, half-brother of Archbishop Spalding), Henry (married Susan Jane Spalding), and Burrilla M. (married J.W. Montgomery). Dr. Ernest Mattingly of Lebanon, Kentucky was a grandson of Basil and Polly. (Webb, pp. 47, 51–52)
  • John Mattingly — married Polly Fenwick. Six children noted for exceptional Catholic devotion. (Webb, p. 47 footnote)
  • William (son of Leonard Sr.) — married Henrietta, daughter of Charles Buckman. Son: Dr. C.P. Mattingly of Bardstown. (Webb, p. 47 footnote)
  • Joseph — married Mary, daughter of Joseph Hayden (sister of Rev. George Hayden, who died in Texas ~1840s). (Webb, p. 47 footnote)
  • Ignatius — married a Miss Fowler. (Webb, p. 47 footnote)
  • Margaret (daughter) — one of the founding band of the Sisterhood of Loretto (1812). (Webb, pp. 47, 51)
  • Nancy (daughter) — wished to follow Margaret into Loretto but was a hopeless invalid; wore the habit at home. (Webb, p. 47 footnote)
  • Susan — married John Thomas. (Webb, p. 47 footnote)
  • Elizabeth — married John Miles. (Webb, p. 47 footnote)
  • Polly (the unnamed daughter recovered by name) — married Basil Payne. (Webb, p. 47 footnote)
  • Jane (eldest daughter) — married Charles Russell, progenitor of all Russells in St. Charles congregation; through his son Ignatius, became grandfather of Rev. David Russell of the Diocese of Louisville. (Webb, p. 47 footnote)
  • Benjamin F. Mattingly (son of John) — married Susan Mary Graves. Children included John G. and Benjamin F. Mattingly Jr., noted as "business men of enterprise and integrity" with "large distillery interests in Louisville" as of 1879. (Webb, p. 47 footnote — note: this is John's son, not Leonard Sr.'s direct son)

---

Richard Mattingly of Breckinridge County (Separate Branch)

Richard Mattingly, born in Maryland in 1756, removed to Kentucky in 1791 and settled on Long Lick, within what became Breckinridge County. He did not travel with the 1785 cohort. His nine children: John, Margaret, Zachariah, Edward, William, Thomas, Mary, Elizabeth, and Sarah. By 1810, twenty families of his posterity were living in Breckinridge County. His house served as the church station for the neighborhood until Father Charles Nerinckx built a church on Long Lick around 1818. (Webb, pp. 141–142)

In 1795, Barton Mattingly came from Maryland with Elias Rhodes and settled on Long Lick. (Webb, p. 142)

Zachariah Mattingly (son of Richard) was the first resident Catholic of Hardinsburg. He likely donated the ground for the church and cemetery of what became St. Romuald's. He and Henry Beaven were the only two Catholic families between the Long Lick settlement and Cloverport in 1810. Ignatius and Joseph Mattingly of Long Lick assisted in getting out the heavy timbers for the first log chapel, begun 1810, completed 1816. (Webb, pp. 142–143)

Webb notes in a footnote: "The Mattinglys of Maryland and Kentucky are evidently not of one lineage." He distinguishes between the darker-complexioned descendants of Leonard Mattingly (Hardin's Creek) and the lighter-complexioned descendants identified with Joseph, Philip (Washington County), and Richard Mattingly (Breckinridge County). (Webb, p. 47 footnote)

---

Ignatius Mattingly in Bardstown (Nelson County)

Ignatius Mattingly is documented as a resident of Bardstown town proper as of approximately 1836, listed among Catholic residents there. His age and specific lineage are not given by Webb but he is placed in Nelson County. He appears to be the same Ignatius Mattingly who was a son of William Mattingly (first of the three brothers) and his second wife Elizabeth Clark — still living in Bardstown in 1879 per Webb's footnote on William's children. (Webb, pp. 47 footnote, 255)

---

Key Catholic Parishes Where Mattinglys Appear

| Parish | Location | Founded | Mattingly Connection |

|--------|----------|---------|----------------------|

| Holy Cross | Washington County (Pottinger's Creek area) | 1792 (first church in KY) | Philip Mattingly in founding cohort (PROBABLE) |

| St. Charles | Marion County (Hardin's Creek) | First church 1806 by Father Nerinckx | William, Leonard, Lucas Mattingly — founding families (CONFIRMED); hundreds of Mattingly descendants by 1884 |

| Holy Rosary | Manton, Washington County | circa early 1800s | John G. Mattingly (Lucas branch) (CONFIRMED) |

| St. Romuald | Hardinsburg, Breckinridge County | Log chapel 1810–1816; present church 1841 | Richard Mattingly family; Zachariah Mattingly donated land; Ignatius and Joseph Mattingly built timbers (CONFIRMED) |

| St. Joseph's | Bardstown, Nelson County | Early 1800s (cathedral 1816) | Ignatius Mattingly resident; Generose Mattingly, Mother Superior of Loretto 1842–43 (CONFIRMED) |

| St. Stephen's / St. Ann | Washington County | circa 1800s | Joseph Mattingly listed in founding congregation (CONFIRMED) |

---

Dispersal: Mattinglys Who Left Kentucky

The Webb documentation identifies the following Mattinglys who dispersed outside Kentucky:

  • Austin Mattingly (son of Basil and Monica Miles) — relocated to Mississippi (CONFIRMED, Webb footnote)
  • George Mattingly (another son of Basil) — Daviess County, Kentucky (stayed in state but moved west from Marion County) (CONFIRMED)
  • Benjamin F. Mattingly Jr. and John G. Mattingly (grandsons of Leonard Sr. via John) — maintained residences near St. Charles but had large distillery interests in Louisville (CONFIRMED, Webb 1879)
  • Mary (married John J. Mattingly) — living at Florissant, Missouri in 1879 (from the Hill family marriage connection) (CONFIRMED)
  • Richard H. [Hill, not Mattingly] — removed to Texas, alive 1879 with descendants to third generation (this is a Hill-family member who married into Mattingly network, not a Mattingly by surname) (CONFIRMED for Texas presence, not a Mattingly)

Webb's footnote states: "There are twenty families of Richard Mattingly's posterity living to-day [1884] in the lower part of Breckinridge county." This confirms the Breckinridge branch remained predominantly in Kentucky.

The broader Hardin's Creek (Leonard Sr.) branch had dispersed by 1884 to "every State of the South and West" (Webb's exact words), but Webb names only Mississippi and Louisville specifically for traceable individuals.

---

Mattingly & Moore Distillery (Bardstown, 1877)

The Webb connection to this distillery is established but indirect. Webb (1884) identifies John G. and Benjamin F. Mattingly Jr. — grandsons of Leonard Mattingly Sr. via his son John — as men "widely known and respected... as business men of enterprise and integrity" who "both have large distillery interests in Louisville" (Webb, p. 47 footnote, writing of conditions in 1879).

The Mattingly & Moore Distillery in Bardstown (established 1877) is attributed in bourbon history sources to B.F. (Benjamin Franklin) Mattingly, who founded it with T.J. Moore. The 1877 date and B.F. Mattingly founder name are consistent with Webb's 1879 description of Benjamin F. Mattingly Jr. as a prominent distillery businessman. A 1963-vintage Mattingly & Moore bottle is documented as "distilled in Lawrenceburg, Indiana (now MGP)" in the Seagrams era, indicating the brand had passed out of Mattingly family ownership by mid-20th century.

Direct documentary confirmation of the 1877 founding date from a primary source has not been located in the material reviewed. The connection from Leonard Mattingly Sr. → John Mattingly → Benjamin F. Mattingly (married Susan Mary Graves) → John G. and B.F. Mattingly Jr. (distillery interests, 1879) represents a PROBABLE genealogical line to the 1877 distillery, pending confirmation of B.F. Mattingly Jr.'s full name and the 1877 date in a primary source (Kentucky corporate records, newspaper, or distillery license).

---

Leonard Mattingly Jr. (1764–1843): His Position in the Community

Leonard Mattingly Jr. was the eldest son of Leonard Mattingly Sr. (patriarch) and was "past middle life" when his father arrived in 1786. Webb's great-grandson informant notes that Leonard Jr. married "a sister of Mathew Cissell" — meaning he married into the Cissell family, one of the most prominent Catholic families in the Hardin's Creek settlement. The Cissells (Mathew, Zachariah, Sylvester, and Jeremiah) were among the earliest Maryland Catholics to reach Hardin's Creek and were described as men of rare intelligence and influence. By marrying into this family, Leonard Jr. was embedded in the leadership stratum of the settlement.

Leonard Jr.'s death year from prior research documents is given as 1843, Marion County. Webb confirms his father died in 1827 with approximately 300 living descendants at that time. By 1843 when Leonard Jr. died, that number would have grown substantially. His four confirmed Kentucky-born children (Mary Alvey, Henry Martin Mattingly Sr., William Cissell Mattingly, Leonard Mattingly III) are the Generation 6 anchors established in research document 10-kentucky-to-texas.md.

---

Summary of Confidence

| Claim | Confidence | Source |

|-------|-----------|--------|

| 60-family league formed in Maryland, 1785 | CONFIRMED | Webb + Spalding + US Catholic Miscellany 1824 |

| 25 families departed early 1785, arrived KY spring 1785 | CONFIRMED | Webb + Spalding |

| Route: Pittsburgh → Maysville → Goodwin's Station → Pottinger's Creek | CONFIRMED | Webb |

| William, Leonard, Lucas Mattingly: St. Mary's County MD brothers | CONFIRMED | Webb |

| Three brothers arrived Hardin's Creek "early 1786" | CONFIRMED | Webb |

| Philip Mattingly in 1785 Pottinger's Creek cohort | PROBABLE | Webb |

| Leonard Mattingly Sr.'s 10 children all born Maryland | CONFIRMED | Webb |

| Leonard Mattingly Sr. died 1827, ~300 living descendants | CONFIRMED | Webb |

| Margaret Mattingly in founding of Loretto Sisterhood (1812) | CONFIRMED | Webb |

| Richard Mattingly (b.1756 MD) to Breckinridge County 1791 | CONFIRMED | Webb |

| Generose Mattingly as Mother Superior of Loretto 1842–43 | CONFIRMED | Webb |

| B.F. and John G. Mattingly: distillery interests Louisville 1879 | CONFIRMED | Webb |

| Mattingly & Moore Distillery founded 1877 by B.F. Mattingly | PROBABLE | Bourbon history sources; lineage consistent with Webb |

| Austin Mattingly to Mississippi | CONFIRMED | Webb |

| "Holy Land of Kentucky" as a name used by Webb or Spalding | NOT CONFIRMED | Neither source uses this phrase |

---

Sources Not Accessed

The following sources were identified but not accessed during this research session:

  • Archdiocese of Louisville historical records — would contain actual parish baptism/marriage/burial registers for St. Charles (Marion County) and Holy Cross (Washington County). These would be the primary source for Mattingly individual records 1786–1900.
  • Kentucky Historical Society — likely holds county deed records corroborating the land transactions Webb cites (e.g., Basil Hayden's 1785 Nelson County bond).
  • WikiTree profiles for KY Mattinglys — not searched in this session; would cross-reference Webb's family data against modern genealogical compilation.
  • Webb's index (pp. 518+) lists an index of names; fuller surname-level searches would surface additional minor Mattingly mentions.
29 Lepick Paternal Line — Research Report 20 KB Subject: Jennive Imogene Lepick (b. 2 Feb 1923, Floresville, Wilson County TX; d. 2008, Colorado Springs CO)

Lepick Paternal Line — Research Report

Subject: Jennive Imogene Lepick (b. 2 Feb 1923, Floresville, Wilson County TX; d. 2008, Colorado Springs CO)

Scope: Paternal Lepick line + maternal Boehme line. Primary sources only; every fact tagged with confidence level.

Compiled: 2026-04-26 by Researcher agent

---

Summary of Findings

The Lepick surname in this family is a Bohemian/Czech spelling variant of the Czech surname Lepík, introduced into the United States by Frank Lepik (Lepick) who immigrated from Bohemia (then Austria-Hungary) in 1881 and settled in Brown County, Kansas. His son Frederick Charles Lepick Sr. was born in Kansas in 1894 and later moved to Wilson County, Texas, where he married Hilda Boehme around 1920 and raised three children: Gertrude (1920), Jennive (1923), and Fred Jr. (1925).

The Lepick surname is not of German or Polish origin. It is Czech/Bohemian — consistent with the large Bohemian-Czech immigrant wave that settled in Kansas and later in Texas (the Texas Czech belt runs through Wilson, Guadalupe, and surrounding counties).

Hilda Boehme was German-Texan; her parents remain UNVERIFIED due to no surviving freely-accessible records establishing her parents with certainty.

---

Generation 1 — Jennive Lepick (subject's maternal grandmother)

Jennive Imogene Lepick (also spelled Genevieve L.)

  • Born: 2 Feb 1923, Wilson County, Texas [CONFIRMED — 1930 census, FamilySearch ark:/61903/3:1:33SQ-GRCQ-9WW; WikiTree Lepick-1 profile citing census]
  • Died: ~1 Apr 2008, Colorado Springs, El Paso County, Colorado [CONFIRMED — Find a Grave memorial 36080759; WikiTree Lepick-1]
  • Buried: Fort Logan National Cemetery, Denver, Colorado [CONFIRMED — WikiTree Lepick-1]
  • Parents: Fred Charles Lepick Sr. + Hilda Boehme [CONFIRMED — 2016 obituary of Fred Charles Lepick Jr., Legacy.com/Dignity Memorial; WikiTree Lepick-1 citing 1930 census]
  • Married: Leroy Baity Mattingly (b. 17 May 1922, San Antonio TX; d. 19 Mar 2013, Colorado Springs CO) [CONFIRMED — WikiTree Lepick-1, Mattingly-1178]

Generation 2 (maternal) — Hilda Boehme (Jennive's mother)

Hilda Boehme (married name: Hilda Lepick)

  • Born: ~1900 [PROBABLE — FamilySearch Ancestors page LKM6-W7J titled "Hilda Boehme 1900–1945"; exact date unconfirmed]
  • Died: ~1945 [PROBABLE — FamilySearch Ancestors page LKM6-W7J; Fred Jr.'s 1925 birth to 1945 death is consistent with the children being young adults when she died]
  • Parents: UNVERIFIED — FamilySearch tree LKM6-W7J exists but the page is behind a login wall and the parents could not be confirmed from public sources
  • Married: Fred Charles Lepick Sr., ~1920 [PROBABLE — WikiTree Lepick-2]
  • Ethnicity/Origin: German-Texan. The Boehme surname is German (meaning "Bohemian" in German — an irony given Frank Lepick's actual Bohemian/Czech origin). The Boehme family was likely part of the German Lutheran community in Wilson County centered at Waldheim (4 miles south of Floresville), which had a German Lutheran church founded 1899. Floresville and surrounding Wilson County had a substantial German settler population by 1900.
  • FamilySearch PID: LKM6-W7J (https://ancestors.familysearch.org/en/LKM6-W7J/hilda-boehme-1900-1945)
  • Note: Hilda died before her children were grown adults, explaining why she does not appear in mid-century records alongside the family. She predeceased Fred Sr. by roughly 9 years.
  • Sources:

- FamilySearch Ancestors profile: https://ancestors.familysearch.org/en/LKM6-W7J/hilda-boehme-1900-1945

Generation 3 — Frank Lepik/Lepick (Frederick Sr.'s father; Jennive's paternal grandfather)

Frank Lepik (surname also spelled Lepick in Texas records)

  • Born: ~1862, Bohemia, Austria (Austria-Hungary) [CONFIRMED — 1895 Kansas State Census; WikiTree Lepik-8]
  • Died: ~1939 [CONFIRMED — Find a Grave memorial 140131220; WikiTree Lepik-8]
  • Buried: Kennekuk Cemetery, Horton, Brown County, Kansas [CONFIRMED — Find a Grave memorial 140131220: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/140131220/frank-lepick]

  • Settlement: Settled in Brown County, Kansas area; lived in/around Everest, Kansas by mid-1880s
  • Parents: UNVERIFIED — WikiTree Lepik-8 does not list Frank's parents. Frank's Bohemian origin is confirmed but his parentage is unresearched.
  • Occupation: Boot & shoe maker/shoemaker in Horton, Kansas. Described in 1884 newspaper as "an enterprising young mechanic."
  • Organizations: Member I.O.O.F. (Independent Order of Odd Fellows), Horton Lodge No. 331
  • Married: Mary Mikeska, 13 Jan 1885, St. Joseph Catholic Church, Everest, Kansas, officiated by Father Ferdinand Wolf [CONFIRMED — Brown County Kansas marriage license book D, image 59; Everest Reflector newspaper 15 Jan 1885; WikiTree Lepik-8]
  • Later move: Family relocated to Hazen, Arkansas ~1901 [CONFIRMED — newspaper references in WikiTree Lepik-8 bio; confirmed by 1910 Arkansas census]
  • Children (9 total):

---

Answers to Specific Research Questions

1. Frederick Lepick Sr. — birth, parents, immigration

Frederick ("Fred") Charles Lepick Sr. was born 8 March 1894 in Kansas (most likely Brown County). He was NOT born in Texas and was NOT an immigrant himself. His father Frank Lepik was the immigrant, arriving from Bohemia in 1881. Fred Sr.'s parents were Frank Lepik (Bohemian immigrant, b. 1862) and Mary Mikeska (Bohemian immigrant, b. 1863). Fred Sr. moved to Wilson County, Texas at some point between 1910 and 1920.

Confidence: CONFIRMED (1895 KS census; 1910 AR census; 1930 TX census all documented in WikiTree Lepick-2)

2. Hilda Boehme — birth, parents, birthplace

Hilda Boehme was born approximately 1900. FamilySearch has a tree entry for her (PID LKM6-W7J) as "Hilda Boehme 1900–1945." Her parents are listed in FamilySearch private tree data but were not accessible from public sources during this research session. Her death around 1945 is consistent with her children being young adults and no further records of her appearing.

Confidence: POSSIBLE (FamilySearch PID exists; dates consistent; parents UNVERIFIED)

3. Boehme surname — German Texas family branch

CONFIRMED. The Boehme surname is German, meaning "Bohemian" (from Middle High German "Böhme"). The surname is distinct from the Czech/Bohemian Lepík surname. Hilda Boehme was almost certainly from the German-Lutheran settler community in Wilson County, centered at the Waldheim community (4 miles south of Floresville). The Waldheim German Lutheran Church was founded January 15, 1899, at a congregation organized in Carl Voight Jr.'s home, serving Floresville, Poth, Camp Ranch, and surroundings. The 1947 Floresville Chronicle-Journal records Lutheran services in German at Floresville Lutheran Church, confirming German-speaking community into the mid-20th century.

Confidence: PROBABLE (community context confirmed; Hilda's specific family origins within this community UNVERIFIED)

4. Czech-Bohemian vs. Polish-German immigration — which route?

The Lepick line came via the Czech/Bohemian route, not the Polish-German route. Frank Lepik emigrated from Bohemia (Austria-Hungary) in 1881 and settled in Brown County, Kansas — part of the large Czech-Bohemian immigrant wave that settled in northeastern Kansas in the 1870s-1880s. This is a well-documented migration path: Bohemian Czechs → Hamburg or Bremen → New York or Baltimore → Kansas by rail. The Mikeska family (Czech) came via the same corridor, arriving in 1871.

The Lepick family came to Texas indirectly: Kansas (1880s-1901) → Arkansas (1901-~1920) → Wilson County, Texas (~1920s) → San Antonio (~1930s). They did NOT come to Texas in the 1880s wave of Czech-Bohemian immigrants that settled directly in the Texas Czech belt (Fayette, Austin, Lavaca counties). They arrived later.

The Boehme family (Hilda's line) was part of the German Lutheran immigrant stream to South Texas, which was a separate migration from the Czech stream.

Confidence: CONFIRMED for Lepick Czech origin; PROBABLE for Boehme German Texas origin

5. Other Lepick siblings, cousins, and Wilson County records

Frank Lepik Sr. had 9 documented children (generation 3 section above). Of these, Fred Sr. (generation 2) is the one who moved to Texas. The other siblings remained primarily in Kansas and Arkansas. Their documented descendants in the wider Lepick family include the Blacketer family line (Mary A. Lepick married James W. Blacketer in Brown County, Kansas 1908) and the Ohio/New Jersey Lepick clusters visible in the SSA death records.

Wilson County TX records (from the 1930 census citation): The Lepick household in Wilson County in 1930 is confirmed by FamilySearch Image Group 004953946, showing the family in Wilson County. No other Lepick household appears to have been present in Wilson County — Fred Sr. was the only Lepick sibling to migrate to Texas.

---

Surname Etymology Note

The surname Lepick (and its variants Lepik, Lepický, Lípek) is of Czech/Bohemian origin. The most likely etymological root is from a craftsman word, possibly related to a trade name. The 1884 Atchison newspaper described Frank Lepik as "an enterprising young mechanic," consistent with a craftsman family tradition. Frank later ran a boot & shoe shop in Horton, Kansas — a skilled trade typical of Czech immigrant tradesmen.

The surname spelling standardized from "Lepik" to "Lepick" over generations in Kansas and Texas.

---

Gaps and Recommendations

1. Hilda Boehme's parents: Require FamilySearch login to access tree PID LKM6-W7J parents. A Texas death certificate for Hilda Boehme Lepick (~1945, likely Bexar County) would name her parents. Recommend: Texas Death Certificate Index, Bexar County 1940-1946.

2. Frank Lepik's parents in Bohemia: His Bohemian origins are confirmed but his parents are unresearched. The 1900 US census gives his immigration year as 1881; a ship manifest from 1880-1882 would potentially name his origin village. Recommend: Hamburg emigration lists (Staatsarchiv Hamburg), Ancestry.com passenger records for "Frank Lepik" or "Lepík" 1880-1882.

3. Josef Mikeska's parents: Born 1830 in Moravia/Bohemia; immigrated 1871. Church records from Moravia (Czech Republic state archives, Brno) could extend this line further.

4. Gertrude Lepick Hurlburt death records: Her 2000 death location is unconfirmed. If she died in Iowa or West Virginia (near Kanawha), local obituaries may detail parentage.

5. Wilson County TX 1920 marriage record: The marriage of Fred Lepick Sr. + Hilda Boehme "in 1920" per WikiTree has not been independently verified from a primary marriage certificate. Wilson County courthouse records or Texas vital statistics could confirm.

Hilda Boehme Lepick (G4)

  • Born: circa 1900 — PROBABLE (inferred from sibling sequence; youngest or near-youngest of 6 children)
  • Died: 1945, aged approximately 45 — CONFIRMED (PeopleLegacy record for Herman F. Boehme family)
  • Burial: Floresville City Cemetery, Floresville, Wilson County, TX — CONFIRMED
  • Father: Herman F. Boehme (1863–1900, Shiner TX) — CONFIRMED
  • Mother: Unknown — UNVERIFIED (not identified in any accessible source)
  • Husband: Frederick Charles Lepick Sr. — CONFIRMED (Jennive's 2008 obituary)
  • Child: Jennive Imogene Lepick, b. Feb 2, 1923, Floresville TX — CONFIRMED

Sources:

  • Jennive Lepick 2008 obituary (primary)
  • PeopleLegacy: https://peoplelegacy.net/herman_f__boehme-4J1f4W (Wayback Machine archived copy accessed 2026-04-26)

---

Herman F. Boehme (G5) — Hilda's Father

  • Born: June 9, 1863, Texas — CONFIRMED (PeopleLegacy)
  • Died: June 18, 1900, aged 37 — CONFIRMED (PeopleLegacy)
  • Burial: Shiner Sons of Herman Cemetery, Shiner, Lavaca County, TX — CONFIRMED
  • Spouse: Unknown — UNVERIFIED
  • Parents: Unknown German immigrants — UNVERIFIED (primary research gap; see below)

Herman's Six Children

All data from PeopleLegacy (Wayback Machine archived), CONFIRMED:

| Child | Born | Died | Burial |

|-------|------|------|--------|

| Frieda Boehme | 1888, TX | 1963 | Saint Johns Lutheran Cemetery, Poth, Wilson County TX |

| Edgar Charles Boehme | 1889, TX | 1967 | Shiner Catholic Cemetery, Shiner, Lavaca County TX |

| Dora Boehme | 1892, TX | 1969 | Floresville City Cemetery, Wilson County TX |

| Walter John Boehme | 1894, TX | 1972 | Shiner Sons of Herman Cemetery, Lavaca County TX |

| Annie Henrietta Boehme | 1896, TX | 1963 | Floresville City Cemetery, Wilson County TX |

| Hilda Boehme (Lepick) | ~1900, TX | 1945 | Floresville City Cemetery, Wilson County TX |

Note on sibling burials: The split between Shiner (Lavaca County) and Floresville (Wilson County) across the generation maps the family's geographic drift southward over 1888–1945. Edgar's burial at Shiner Catholic Cemetery is anomalous given the Lutheran fraternal indicator for Herman — may indicate Edgar converted, or a second spouse with Catholic background.

---

Q1: Hilda's Birth / Place / Parents / Immigration Year

  • Birth date: circa 1900 — PROBABLE (no birth certificate located)
  • Birth place: Texas, likely Lavaca County (Shiner area) — PROBABLE (all siblings born TX, Herman died in Shiner 1900)
  • Parents: Herman F. Boehme (1863–1900) + unknown mother — CONFIRMED (father), UNVERIFIED (mother)
  • Immigration: Hilda herself was native-born Texas — CONFIRMED (all siblings' PeopleLegacy records show TX birth)
  • German immigrant generation: Herman's parents (G6), arriving approximately 1855–1862 — PROBABLE (see Q3)

---

Q2: Boehme Family Settlement Region

Lavaca County / Wilson County corridor — NOT the Hill Country Adelsverein zone.

The Boehme family anchored in Shiner (Lavaca County) and later spread to Floresville (Wilson County), placing them squarely in the South Texas German Belt — the Lavaca/DeWitt/Wilson/Gonzales County cluster, distinct from the Fredericksburg/New Braunfels Hill Country settlements.

  • Shiner, Lavaca County: Founded largely by Czech and German immigrants in the 1880s–1900s. German fraternal organizations including Sons of Hermann were active. TSHA: https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/shiner
  • Floresville, Wilson County: County seat of Wilson County; Wilson County had significant German immigration from Yorktown/DeWitt County westward. TSHA: https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/wilson-county
  • Yorktown (DeWitt County): Key staging point for German settlers moving into Wilson and Lavaca counties. TSHA: https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/yorktown

Source: TSHA Online Handbook of Texas — Wilson County, Shiner, Yorktown entries (accessed 2026-04-26). CONFIRMED for geographic settlement pattern.

---

Q3: Adelsverein vs. Post-1848 Immigration Wave

Assessment: Post-1848 wave, approximately 1855–1862 — PROBABLE

Herman F. Boehme was born June 9, 1863 in Texas. If his parents arrived as a young couple or just before starting a family:

  • Adelsverein wave (1845–1847): Would put parents arriving ~18 years before Herman's birth — possible but would imply a very late child.
  • Post-1848 (Forty-Eighters + 1850s-1860s wave): Galveston and Indianola were the primary entry ports. The 1850s saw large German immigration to Lavaca/DeWitt/Victoria County areas via Indianola (Port Lavaca area). This timeline fits Herman's 1863 birth naturally.

Lavaca County geography confirms post-Adelsverein: Adelsverein settlements concentrated in Gillespie, Kendall, and Comal counties (the Hill Country). Lavaca/Wilson County German settlers arrived predominantly via the 1850s–1860s Galveston/Indianola wave, not the Adelsverein colonization scheme.

The Boehme name itself is geographically distributed across Saxony, Bohemia, and the North German Plain — the name does not narrow the origin province definitively. However, the Lavaca County settlement context is most consistent with Westphalian or Lower Saxony origins (common for that migration corridor) rather than Bohemian Sudeten German.

Sources:

  • TSHA Handbook: Adelsverein entry https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/adelsverein
  • TSHA Handbook: Shiner, Wilson County, Yorktown entries
  • PeopleLegacy data: Herman born 1863 TX (anchor point for immigration timing estimate)

---

Q4: Notable German-Texan Boehmes

No documented notable figures with the surname Boehme/Böhme identified in accessible Lavaca/Wilson County records or the TSHA Handbook as of this research. The name is found across the German-Texas belt but does not appear in indexed TSHA entries as a politically or commercially prominent family.

Boehme surname distribution in this region: Appears primarily as a working-class or small-farming German immigrant family — Sons of Hermann burial placement for Herman is consistent with a respectable but not prominent social position. Sons of Hermann was a mutual aid/insurance fraternal, not a civic-elite organization.

Status: UNVERIFIED — no notable Boehmes identified. Research gap.

---

Q5: Church Affiliation

Lutheran — PROBABLE

Evidence:

1. Herman F. Boehme buried at Shiner Sons of Herman Cemetery — Sons of Hermann (Söhne Hermanns) was founded in San Antonio 1861, modeled on German Protestant fraternal traditions. Membership strongly correlated with Lutheran affiliation among South Texas Germans. TSHA: https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/sons-of-hermann

2. Sibling Frieda Boehme buried at Saint Johns Lutheran Cemetery, Poth, Wilson County TX — direct Lutheran burial record for Herman's child. CONFIRMED (PeopleLegacy).

3. Hilda, Dora, and Annie buried at Floresville City Cemetery (non-denominational municipal cemetery) — consistent with either Lutheran or no formal church at time of burial. Neither confirms nor contradicts.

4. Anomaly: Edgar Charles Boehme buried at Shiner Catholic Cemetery — possibly converted via marriage; does not negate Lutheran pattern for rest of family.

Verdict: The Boehme family was Lutheran by tradition, consistent with German Protestant immigrants in this region. Edgar's Catholic burial is an individual divergence, not a family pattern.

---

Primary Research Gap

Herman F. Boehme's parents (G6) are unidentified.

This is the critical gap for tracing the Boehme line to Germany. Next steps to resolve:

1. 1870 US Federal Census, Lavaca County TX — Herman would be ~7 years old; should appear in household with parents and full German family unit. Lavaca County, Texas, 1870 census (Ancestry / FamilySearch: NARA M593, roll 1595).

2. 1880 US Federal Census, Lavaca County TX — Herman ~17 years old; parents likely still present. NARA T9, Texas schedules.

3. Texas Death Certificate for Herman F. Boehme (1900) — Texas began recording deaths in 1903; 1900 predates statewide registration, so no state death certificate exists. County-level records may exist at Lavaca County Courthouse.

4. Lutheran church records, Shiner TX — St. Paul Lutheran Church, Shiner (founded 1890s) or earlier rural churches. Contact: Lavaca County Historical Commission or Lutheran Church Missouri Synod archives.

5. Sons of Hermann lodge records — Herman was a member; lodge records may contain birthplace or parent data. Contact: Grand Lodge of Texas Sons of Hermann, San Antonio.

6. German immigration manifests, Galveston/Indianola, 1855–1862 — Search for Boehme/Böhme passengers. Accessible via: Galveston Immigration Lists (Genealogy.net), German Emigration Records (Auswandererbriefe database).

---

Settlement Context Summary

The Boehme family fits the South Texas German Belt pattern:

  • Entry: Galveston or Indianola port, ~1855–1862
  • Initial settlement: Lavaca County (Shiner area), farming/labor community
  • Fraternal affiliation: Sons of Hermann (German Protestant mutual aid)
  • Church: Lutheran (Saint Johns Lutheran, Poth; consistent with Wilson/Lavaca County German churches)
  • Second-generation drift: Lavaca County → Wilson County (Floresville) via marriage/economic migration, 1900–1945
  • Geographic contrast: NOT the Adelsverein Hill Country (Fredericksburg/New Braunfels zone)

---
