  • Lyman Carrier's Agriculture in Virginia, 1607-1699 states that William Spence arrived on the Phoenix on 20 April 1608, describes him as the first experienced English farmer in Virginia, and notes that he served in the first House of Burgesses in 1619.
  • A separate 1624 Virginia census transcription lists Sarah Spence at James Island.
  • Confidence: HIGH for the records themselves; LOW for any direct connection to Hunter's branch
  • Sources:

- http://www.gutenberg.org/cache/epub/28730/pg28730-images.html

- https://www.cynthiaswope.com/withinthevines/jamestown/jamestowncensus1624.html

- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Spence_%28burgess%29

Henslee surname context

  • Ancestry's surname page treats Henslee as an English variant of Hensley and notes that the surname was already present in the United States by 1840, with Alabama the largest early concentration in that census year.
  • Confidence: LOW to MEDIUM as surname context; not a proven direct line for Hunter
  • Source:

- https://www.ancestry.com/last-name-meaning/henslee

What I can say with confidence now

  • Alice Marie Henslee Spence was real, deceased, and publicly indexed with specific birth, death, and burial information in Beaumont.
  • Lee S. Henslee and Frances Virginia Henslee were also real, deceased, and publicly indexed in the same Beaumont cemetery, with Lee and Frances identified as spouses.
  • The public academic record strongly supports the Rice/Beaumont Spence branch described in local context.
  • The Spence surname has a documented Virginia presence by 1608-1624, but that does not yet prove Hunter's direct colonial line.

Open gaps

1. Prove Alice-to-Lee/Frances with a directly retrievable obituary, death certificate, or cemetery-family page.

2. Identify Frances's maiden name and parents.

3. Identify Lee Stuart Henslee's parents and earlier Henslee line.

4. Resolve Dovie (?) Spence with a document stronger than family memory.

5. Find a direct immigration or English birth record for the older Spence branch if the family tradition about England is to be pushed further back.

Practical next records to target

1. Jefferson County or Beaumont-area obituary archives for Alice Marie Henslee Spence (d. 3 December 2005).

2. Broussard's Mortuary or Forest Lawn memorial pages for Frances Henslee and Lee S. Henslee.

3. Texas death certificates for Alice (2005), Lee (1994), and Frances (2008).

4. Texas birth record for Alice Marie Henslee, born 13 January 1936, indexed as Rusk, Texas.

5. 1940 and 1950 census images for Lee/Frances Henslee households once a non-paywalled image path is located.

43 Mattingly Colonial / Early-America Notables and Collateral Lines 16 KB Compiled: 2026-04-26

Mattingly Colonial / Early-America Notables and Collateral Lines

Compiled: 2026-04-26

Scope: Verifiable deceased Mattingly ancestors and collateral lines tied to colonial Maryland and early Kentucky, with emphasis on arrival timing, named tracts, militia/public roles, Catholic settlement, and source-backed notable descendants.

Confidence tiers: CONFIRMED (direct public record or 2+ independent sources) | PROBABLE (strong compiled source, partially corroborated) | POSSIBLE (plausible but not fully bridged this session) | UNVERIFIED (not retained here)

---

Executive Summary

The strongest early-America Mattingly facts available in public, linkable sources are these:

1. The immigrant-generation correction is probably 1663-1664, not a 1660 arrival with Lord Baltimore. The best easily accessible citation in this session is Herman E. Mattingly's 1975 book as surfaced by Google Books.

2. Maryland archival indexes confirm that Mattinglys Hope, Mount Misery, St. Thomas, and St. Johns als Mattingly Lane were real Mattingly-associated tracts in the St. Mary's / Charles County record stream.

3. Edward Mattingly is directly documented in Maryland state records as a captain in the Upper Battalion of Militia in Saint Mary's County in 1779.

4. Benedict Joseph Webb's 1884 Kentucky history remains the key public source for the 1785 Catholic migration and the 1786 arrival of William, Leonard, and Lucas Mattingly from St. Mary's County, Maryland.

5. The best source-backed early Kentucky public/community role in the collateral lines is Zachariah Mattingly at Hardinsburg, with Ignatius and Joseph Mattingly helping build the first chapel.

6. The clearest later notable collateral descendant in this research pass is Mother Generose Mattingly of the Sisters of Loretto; Marie Mattingly Meloney is a strong later Bardstown collateral but not fully bridged here to the exact branch.

---

1. Colonial Maryland: Arrival and Land

1.1 Thomas Mattingly family in Maryland by 1663-1664

Claim: The first American Mattingly family was in Maryland by 1663-1664, and Thomas Mattingly died soon after arrival in 1664.

Assessment: PROBABLE

Why this holds: The strongest public citation I could verify directly this session is the Google Books snippet for Herman E. Mattingly's The Mattingly Family in Early America (1975), which states that Thomas Mattingly and his family left England for Maryland in 1663 and that he died soon after arrival in 1664. That is a compiled genealogy rather than a primary image, so I am not marking it CONFIRMED by itself.

What this displaces: I did not find a directly viewed public record in this session supporting a 1660 arrival with Lord Baltimore. The accessible evidence points later.

Sources:

  • Google Books, The Mattingly Family in Early America metadata/snippet: https://books.google.com/books/about/The_Mattingly_Family_in_Early_America.html?id=nlw0AAAAMAAJ
  • Open Library metadata for the same book: https://openlibrary.org/works/OL6663283W/The_Mattingly_family_in_early_America

1.2 Mattinglys Hope as an early tract

Claim: Mattinglys Hope was a real Mattingly-associated tract in the Maryland land record tradition.

Assessment: CONFIRMED

Why this holds: Maryland State Archives rent-roll and tract-index materials publicly surface the tract name in official archival series. The spelling varies (Mattenlyes Hope, Mattenlys Hope, Mattinglys Hope), which is normal for the period.

Sources:

  • Maryland State Archives rent-roll PDF snippet showing Mattenlyes Hope, Mattenlys Hope, and Mattinglys Lane: https://msa.maryland.gov/megafile/msa/stagserm/sm100/sm130/000000/000014/pdf/mdsa_sm130_14.pdf
  • Maryland State Archives rent-roll index snippet showing MATTINGLY, JAMES ... Mattinglys Hope ... St. Johns: https://msa.maryland.gov/megafile/msa/stagser/s1400/s1430/000000/000019/pdf/mdsa_s1430_19.pdf

1.3 Precise 1666 patent details for Mattinglys Hope

Claim: The immigrant family's rights became a 300-acre Mattingly's Hope patent in 1666, associated with the sons Thomas and Cezar.

Assessment: PROBABLE

Why this is not higher: This is repeated in later Mattingly compilations and derivative genealogy pages, and it fits the official tract-name evidence, but I did not inspect the original patent image or a Maryland State Archives item page for the 1666 patent in this session. The tract is real; the exact 1666 transaction detail remains one step removed here.

Use cautiously: Good enough for a research note, not good enough yet for a final narrative claim without checking the actual patent/certificate image or a direct MSA item entry.

Sources:

  • Compiled family-history quotation of the 1666 patent sequence, explicitly tied to Herman E. Mattingly's research: https://maineandbeyond.tripod.com/id14.html
  • Maryland State Archives tract/rent-roll continuity evidence for Mattinglys Hope: https://msa.maryland.gov/megafile/msa/stagserm/sm100/sm130/000000/000014/pdf/mdsa_sm130_14.pdf

1.4 Mount Misery / St. Thomas / Mattingly Lane continuity

Claim: By the eighteenth century, Thomas/Edward Mattingly are tied in archival indexes to Mount Misery, St. Thomas, and St. Johns als Mattingly Lane.

Assessment: CONFIRMED

Why this matters: Even without the original seventeenth-century patent image in hand, the official Maryland archival indexes show the family's land-name continuity in the same region.

Sources:

  • Maryland State Archives rent-roll name index snippet for MATTINGLY, THOMAS ... Mount Misery ... St. Thomas ... St. Johns als Mattingly Lane: https://msa.maryland.gov/megafile/msa/stagser/s1400/s1430/000000/000019/pdf/mdsa_s1430_19.pdf
  • Maryland State Archives plat/tract continuity for Mattinglys Hope as part of later St. Mary's surveys: https://guide.msa.maryland.gov/pages/series.aspx?id=C2225

---

2. Revolutionary-Era Maryland Public Role

2.1 Edward Mattingly, militia captain

Claim: Edward Mattingly served as a captain in the Upper Battalion of Militia in Saint Mary's County in 1779.

Assessment: CONFIRMED

Why this holds: The Maryland State Archives page for Journal and Correspondence of the Council of Maryland, 1779-1780 includes the commission notice naming Edward Mattingly as captain, with Jonathan Edwards as first lieutenant and William Cartwright as second lieutenant.

Research value: This is the cleanest directly linkable Mattingly public office / militia role I found in colonial Maryland records this session.

Source:

  • Maryland State Archives, Journal and Correspondence of the Council of Maryland, 1779-1780, vol. 43, p. 18: https://msa.maryland.gov/megafile/msa/speccol/sc2900/sc2908/000001/000043/html/am43--18.html

---

3. Maryland Catholics to Kentucky Catholics

3.1 The 1785 Catholic league from Maryland

Claim: In 1785 a league of sixty Catholic families, mostly from St. Mary's County, Maryland, organized emigration to Kentucky; twenty-five families left early in 1785 and reached Kentucky by late spring.

Assessment: CONFIRMED

Why this holds: Webb is explicit on the league, the first wave size, and the route via Pittsburgh and Maysville to Pottinger's Creek. This is the foundational migration context for the Kentucky Mattinglys.

Source:

  • Searchable mirror of Benedict Joseph Webb, The Centenary of Catholicity in Kentucky (1884), Pottinger's Creek chapter: https://indianacatholic.mwweb.org/The_Centenary_of_Catholicity_in_Kentucky.pdf

3.2 Philip Mattingly in the Pottinger's Creek cluster

Claim: Philip Mattingly appears among the Pottinger's Creek Catholic settlers listed by Webb.

Assessment: PROBABLE

Why not confirmed: Webb says the full names of the original twenty-five are not all certainly known, and the longer Pottinger's Creek list covers settlers up to 1800. Philip Mattingly is definitely in Webb's list, but his exact status as one of the spring 1785 founders is slightly softer than the restatement sometimes given in family lore.

Source:

  • Webb mirror, Pottinger's Creek settlers list including Walter Burch, Philip Mattingly: https://indianacatholic.mwweb.org/The_Centenary_of_Catholicity_in_Kentucky.pdf

3.3 William, Leonard, and Lucas Mattingly at Hardin's Creek

Claim: William, Leonard, and Lucas Mattingly, brothers from St. Mary's County, Maryland, arrived at the Hardin's Creek settlement in early 1786.

Assessment: CONFIRMED

Why this matters: This is the central Kentucky Mattingly founding fact in the public literature. Webb explicitly says the three brothers came from St. Mary's County and that the connection spread across the South and West.

Source:

  • Webb mirror, Hardin's Creek chapter: https://indianacatholic.mwweb.org/The_Centenary_of_Catholicity_in_Kentucky.pdf

3.4 Leonard Mattingly as Kentucky patriarch

Claim: Leonard Mattingly died in 1827 with an estimated nearly 300 living descendants.

Assessment: CONFIRMED

Why this matters: It explains why so many nineteenth-century Kentucky Catholic Mattinglys appear quickly in Marion, Nelson, Washington, and nearby counties.

Source:

  • Webb mirror, Hardin's Creek chapter and footnote: https://indianacatholic.mwweb.org/The_Centenary_of_Catholicity_in_Kentucky.pdf

---

4. Early Kentucky Public / Community Roles in Collateral Lines

4.1 Richard Mattingly of Long Lick

Claim: Richard Mattingly, born in Maryland in 1756, removed to Kentucky in 1791 and settled on Long Lick in what became Breckinridge County.

Assessment: CONFIRMED

Why this matters: This is a distinct but clearly documented early Kentucky Mattingly branch, separate from the three Hardin's Creek brothers.

Source:

  • Webb mirror, Breckinridge chapter: https://indianacatholic.mwweb.org/The_Centenary_of_Catholicity_in_Kentucky.pdf

4.2 Zachariah Mattingly at Hardinsburg

Claim: Zachariah Mattingly was the first resident Catholic of Hardinsburg and likely the donor of the church and cemetery ground used by the early Catholic community there.

Assessment: CONFIRMED

Why this matters: This is one of the better source-backed community roles in the Kentucky collateral lines: not just residence, but visible local church-building leadership.

Source:

  • Webb mirror, Breckinridge chapter: https://indianacatholic.mwweb.org/The_Centenary_of_Catholicity_in_Kentucky.pdf

4.3 Ignatius and Joseph Mattingly helped build the first Hardinsburg chapel

Claim: Ignatius and Joseph Mattingly, of Long Lick, materially helped build the first log chapel at Hardinsburg by getting out heavy timbers and hand-sawing planks.

Assessment: CONFIRMED

Why this matters: It is a small but unusually concrete labor-history detail in the early Kentucky Mattingly record.

Source:

  • Webb mirror, Breckinridge chapter: https://indianacatholic.mwweb.org/The_Centenary_of_Catholicity_in_Kentucky.pdf

---

5. Source-Backed Notable Descendants / Collaterals

5.1 Mother Generose Mattingly (Loretto)

Claim: Mother Generose Mattingly served as a Loretto leader from 1842 to 1844, and Webb identifies Sister Generose as Martha, daughter of Basil Mattingly.

Assessment: CONFIRMED

Why this holds: Webb identifies Basil's daughter Martha as the future Sister Generose. The Loretto Community's own leadership timeline independently confirms a Mother Generose Mattingly in 1842-1844.

Why it matters: This is a well-sourced example of the Kentucky Mattinglys producing a clearly identifiable public religious leader in the nineteenth century.

Sources:

  • Webb mirror, Hardin's Creek family notes: https://indianacatholic.mwweb.org/The_Centenary_of_Catholicity_in_Kentucky.pdf
  • Loretto Community leadership timeline: https://www.lorettocommunity.org/about/timeline-loretto-leaders/

5.2 Marie Mattingly Meloney (journalist, Curie fundraiser)

Claim: Marie Mattingly Meloney (1878-1943), born in Bardstown, Kentucky, daughter of physician Cyprian Peter Mattingly, was a major American journalist and editor.

Assessment: CONFIRMED for identity and parentage; POSSIBLE to PROBABLE as a specifically bridged collateral to the Leonard/Hardin's Creek line in this session.

Why this stays cautious: Britannica cleanly establishes who she was and where she was born. Her Bardstown origin makes same-clan descent highly plausible, but I did not bridge her exact father Cyprian Peter Mattingly to a documented Leonard/William/Lucas descendant in this pass.

Research value: Strong notable collateral candidate, but still needs one more generational bridge before being treated as a precise cousin line.

Source:

  • Britannica biography: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Marie-Mattingly-Meloney

---

6. Claims I Would Not Upgrade Yet

6.1 "Thomas Mattingly arrived with Lord Baltimore in 1660"

Status: UNVERIFIED

I did not find a directly viewed public record supporting the 1660 version in this session. The best accessible compiled source points instead to 1663-1664.

6.2 Exact original 1666 patent chain without viewing the patent image

Status: keep at PROBABLE

The tract name is secure. The exact certificate/patent mechanics need the original Maryland Land Office image or a direct MSA item-level citation.

6.3 Famous modern Mattinglys as Kentucky-clan cousins

Status: not included here

I did not keep Don Mattingly, Ken Mattingly, or other modern surname bearers in this file because this task was better served by deceased, source-backed early-America lines and later deceased collateral notables with tighter evidence.

---

7. Best Next Checks

1. Pull the original Maryland Land Office patent/certificate image for Mattingly's Hope and replace the provisional 1666 wording with an item-level citation.

2. Bridge Cyprian Peter Mattingly into the Bardstown / St. Charles Kentucky tree to upgrade Marie Mattingly Meloney from POSSIBLE/PROBABLE to a specific collateral placement.

3. Check whether Edward Mattingly's 1779 militia commission can be tied directly to the same branch holding Mount Misery / Mattinglys Lane, or whether that should remain a surname-level St. Mary's County placement.

---

Source Index

  • Benedict Joseph Webb, The Centenary of Catholicity in Kentucky mirror: https://indianacatholic.mwweb.org/The_Centenary_of_Catholicity_in_Kentucky.pdf
  • Google Books, Herman E. Mattingly, The Mattingly Family in Early America: https://books.google.com/books/about/The_Mattingly_Family_in_Early_America.html?id=nlw0AAAAMAAJ
  • Open Library, The Mattingly Family in Early America: https://openlibrary.org/works/OL6663283W/The_Mattingly_family_in_early_America
  • Maryland State Archives, Council journal naming Edward Mattingly captain: https://msa.maryland.gov/megafile/msa/speccol/sc2900/sc2908/000001/000043/html/am43--18.html
  • Maryland State Archives, rent-roll PDF with Mattenlyes Hope / Mattenlys Hope / Mattinglys Lane: https://msa.maryland.gov/megafile/msa/stagserm/sm100/sm130/000000/000014/pdf/mdsa_sm130_14.pdf
  • Maryland State Archives, rent-roll index showing Thomas/James Mattingly tract associations: https://msa.maryland.gov/megafile/msa/stagser/s1400/s1430/000000/000019/pdf/mdsa_s1430_19.pdf
  • Maryland State Archives, later tract continuity in St. Mary's County plat books: https://guide.msa.maryland.gov/pages/series.aspx?id=C2225
  • Loretto Community leadership timeline: https://www.lorettocommunity.org/about/timeline-loretto-leaders/
  • Britannica, Marie Mattingly Meloney: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Marie-Mattingly-Meloney
  • Lower-confidence compiled page preserving the 1666 patent sequence: https://maineandbeyond.tripod.com/id14.html
44 Lepik / Lepick + Mikeska + Boehme Lines - Expanded Maternal Research 21 KB Scope: Jennive Imogene Lepick Mattingly's maternal Lepik/Lepick, Mikeska, and Boehme ancestry

Lepik / Lepick + Mikeska + Boehme Lines - Expanded Maternal Research

Scope: Jennive Imogene Lepick Mattingly's maternal Lepik/Lepick, Mikeska, and Boehme ancestry

Compiled: 2026-04-26

Research standard for this pass: favor direct obituary, census, marriage-book, cemetery, and local-history sources; flag secondary aggregators clearly

---

Bottom line

The best-supported line at present is:

Jennive Imogene Lepick (1923-2008)

daughter of Fred Charles Lepick Sr. (1894-1954) and Hilda Boehme Lepick (1900-1945)

granddaughter of Frank Lepik/Lepick (Bohemia, immigrated 1881) and Mary Mikeska (Bohemia, immigrated by 1871 per 1900 census)

and of Herman F. Boehme (1863-1900, Lavaca County/Shiner, Texas).

The strongest new finding in this pass is on the Boehme side: a cemetery aggregator with internally consistent relationship data identifies Hilda's mother as Minna Marie Macker, later Luedecke, which would explain why Hilda's younger half-siblings carried the Luedecke surname. I am treating that as strong secondary evidence, not final proof, until matched to a death certificate, church register, or census household.

The strongest immigration evidence in this pass is not a passenger manifest but the 1900 U.S. census:

  • Frank Lepik reported immigration in 1881.
  • Mary Lepik reported immigration in 1871.

I did not find a ship manifest for either person in this pass.

---

High-confidence lineage

1. Jennive Imogene Lepick Mattingly (1923-2008)

Status: CONFIRMED

Direct obituary evidence states that Jennive was born in Floresville, Texas on February 2, 1923 to Frederick and Hilda (Boehme) Lepick. Her obituary also states that she had three children, that she was married to Leroy B. Mattingly, and that she was predeceased by her parents and sister Gertrude (Hurlburt).

Supporting sources:

  • The Gazette obituary: https://obits.gazette.com/us/obituaries/gazette/name/jennive-mattingly-obituary?id=24083135
  • 1930 U.S. census, Wilson County, Texas: https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:33SQ-GRCQ-9WW

Why this matters: this obituary is the cleanest direct link tying Jennive -> Hilda Boehme -> Fred Charles Lepick Sr.

---

2. Fred Charles Lepick Sr. (1894-1954)

Status: CONFIRMED for parent-child relationship to Jennive; PROBABLE/CONFIRMED for Kansas-born son of Frank and Mary depending on source used

Fred Sr. is named as Jennive's father in Jennive's obituary and as the father of Fred Jr. in Fred Jr.'s obituary. Earlier repo research also tied him to the Kansas-born household of Frank and Mary Lepik/Lepick through the Kansas state census and later federal census records.

Supporting sources:

  • Jennive obituary: https://obits.gazette.com/us/obituaries/gazette/name/jennive-mattingly-obituary?id=24083135
  • Fred Charles Lepick Jr. obituary: https://www.dignitymemorial.com/obituaries/san-antonio-tx/fred-lepick-12178840
  • 1895 Kansas State Census, Brown County: https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:3Q9M-CS3B-RSJ8-H
  • 1910 U.S. census, Prairie Township, Prairie County, Arkansas: https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:33S7-9RVL-147
  • Find a Grave memorial used in prior repo research: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/55217353/fred_charles-lepick

Notes:

  • Fred Jr.'s obituary gives a direct, contemporary-family statement that he was born to Hilda Boehme and Fred Charles Lepick Sr.
  • The Kansas and Arkansas census trail is consistent with a family move from Brown County, Kansas -> Prairie County, Arkansas -> Wilson/Bexar County, Texas.

---

3. Hilda Boehme Lepick (1900-1945)

Status: CONFIRMED as mother of Jennive and wife of Fred Sr.; STRONG SECONDARY for exact dates and for parentage

Direct obituary evidence confirms Hilda as Jennive's mother and Fred Jr.'s mother. A secondary burial aggregator identifies her as:

  • born 8 January 1900
  • died 22 February 1945
  • buried at Floresville City Cemetery
  • daughter of Herman F. Boehme and Minna Marie Macker
  • wife of Fred Charles Lepick, Sr.

Supporting sources:

  • Jennive obituary: https://obits.gazette.com/us/obituaries/gazette/name/jennive-mattingly-obituary?id=24083135
  • Fred Jr. obituary: https://www.dignitymemorial.com/obituaries/san-antonio-tx/fred-lepick-12178840
  • PeopleLegacy profile for Hilda: https://peoplelegacy.com/hilda_boehme_lepick-4n6E2q

Assessment:

  • I treat "Hilda Boehme was Jennive's mother" as CONFIRMED.
  • I treat the exact birth/death dates and parents from PeopleLegacy as PROBABLE to STRONG SECONDARY, because PeopleLegacy explicitly disclaims independent verification.

Still, the Hilda page is useful because it fits the independently known facts:

  • correct married surname
  • correct husband
  • correct children, including Jennive and Fred Jr.
  • correct burial region in Floresville

---

4. Herman F. Boehme (1863-1900)

Status: STRONG SECONDARY / PROBABLE as Hilda's father; exact dates from cemetery aggregator

The strongest current evidence for Herman is the cemetery record aggregation that identifies:

  • Herman F. Boehme, born June 9, 1863
  • died June 18, 1900
  • buried in Shiner Sons of Herman Cemetery, Shiner, Lavaca County, Texas
  • father of Frieda Augusta Boehme Donnerberg, Edgar Charles Boehme, Dora M. Boehme Koether, Walter John Boehme, Annie Henrietta Boehme Lange, and Hilda Boehme Lepick

Supporting sources:

  • PeopleLegacy Herman page: https://peoplelegacy.com/herman_f__boehme-4J1f4W
  • TSHA, Shiner, TX: https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/shiner-tx

Why this is believable:

  • The sibling cluster is internally consistent.
  • Those sibling surnames and burial places line up with the Floresville / Poth / Shiner corridor.
  • Shiner was a town where Czech and German immigrants soon became the dominant ethnic groups after the railroad town formed in the late 1880s, which matches the Boehme family's setting.

Why this is not yet final:

  • I do not yet have Herman on a census page or in a church register from this pass.
  • I do not yet have a primary burial transcription or a photographed stone from a directly verifiable cemetery source.

---

The new Boehme-side finding: Minna Marie Macker, later Luedecke

Status: STRONG SECONDARY, needs primary confirmation

The most useful new lead is that the Hilda and Herman burial pages point to Minna Marie Macker Luedecke as the wife/mother in this line.

The pattern implied by the cemetery aggregator is:

1. Minna Marie Macker married Herman F. Boehme.

2. They had the six Boehme children, including Hilda.

3. Herman died in 1900.

4. Minna later married John W. Luedecke.

5. Minna and John had at least two additional children, Herbert John Luedecke and William Luedecke.

Supporting sources:

  • Hilda page: https://peoplelegacy.com/hilda_boehme_lepick-4n6E2q
  • Minna page: https://peoplelegacy.com/minna_marie_macker_luedecke-4f6E2q

Why this matters:

  • It explains why the same cemetery network shows both Boehme and Luedecke children attached to the same mother.
  • It gives a plausible explanation for why Hilda's mother was hard to identify when looking only for a "Boehme" mother.

What would upgrade this to confirmed:

  • 1900 census household for Minna shortly after Herman's death
  • remarriage record for Minna Macker/Minna Boehme -> John W. Luedecke
  • Hilda's death certificate
  • church burial or baptism records in Lavaca or Wilson County

---

The Kansas / Bohemian branch

5. Frank Lepik / Lepick (Bohemia -> Brown County, Kansas)

Status: CONFIRMED that he was the immigrant ancestor in this line; direct ship manifest still not found

Frank is the clearest immigrant ancestor in the Lepick surname line. Prior repo research, backed by Brown County marriage records, census citations, and Kansas newspaper citations, places him in Brown County, Kansas by the mid-1880s as a Bohemian-born shoemaker.

Best-supported facts:

  • born about 1862 in Bohemia
  • reported immigration in 1881 on the 1900 U.S. census
  • married Mary Mikeska on January 13, 1885
  • worked as a shoemaker / bootmaker
  • later associated with Horton / Everest / Kennekuk in Brown County

Supporting sources:

  • 1900 U.S. census, Brown County, Kansas: https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:S3HT-6LD4-QVJ
  • Brown County marriage book, Frank Lepik / Mary Mikeska: https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:939J-TCSZ-CM
  • 1895 Kansas State Census: https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:3Q9M-CS3B-RSJ8-H
  • Brown County cemetery location context: https://usgenwebsites.org/KSGenWeb/archives/brown/cemeteries/Mission%20Twp%20Cemeteries.htm
  • Find a Grave memorial used in prior repo research: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/140131220/frank-lepick

Important immigration note:

  • The 1900 census is the best located immigration record in this pass. It is an immigration-year statement, not a passenger list.
  • I did not locate Frank's ship manifest in this pass.

Local context:

  • Everest was a Catholic-centered Brown County settlement with an established church before the railroad town expanded in 1882; by the early 1880s it had a substantial Catholic population and became a natural place for a Bohemian Catholic marriage like Lepik-Mikeska.
  • Source: https://legendsofkansas.com/everest-kansas/

---

6. Mary Mikeska Lepick (Bohemia -> Brown County, Kansas)

Status: CONFIRMED as Frank's wife and Fred Sr.'s mother; STRONG INFERENCE that she arrived with the Mikeska family

Mary is firmly placed in the same Brown County, Kansas immigrant cluster. The most useful immigration clue is the 1900 census, which reportedly gives her immigration year as 1871. That makes her the earlier immigrant in the couple and strongly suggests she arrived as a child with her parents.

Best-supported facts:

  • born about 1863 in Bohemia
  • married Frank Lepik on January 13, 1885
  • reported immigration by 1871 on the 1900 U.S. census

Supporting sources:

  • 1900 U.S. census, Brown County, Kansas: https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:S3HT-6LD4-QVJ
  • Brown County marriage book: https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:939J-TCSZ-CM
  • 1880 U.S. census citation used in prior repo research: https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:33SQ-GYBH-T2X
  • Find a Grave memorial used in prior repo research: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/140131016

Important caution:

  • I am not repeating the earlier claim that Josef Mikeska's 1871 arrival was "confirmed by the 1880 census." The 1880 federal census does not record immigration year.
  • What is supportable is narrower: if Mary's 1900 census entry reports arrival in 1871, then her family was likely in the United States by 1871.

---

Probable extension beyond Mary: Josef and Johanna Mikeska

Status: PROBABLE, not fully re-proved in this pass

Earlier repo research linked Mary to Josef Mikeska and Johanna / Johana Mikeska, both buried in Brown County. I am keeping that extension, but with a more conservative label than the earlier file used.

What is likely:

  • Josef Mikeska was the older Bohemian/Moravian immigrant generation in Brown County.
  • Johanna Mikeska survived him and appears in Brown County newspaper-obituary citations used in the earlier repo pass.
  • If Mary's 1900 census immigration year is correctly read as 1871, then Josef/Johanna are the likeliest parent generation for that arrival.

Sources carried forward from earlier repo research:

  • Josef obituary citation: https://kansashistoricalopencontent.newspapers.com/article/the-kansas-democrat/195265760/
  • Johana obituary citation: https://kansashistoricalopencontent.newspapers.com/article/everest-enterprise-obituary-for-johana-m/195149300/
  • Find a Grave memorial used in prior repo research for Josef: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/140130956/josef-mikeska

Why I am not marking this confirmed:

  • The Kansas newspaper pages are currently blocked behind 403 in this environment.
  • I did not independently re-open the obituary images in this pass.

This is still a good next-step cluster, but it should be rechecked from the actual obituary images or cemetery transcriptions before being promoted to final.

---

Immigration and migration pattern

Lepik / Mikeska

Most likely route currently supported by records:

  • Mary Mikeska came from Bohemia by 1871 (per 1900 census report)
  • Frank Lepik came from Bohemia in 1881 (per 1900 census report)
  • they married in Brown County, Kansas in 1885
  • their family appears in Kansas, then later in Prairie County, Arkansas by 1910
  • Fred Sr.'s branch then moved to Wilson County / San Antonio, Texas

This is a classic Bohemian/Czech migration chain into northeastern Kansas, not a direct Texas Czech settlement from Europe.

Context sources:

  • Brown County history: https://www.kansashistory.gov/kansapedia/brown-county-kansas/15284
  • Everest Catholic-town background: https://legendsofkansas.com/everest-kansas/

Boehme

Most likely route currently supported by context, not by a found ship record:

  • Herman F. Boehme was born in Texas in 1863
  • his family therefore belongs to an earlier German-Texan immigrant generation
  • Shiner/Lavaca County and later Wilson County fit the South Texas German belt, not the original core Adelsverein Hill Country settlements

Context sources:

  • TSHA on Adelsverein: https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/adelsverein
  • TSHA on Shiner: https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/shiner-tx
  • TSHA on Wilson County: https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/wilson-county
  • TSHA on Sons of Hermann: https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/sons-of-hermann

Interpretation:

  • The Boehme line looks like an established Texas-born second-generation German-Texan line by Herman's 1863 birth.
  • The Lepik/Mikeska line looks like a later Bohemian immigrant line entering through Kansas.

---

Early Texas / German / Czech context

Boehme in South Texas

Useful context, not direct proof:

  • TSHA notes that Shiner became a town where Czech and German immigrants soon became the dominant ethnic groups after the railroad era began.
  • TSHA notes that Wilson County received German immigrants from Guadalupe County and Polish immigrants from Karnes County between 1850 and 1860.
  • The Sons of Hermann order was founded in San Antonio on July 6, 1861, and remained a major German fraternal institution in Texas. Herman's burial in a Sons of Hermann cemetery fits that ethnic setting, though it is not by itself proof of denomination.

Lepik / Mikeska in northeastern Kansas

Useful context, not direct proof:

  • Everest's Catholic church community predated the railroad boom and remained substantial after the town's 1882 expansion.
  • That setting is consistent with the 1885 Lepik-Mikeska marriage in the Brown County / Everest Catholic orbit.

---

Notable ancestors or relatives

Direct ancestors

I did not identify a clearly famous or nationally prominent direct ancestor in this cluster during this pass.

The "notability" here is mostly historical rather than celebrity:

  • Frank Lepik represents the documented Bohemian immigrant tradesman line.
  • Herman F. Boehme represents an early Texas-born German-Texan line in the Lavaca/Wilson corridor.

Notable collateral relative

One collateral relative is plainly notable in San Antonio civic life:

  • Fred Charles Lepick Jr. (1925-2016), Jennive's brother, became president of Frost National Bank and had a long legal and civic career.

Supporting sources:

  • Dignity obituary: https://www.dignitymemorial.com/obituaries/san-antonio-tx/fred-lepick-12178840
  • Texas Bar memorial snippet: https://www.texasbar.com/AM/Template.cfm?ContentID=34993&Section=articles&Template=%2FCM%2FHTMLDisplay.cfm

He is not a direct ancestor, but he is the most obviously notable verified person in this branch.

---

Corrections and cautions relative to earlier repo notes

1. Do not treat the 1880 census as proof of an immigration year.

The earlier repo file leaned too hard on that point for the Mikeskas. The safer statement is that Mary's 1900 census entry is the immigration-year evidence.

2. Do not treat PeopleLegacy as primary evidence.

It is useful here because the relationship clusters are coherent and match independent obituary facts, but the site explicitly disclaims verification.

3. Do not treat "buried in Sons of Hermann cemetery" as automatic proof of Lutheran confession.

It is excellent German-Texan context, but not enough by itself to settle religion.

4. No ship manifest has been found yet for Frank Lepik or the Mikeska arrival.

The immigration section should remain framed around census-reported arrival years until a passenger list is found.

---

Best next records to pull

1. Hilda Boehme Lepick death certificate

Expected payoff: confirms exact birth/death dates and parents.

2. 1900 census household for Minna / Boehme children in Texas

Expected payoff: confirms widowhood structure immediately after Herman's death.

3. Marriage record for Minna Macker / Boehme to John W. Luedecke

Expected payoff: locks the Boehme -> Luedecke blended-family structure.

4. Passenger list or naturalization for Frank Lepik

Expected payoff: town of origin in Bohemia.

5. Passenger list or naturalization for the Mikeska family

Expected payoff: identifies whether Mary's 1871 immigration can be tied firmly to Josef/Johanna.

6. Actual images for the Kansas obituary citations

Expected payoff: upgrades Josef/Johanna Mikeska from probable to confirmed.

---

Source list

Directly used in this pass

  • Jennive Mattingly obituary, The Gazette:

https://obits.gazette.com/us/obituaries/gazette/name/jennive-mattingly-obituary?id=24083135

  • Fred Charles Lepick obituary, Dignity Memorial:

https://www.dignitymemorial.com/obituaries/san-antonio-tx/fred-lepick-12178840

  • Hilda Boehme Lepick burial/relationship page, PeopleLegacy:

https://peoplelegacy.com/hilda_boehme_lepick-4n6E2q

  • Herman F. Boehme burial/relationship page, PeopleLegacy:

https://peoplelegacy.com/herman_f__boehme-4J1f4W

  • Minna Marie Macker Luedecke burial/relationship page, PeopleLegacy:

https://peoplelegacy.com/minna_marie_macker_luedecke-4f6E2q

  • TSHA, Shiner, TX:

https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/shiner-tx

  • TSHA, Wilson County:

https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/wilson-county

  • TSHA, Adelsverein:

https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/adelsverein

  • TSHA, Sons of Hermann:

https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/sons-of-hermann

  • Legends of Kansas, Everest, Kansas:

https://legendsofkansas.com/everest-kansas/

  • Kansas Historical Society, Brown County, Kansas:

https://www.kansashistory.gov/kansapedia/brown-county-kansas/15284

Stable record links carried forward from earlier repo research and retained here

  • 1930 U.S. census, Wilson County, Texas:

https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:33SQ-GRCQ-9WW

  • 1895 Kansas State Census, Brown County:

https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:3Q9M-CS3B-RSJ8-H

  • 1900 U.S. census, Brown County, Kansas:

https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:S3HT-6LD4-QVJ

  • 1910 U.S. census, Prairie County, Arkansas:

https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:33S7-9RVL-147

  • Brown County, Kansas marriage book (Frank Lepik / Mary Mikeska):

https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:939J-TCSZ-CM

  • 1880 U.S. census citation previously used for Mary Mikeska in Brown County research:

https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:33SQ-GYBH-T2X

  • Brown County Mission Township cemetery page:

https://usgenwebsites.org/KSGenWeb/archives/brown/cemeteries/Mission%20Twp%20Cemeteries.htm

  • Prior Kansas obituary citation for Josef Mikeska:

https://kansashistoricalopencontent.newspapers.com/article/the-kansas-democrat/195265760/

  • Prior Kansas obituary citation for Johana Mikeska:

https://kansashistoricalopencontent.newspapers.com/article/everest-enterprise-obituary-for-johana-m/195149300/

  • Prior burial corroborants used in repo research:

https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/55217353/fred_charles-lepick

https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/140131220/frank-lepick

https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/140131016

https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/140130956/josef-mikeska

---

Working conclusion

This branch now resolves into two distinct immigrant stories:

  • Lepik/Mikeska: late 19th-century Bohemian/Czech immigration into Brown County, Kansas, with Frank arriving in 1881 and Mary already in the United States by 1871.
  • Boehme: an earlier German-Texan South Texas line already rooted in Texas by Herman F. Boehme's 1863 birth, probably in the Shiner/Lavaca County German-Czech belt.

The most important unresolved document gap is the lack of a primary record for Hilda's parents. The best current working answer is:

Hilda Boehme Lepick (1900-1945), daughter of Herman F. Boehme and probably Minna Marie Macker, later Luedecke.

That is the strongest reading of the evidence now on hand, but it still needs one more primary record to become final.

45 Teichmueller / Kellner / Mollenhauer / Girsewald Expanded Research 11 KB Research date: 2026-04-26

Teichmueller / Kellner / Mollenhauer / Girsewald Expanded Research

Research date: 2026-04-26

Scope: Verify deceased ancestors, notable collateral branches, and source-backed German/Texas records for the Teichmueller / Teichmüller, Kellner, Mollenhauer, and Girsewald lines.

Method: This pass keeps only what I could tie to accessible institutional or published sources. I separate confirmed facts from narrower inferences.

---

Executive Summary

1. Hans Teichmueller (1837-1901) and Gustav Teichmüller (1832-1888) are confirmed brothers. The strongest source is the 2016 Neue Deutsche Biographie article for Gustav, which explicitly names Hans, Gustav, Wilhelm, and Wilhelmina (Minette) as siblings and identifies Hans's wife as Auguste Kellner.[1]

2. The Teichmüller patriline can be pushed back, with source support, to the late 1500s. The same NDB article states that the family is traceable to Hans / Johann Teichmüller (ca. 1580-1638), a master miller in the southern Harz, and then names later ancestors Joachim Andreas Teichmüller (1705-1778), Wilhelm Ernst Conrad Teichmüller (1758-1835), and August Wilhelm Teichmüller (1795-1855).[1][2][3][4]

3. The Girsewald connection is also confirmed one generation further back than earlier repo notes suggested. Charlotte Georgine Elisabeth, mother of Hans and Gustav, is identified by NDB as the daughter of Georg von Girsewald (1735-1816) and Christine Elisabeth Boyer.[1][5]

4. The Texas branch is well anchored. TSHA confirms Hans's birth in Braunschweig on March 7, 1837, his migration to Texas after his father's death, his marriage to Augusta Kellner in Fayette County, his five children, his judicial career, and his death at La Grange on February 17, 1901.[6]

5. The Kellner line is only partly expanded in this pass. Augusta/Auguste Kellner is securely documented as Hans's wife, and her father is described by TSHA as a modern-language teacher who taught Hans English. I did not find an accessible institutional source in this pass naming that father or extending the Kellner line further.[6][7]

---

Confirmed Direct and Near-Direct Ancestors

| Person | Dates | Status | What is confirmed |

|---|---|---|---|

| Hans / Johann Teichmüller | ca. 1580-1638 | Confirmed by NDB genealogy | Earliest documented ancestor named in the Teichmüller patriline; master miller in the southern Harz.[1] |

| Joachim Andreas Teichmüller | 1705-1778 | Confirmed | Named by NDB as Gustav's great-grandfather line; separately indexed by Deutsche Biographie as an Oberfaktor in Goslar.[1][4] |

| Wilhelm Ernst Conrad Teichmüller | 1758-1835 | Confirmed | Named by NDB as father of August Wilhelm; Deutsche Biographie indexes him as Oberhütteninspektor / mining official.[1][3] |

| August Wilhelm Teichmüller | 1795-1855 | Confirmed | Father of Gustav and Hans; Brunswick officer, specifically Seconde-Lieutenant in the Schwarzen Corps des Majors Olfermann.[1][2] |

| Charlotte Georgine Elisabeth (von Girsewald) Teichmüller | 1799-1860 | Confirmed | Mother of Gustav and Hans; daughter of Georg von Girsewald and Christine Elisabeth Boyer.[1] |

| Georg von Girsewald | 1735-1816 | Confirmed | Maternal grandfather of Gustav and Hans; Deutsche Biographie indexes him as Oberstallmeister, Hauptmann, and officer.[1][5] |

| Hans Teichmueller | 1837-1901 | Confirmed | Born Braunschweig; migrated to Texas; lawyer, county judge, then judge of the 22nd Judicial District; died La Grange, Texas.[1][6] |

Confirmed Spouse Line

| Person | Dates | Status | What is confirmed |

|---|---|---|---|

| Augusta / Auguste Kellner | not fully resolved in this pass | Confirmed as spouse | NDB names Hans's wife as Auguste Kellner; TSHA says Hans married Augusta Kellner in Fayette County in 1858. TSHA further says her father was a modern-language teacher who taught Hans English.[1][6] |

Important Kellner limitation: I did not find a reliable, accessible record in this pass that names Augusta Kellner's father, mother, birth date, or place of birth beyond TSHA's brief description.[6][7]

---

Confirmed Siblings and Notable Collateral Branches

1. Gustav Teichmüller branch

Gustav Teichmüller (1832-1888) is not a speculative match anymore. NDB explicitly places him as Hans's brother and gives the same parents. He is the strongest "notable branch" in the German line: philosopher, historian of philosophy, and professor at Dorpat/Tartu.[1]

NDB also identifies Gustav's daughter Anna Teichmüller (1861-1940) as a composer, pianist, and music teacher, which creates a documented artistic branch off the same sibling set.[1]

2. Wilhelmina (Minette) Teichmüller / Mollenhauer branch

NDB explicitly identifies Wilhelmina (Minette) Teichmüller (1829-1886) as Hans's sister and states that she married Karl Mollenhauer (1818-1864), described there as Pastor and Superintendent in Bockenem, Ambergau.[1]

This is enough to confirm a real Mollenhauer ecclesiastical branch attached to the family, even though I did not extend that branch to later descendants in this pass.

3. Wilhelm Teichmüller branch

NDB identifies another brother, Wilhelm Teichmüller (1834-1869), as Premier-Lieutenant and writer. That gives the family a second documented German collateral line outside Texas.[1]

4. Texas art branch: Minette Teichmueller / Pohl

The Smithville Post Office mural record is straightforward: the New Deal Art Registry identifies "The Law--Texas Rangers" as a 1940 oil-on-canvas mural by Minette Teichmueller, created under the Treasury Section of Fine Arts.[8][9]

What I can say with high confidence:

  • A documented Texas artist named Minette Teichmueller painted the Smithville federal mural.[8][9]
  • Hans and Augusta had five children according to TSHA.[6]

What remains slightly weaker in this pass:

  • Secondary art-market biographies identify the muralist as the daughter of Judge Hans Teichmueller and Augusta Kellner.[10]
  • I did not, in this pass, pull a page-image or institutional vital record that independently proves that parent-child link.

So the right wording here is: the muralist Minette Teichmueller is very likely the same family member long associated with the Hans Teichmueller branch, but the parent-child link should still be tightened with a direct census, cemetery, or vital record before being treated as closed proof.[6][8][10]

---

Best Source-Backed Texas Record Set Located

A. TSHA Handbook entry for Hans Teichmueller

This is the cleanest Texas summary source currently accessible online. It gives:

  • Hans's exact birth date: March 7, 1837
  • birthplace: Braunschweig, Germany
  • migration context after his father's death
  • marriage to Augusta Kellner in 1858
  • note that her father taught Hans English
  • statement that the couple had five children
  • legal and judicial career in Fayette County
  • death in La Grange on February 17, 1901[6]

B. 1908 memorial volume prepared by Auguste Kellner Teichmueller

UNT's Portal to Texas History has a digitized memorial volume titled _Hans Teichmueller; biographical sketch, addresses and letters to his family_. The metadata credits Teichmueller, Auguste Kellner as creator and says it was prepared by his "loving wife and daughters." This is a strong Texas family record and a direct link back to the Kellner spouse line.[7]

C. 1902 Fayette County history

The 1902 county history _Fayette County, Her History and Her People_ is online through Internet Archive / Library of Congress metadata and is also cited by TSHA in its bibliography for Hans. I am listing it as a priority primary source for further page-level extraction, but I am not over-claiming details from it here unless independently confirmed above.[6][11]

D. New Deal / federal art record for Minette

The New Deal Art Registry entry for the Smithville Post Office is the best clean institutional confirmation for the Texas art branch: title, medium, year, program, location, and artist name are all explicit.[8][9]

---

Best Source-Backed German Record Set Located

The backbone for the German side is Heiner Schwenke's 2016 NDB article on Gustav Teichmüller in Deutsche Biographie. It is doing a lot of the heavy lifting because it supplies, in one scholarly genealogy:

  • the late-1500s Harz origin of the family
  • four generations of named Teichmüller ancestors before Hans
  • Hans and Gustav as brothers
  • Charlotte's maiden line to Georg von Girsewald and Christine Elisabeth Boyer
  • the sibling Wilhelmina (Minette) and her husband Karl Mollenhauer
  • Gustav's daughter Anna Teichmüller as a notable musical descendant[1]

Deutsche Biographie also provides separate authority/index pages for August Wilhelm Teichmüller, Wilhelm Ernst Conrad Teichmüller, Joachim Andreas Teichmüller, and Georg von Girsewald, which helps stabilize names, dates, and occupations for downstream use.[2][3][4][5]

---

What Is Still Open

1. Kellner parentage: I have not yet identified Augusta Kellner's father by name, only by profession via TSHA.[6]

2. Direct proof that muralist Minette is Hans's daughter: highly plausible and supported by secondary art biographies, but still worth locking down with a census, probate, cemetery, or death record.[10]

3. Mollenhauer descendants: Karl Mollenhauer's role is confirmed, but the branch is not extended here.[1]

4. Further Girsewald expansion: Georg von Girsewald is now confirmed. The next step would be archival or published noble genealogy work on the Girsewald/Boyer line rather than broad web searching.[1][5]

---

Sources

1. Heiner Schwenke, "Teichmüller, Gustav," Neue Deutsche Biographie 26 (2016), Deutsche Biographie: https://www.deutsche-biographie.de/gnd118801554.html

2. Deutsche Biographie index entry, "Teichmüller, August Wilhelm": https://www.deutsche-biographie.de/sfz131010.html

3. Deutsche Biographie index entry, "Teichmüller, Wilhelm Ernst Conrad": https://www.deutsche-biographie.de/sfz131011.html

4. Deutsche Biographie index entry, "Teichmüller, Joachim Andreas": https://www.deutsche-biographie.de/sfz131013.html

5. Deutsche Biographie index entry, "Girsewald, Georg von": https://www.deutsche-biographie.de/sfz131012.html

6. Rudolph L. Biesele, "Teichmueller, Hans," Handbook of Texas Online (TSHA): https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/teichmueller-hans

7. Auguste Kellner Teichmueller, Hans Teichmueller; biographical sketch, addresses and letters to his family (1908), Portal to Texas History: https://texashistory.unt.edu/ark:/67531/metapth29783/

8. New Deal Art Registry, "Smithville Post Office" / "The Law--Texas Rangers": https://newdealartregistry.org/map/SmithvillePostOffice/Smithville/TX/

9. New Deal Art Registry, artist page for Minette Teichmueller: https://newdealartregistry.org/artists/Teichmuellerminette/

10. Secondary artist biography identifying Minette as daughter of Hans and Augusta: https://www.vintagetexaspaintings.com/artists/287-minette-teichmueller-mrs-hugo-pohl-paintings

11. Fayette County, Her History and Her People metadata / scan entry: https://openlibrary.org/books/OL6920491M/Fayette_County_her_history_and_her_people

46 Baity / Pearl Baity / North Carolina-to-Texas Colonial Side 14 KB Prepared: 2026-04-26

Baity / Pearl Baity / North Carolina-to-Texas Colonial Side

Prepared: 2026-04-26

Researcher: Codex parallel worker

Scope: direct Baity-side research only; no updates to entities.json or build artifacts

Confidence tiers used here: CONFIRMED = directly supported by a cited record in this pass; PROBABLE = strong fit but one generational link still needs a census/probate/deed image; POSSIBLE = plausible line, not yet safe to treat as proved

---

Executive Summary

The defensible Baity line, using records I could actually put under citation in this pass, runs most safely as:

1. Paralee "Pearl" Baity of San Antonio, Texas (CONFIRMED as a married Baity woman in San Antonio by 1910)

2. wife of W. A. Baity (CONFIRMED as her husband in Bexar County land records)

3. William Alexander Baity of Yadkin County, North Carolina (PROBABLE identification from prior repo work; this pass independently re-found the Texas land traces but not the vital-record image)

4. probable son of William D. Baity (1829/1830-1894, Courtney Baptist Cemetery, Yadkin County) (PROBABLE)

5. probable son of Isham "Isom" Baity (19 Oct 1804-23 Dec 1892, Courtney Baptist Cemetery) (PROBABLE)

6. probable/possible son of David Baity (adult in Surry County by 1830) (POSSIBLE/PROBABLE)

7. probable/possible son of George Baity / Batee / Baty (adult in Rowan County by May 6, 1774) (POSSIBLE)

Two bottom lines matter:

  • I did not find a verifiable immigrant arrival for the direct Baity line.
  • The earliest defensible Baity presence is already colonial North Carolina, with George Baity/Batee/Baty appearing in Rowan/Surry records from 1774 forward.

That means the "colonial side" is real, but the pre-North-Carolina jump is still unproved.

---

1. Pearl / Paralee Baity in Texas

What is solid

  • Shari's transcript says her great-grandmother Pearl Baity came from North Carolina to Texas as a child, later bought Reeves County land in April 1901, and retained the mineral rights when she sold the surface.

Source: transcripts/final.md

  • A Bexar County archival description confirms that "W. A. Baity and Paralee Baity" conveyed San Antonio land on November 2, 1910.

Source: East Texas Research Center, Stephen F. Austin State University, "Deed: W. A. Baity and Paralee Baity to W. F. Daniel, E. D. Wintz and J. T. Gallant, Bexar County, Texas, land in San Antonio, November 2, 1910"

https://archives.sfasu.edu/repositories/2/archival_objects/19330

  • Portal to Texas History search snippets show the same couple in Bexar County real-estate notices in 1910 and 1925, which independently supports that the Baitys were active property holders in San Antonio:

- San Antonio Light and Gazette, May 30, 1910, page 8

https://texashistory.unt.edu/ark%3A/67531/metapth1692586/m1/8/

- San Antonio Light, October 16, 1925, page 33

https://texashistory.unt.edu/ark%3A/67531/metapth1593025/m1/33/

What remains weaker than I want

  • Prior repo work identifies "Pearl" as Paralee Johnson Baity and identifies her husband as William Alexander Baity. I did not independently re-pull the Grayson County marriage record or the Texas death certificate image in this pass, so I am treating that exact identification as PROBABLE, not CONFIRMED, here.
  • A low-grade modern death-index aggregator gives Paralee Baity, born 26 Jul 1878, died Dec 1969, San Antonio. That is directionally useful but not strong enough to stand alone.

Source: AncientFaces surname directory snippet

https://www.ancientfaces.com/surname/baity-family-history/410608

Reeves County / mineral-rights story

  • The oral-history claim is clear and consistent inside the transcript.
  • The geology claim is true: the Wolfcamp/Spraberry-Wolfcamp play in Reeves County became one of the major modern oil targets, and the USGS 2016 assessment was the headline event behind the "largest assessment" language.

Source: USGS Fact Sheet 2016-3092

https://pubs.usgs.gov/fs/2016/3092/fs20163092.pdf

I did not recover the original April 1901 Reeves County purchase instrument in this pass. I would treat the land/mineral story as credible oral history with modern geological corroboration, but the original purchase deed or GLO file still needs to be pulled before treating it as fully proved.

---

2. The Best-Supported North Carolina Line

A. William D. Baity (1829/1830-1894)

Status: PROBABLE direct-line ancestor

Why he matters:

  • Prior project work already pointed to William D. Baity as the father-generation immediately above William Alexander.
  • A cemetery transcription places William D. Baity at Courtney Baptist Church Cemetery, Yadkin County, with dates 1830-1894.

Source: Yadkin County cemetery census

https://www.cemeterycensus.org/nc/yadk/cem049.htm

  • A burial-index aggregation independently echoes William D Baity (1829-1894) at the same cemetery.

Source: PeopleLegacy burial index

https://peoplelegacy.com/burial-records/william/baity/

Assessment:

  • I am comfortable saying a real deceased William D. Baity existed in the right place and time.
  • I am not comfortable calling the William-D-to-William-Alexander parent-child link CONFIRMED until the 1870/1880 census or a probate/deed record is cited directly.

B. Isham "Isom" Baity (19 Oct 1804-23 Dec 1892)

Status: PROBABLE direct-line ancestor

Why he matters:

  • Courtney Baptist Church Cemetery lists Isham "Isom" Baity with exact dates 19 Oct 1804 - 23 Dec 1892.

Source: cemetery census

https://www.cemeterycensus.org/nc/yadk/cem049.htm

  • A separate cemetery-census snippet identifies Mary Jane Baity as a daughter of Isham Baity and Nancy Plowman, which is useful evidence that the Isham/Nancy household is a real documented family unit in this neighborhood.

Source: Deep Creek Baptist Church cemetery snippet

https://www.cemeterycensus.org/nc/yadk/cem026.htm

Assessment:

  • Isham is a real, well-placed Yadkin/Surry-era Baity ancestor.
  • The step from William D. Baity to Isham Baity still needs a directly cited census or estate record, but it fits the local cemetery cluster and the prior project's candidate line well enough to call PROBABLE.

C. David Baity (adult by 1830)

Status: POSSIBLE/PROBABLE

Why he matters:

  • The 1830 Surry County census includes separate heads of household named David Baity, George Baity, Isam Baity, and Pleasant Baity.

Source: NCGenWeb transcription of the 1830 census

https://www.ncgenweb.us/alleghany/surry1830.html

Assessment:

  • This is the strongest public clue that the family cluster was already in Surry County by 1830.
  • It does not, by itself, prove that David was Isham's father, but it makes the older-project candidate line plausible.
  • I would keep David -> Isham at POSSIBLE/PROBABLE until a deed, estate, or 1850 census household link is cited directly.

D. George Baity / Batee / Baty (adult by 1774)

Status: POSSIBLE

Why he matters:

  • A public Ancestry-sharing page reproduces a useful summary of earlier Rowan/Surry references for George Baity / Batee / Baty:

- May 6, 1774: "George Beate vs. Hugh Montgomery" in Rowan County Inferior Court

- April 2, 1785: bond from Sylvester and John Thomson to sell George Batee 124 acres on Dutchman's Creek

- 1787: George Batey paying Rowan County taxes

- January 19, 1798: Thomas Horn to George Baty, 300 acres on Chiquapin Creek near the Rowan line

- 1799-1800: David and William Batey taxed in the same district

- 1800: George Beaty in the census

Source: Ancestry-shared story snippet for George C. Baity

https://www.ancestry.com/sharing/21081190?h=af0454

Assessment:

  • I would not treat the above as fully proved until those entries are rechecked in the original Rowan/Surry court, tax, deed, and census books.
  • But it is enough to say the Baity line is documented in colonial/revolutionary-era North Carolina by 1774.

---

3. Military and Civic Records Near the Direct Line

These are not all direct ancestors, but they are clearly part of the same Yadkin Baity network and are useful context.

Confederate service

  • Pleasant H. Baity, resident of Yadkin County, enlisted as a private in Company I, 28th North Carolina Infantry on March 8, 1862, age 18.
  • William W. Baity, also in Company I, enlisted on August 13, 1861 and died of disease on December 8, 1862 at Winchester, Virginia; burial is given as Stonewall Confederate Cemetery, grave 328.

Source: MilitaryHistoryOnline genealogy transcription

https://www.militaryhistoryonline.com/Genealogy/Regiment/North%20Carolina/4/2389

  • The official North Carolina Troops Roster Index also lists Baity soldiers including I. H. Baity, Pleasant H. Baity, and William D., Sr.

Source: North Carolina Department of Natural and Cultural Resources roster index PDF

https://www.dncr.nc.gov/documents/files/civil-war-roster-may-2020/download

Assessment:

  • The Yadkin Baity family clearly had Civil War service.
  • For direct-line purposes, this is strongest as network evidence around Isham/William-D-era Baitys.

Land / property records in San Antonio

  • W. A. and Paralee Baity appear in Bexar County land transactions in 1910 and 1925.
  • That fits the oral history that this branch became prosperous in San Antonio real estate/building circles, even if I did not independently recover a contractor-license or business record in this pass.

---

4. Colonial-Side Conclusion

What I would state now

  • The Baity branch is solidly North Carolina before 1800.
  • The earliest defensible ancestor in this pass is George Baity/Batee/Baty, active in Rowan County records by May 6, 1774.
  • The line George -> David -> Isham -> William D. -> William Alexander -> W.A./Paralee Texas branch is plausible and probably right, but only the lower Texas couple and the cemetery-anchored North Carolina generations are near-documentary strength right now.

What I would *not* state yet

  • I would not claim a specific immigrant ancestor.
  • I would not claim George Baity's parents.
  • I would not claim Rachel Allgood as a proved wife of George for direct-line purposes without a better marriage, deed, probate, or child-link record.

---

5. Best Follow-Up Targets

If this branch gets another research pass, these are the highest-value next documents:

1. 1900 and 1910 census images for William Alexander / W. A. Baity and Paralee in Texas.

Goal: lock the Texas branch to the Yadkin-born William Alexander without relying on prior repo notes.

2. Texas death certificate image for William Alexander Baity.

Goal: confirm birth date/place and parents if named.

3. Reeves County deed or Texas GLO purchase file for the claimed April 1901 land purchase.

Goal: turn the mineral-rights story from oral-history-plus-context into a primary-record event.

4. 1850/1860/1870 census or probate/deed records linking:

- William D. Baity -> Isham Baity

- Isham Baity -> David Baity

5. Original Rowan/Surry court, tax, and deed books for the George Baity entries summarized in the Ancestry snippet.

Starting point: NC Archives guide for Surry County materials

https://archives.ncdcr.gov/guide-research-materials-north-carolina-state-archives-surry-county-0

---

Source List

1. Shari interview transcript, oral-history evidence for Pearl Baity's NC-to-TX move and Reeves County land story: transcripts/final.md

2. East Texas Research Center, SFA: W. A. Baity and Paralee Baity deed, Nov. 2, 1910

https://archives.sfasu.edu/repositories/2/archival_objects/19330

3. Portal to Texas History, San Antonio Light and Gazette, May 30, 1910

https://texashistory.unt.edu/ark%3A/67531/metapth1692586/m1/8/

4. Portal to Texas History, San Antonio Light, Oct. 16, 1925

https://texashistory.unt.edu/ark%3A/67531/metapth1593025/m1/33/

5. USGS Fact Sheet 2016-3092, Wolfcamp assessment context

https://pubs.usgs.gov/fs/2016/3092/fs20163092.pdf

6. Yadkin County cemetery census, Courtney Baptist Church Cemetery

https://www.cemeterycensus.org/nc/yadk/cem049.htm

7. Yadkin County cemetery census, Deep Creek Baptist Church Cemetery

https://www.cemeterycensus.org/nc/yadk/cem026.htm

8. NCGenWeb 1830 Surry County census transcription

https://www.ncgenweb.us/alleghany/surry1830.html

9. MilitaryHistoryOnline, 28th North Carolina Infantry genealogy page

https://www.militaryhistoryonline.com/Genealogy/Regiment/North%20Carolina/4/2389

10. North Carolina Troops Roster Index (official PDF)

https://www.dncr.nc.gov/documents/files/civil-war-roster-may-2020/download

11. Ancestry-shared George C. Baity story snippet summarizing Rowan/Surry record references; useful lead, but should still be checked against originals

https://www.ancestry.com/sharing/21081190?h=af0454

12. PeopleLegacy burial-index echo for William D. Baity

https://peoplelegacy.com/burial-records/william/baity/

13. AncientFaces directory snippet for Paralee Baity death-month/year lead

https://www.ancientfaces.com/surname/baity-family-history/410608

---

Bottom Line

The Baity branch is genuinely colonial-era North Carolina, but the file should currently stop at George Baity (adult by 1774) unless someone rechecks the Rowan/Surry originals and rebuilds the chain upward. The Texas end of the line is real and documentable, but Pearl/Paralee's 1901 Reeves purchase still needs its actual deed or GLO file.

47 Frost / Herff / Chabot / San Antonio Notable Maternal-Side Research 8 KB Compiled: 2026-04-26

Frost / Herff / Chabot / San Antonio Notable Maternal-Side Research

Compiled: 2026-04-26

Status: Focused collateral research. This memo documents the Frost/Herff/Kampmann San Antonio network and separates it from Shari's confirmed maternal line.

Bottom Line

Shari Mattingly's documented mother was Jennive Imogene Lepick, not a Frost or Herff. Jennive's obituary names her as the mother of Sharyn Mattingly and gives her maiden name and parents. That means no source-backed Frost/Herff descent is currently established for Shari's maternal line. [1]

What is source-backed is a real San Antonio notable-family cluster:

  • Thomas Claiborne Frost (1833-1903) founded what became Frost National Bank. [2]
  • Ferdinand Ludwig Herff (1820-1912) was an early German-Texan physician who reached Texas in 1847 and moved to San Antonio in 1850. [3]
  • John Herman Kampmann (1819-1885) reached San Antonio in 1848 and became one of its major 19th-century builders and businessmen. [4]
  • Florence Ilse Herff Frost (1906-1984) belonged to the Herff/Kampmann network and was the mother of Tom Frost Jr., the fourth-generation Frost Bank leader. [5][6][7][8]

So the Frost/Herff story is historically real, but it is not the confirmed Lepick maternal line.

Confirmed: Shari's Maternal Line Is Lepick, Not Frost

  • Jennive Imogene Lepick Mattingly's published obituary states that she was born on February 2, 1923, in Floresville, Texas, to Frederick and Hilda (Boehme) Lepick.
  • The same obituary names her surviving children, including Sharyn Mattingly. [1]

This is the cleanest source-backed reason the older Frost-mother hypothesis should stay rejected.

Confirmed: The Frost / Herff / Kampmann San Antonio Network

1. Frost bank founder

Thomas Claiborne Frost was born in Alabama on December 31, 1833, arrived in Texas in 1854, later settled in San Antonio, and founded the banking enterprise that became Frost National Bank. TSHA states that after his death in 1903, the presidency passed first to his son Thomas C. Frost and then to another son, Joseph H. Frost. [2]

2. Early Herff arrival in Texas

Ferdinand Ludwig von Herff was born in Darmstadt, Germany, on November 29, 1820. TSHA states that he helped organize the Bettina colony in Texas in 1847, returned to Germany, then came back to Texas permanently, settling first in New Braunfels and then moving to San Antonio in 1850. TSHA also credits him as a pioneer physician and says he was instrumental in establishing San Antonio's first hospital. [3]

3. Early Kampmann arrival in San Antonio

John Herman Kampmann was born in Prussia on December 25, 1819, and TSHA states that he left Europe in 1848 and settled in San Antonio. He became a major builder, contractor, hotel owner, and businessman; TSHA specifically notes his work on the Menger Hotel and his role in San Antonio civic development. [4]

4. Kampmann daughter Eda and the Herff branch

TSHA identifies Eda as one of John Herman Kampmann's children. [4]

The descendant-published family-history book Eda and Ilse identifies Eda Kampmann Herff as Kampmann's daughter and states that her granddaughter was Ilse Herff Frost. It further states that Ilse was the daughter of Eda's son "Johnny" / Dr. John Herff. [6]

Judith Carrington's San Antonio Report essay, written from the same family archive behind Eda and Ilse, likewise identifies Eda Kampmann Herff as John Kampmann's daughter and describes the Ilse memoir section as written by Kampmann's granddaughter. [7]

5. John Bennett Herff and Florence Ilse Herff

John Benedict's local-history article on Caleb Thomas's log cabin states that Dr. John Bennett Herff (1878-1959) was the son of Dr. John Adolph Herff and the grandson of Dr. Ferdinand Ludwig Herff. It also states that John B. Herff married Florence Harris in 1902 and that their children were:

  • Carol (1904-1922)
  • Florence Ilse (1906-1984)
  • Jean (1910-1996) [5]

That gives a source-backed placement for Florence Ilse Herff inside the Herff line.

6. Florence Ilse Herff Frost and Tom Frost Jr.

In Rivers, Ranches, Railroads, & Recreation, Ilse Herff Frost wrote that:

  • her father was Dr. John Herff
  • the ranch came down from Dr. Ferdinand Ludwig von Herff
  • her husband's grandfather, Col. T. C. Frost, founded Frost National Bank
  • her son Tom was chief executive of the Frost banking family in San Antonio. [8]

Tom Frost Jr.'s obituary independently states that he was born on October 29, 1927, in San Antonio to Thomas Clayborne Frost and Ilsé Herff Frost, and that he was the fourth generation to lead Frost Bank. [9]

Taken together, the accessible sources support this San Antonio notable-family chain:

1. John Herman Kampmann (arrived San Antonio 1848) [4]

2. his daughter Eda Kampmann Herff [4][6][7]

3. her son Dr. John Herff / John Bennett Herff as preserved in family-history and local-history sources [5][6]

4. Florence Ilse Herff (1906-1984) [5]

5. Tom Frost Jr. (1927-2018), Frost Bank leader [8][9]

Chabot: Useful Period Source, Not a Proven Family Link

Frederick Charles Chabot (1891-1943) was a San Antonio historian. TSHA identifies his most notable work as _With the Makers of San Antonio_ (1937). [10]

Google Books confirms that With the Makers of San Antonio is a 412-page genealogy and biography compilation of early San Antonio families, published as Yanaguana Society Publication 4. [11]

That makes Chabot a legitimate period source for this cluster. But in this pass I did not use Chabot's book to prove any exact Frost/Herff kinship, because the openly accessible scan did not give me page-level Herff/Frost passages to quote or verify directly.

Probable, But Not Yet Source-Backed Enough To Assert As Fact

  • That Shari's oral memory about a Frost Bank relative came from a real Frost/Herff/Kampmann social-family circle around old San Antonio, rather than from her actual maternal ancestry.
  • The exact documentary step from Eda Kampmann Herff to John Bennett Herff is well supported by descendant/family-history material in this pass, but I did not independently verify it with a civil record, probate file, or cemetery record here.
  • Any blood or marriage link from Jennive Lepick into the Frost/Herff family remains unproven.

Practical Takeaway For The Project

For this genealogy site, the safest treatment is:

  • keep Jennive Lepick as Shari's confirmed mother
  • treat the Frost/Herff/Kampmann material as an investigated San Antonio notable-family cluster
  • only connect Shari to that cluster if a new source appears that explicitly bridges Lepick/Mattingly to Frost/Herff

Sources

1. Jennive Mattingly obituary, The Gazette / Legacy: https://obits.gazette.com/us/obituaries/gazette/name/jennive-mattingly-obituary?id=24083135

2. TSHA, "Frost, Thomas Claiborne": https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/frost-thomas-claiborne

3. TSHA, "Herff, Ferdinand Ludwig": https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/herff-ferdinand-ludwig

4. TSHA, "Kampmann, John Herman": https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/kampmann-john-herman

5. John Benedict, "Caleb's Log Cabin," Echoes…..for the Archives, Boerne Public Library, 2019 PDF: https://hcarcheology.org/wp-content/uploads/Fieldwork/CurrentFieldwork/Caleb-Cabin-Echoes-Spr2019.pdf

6. Eda and Ilse book page, Xlibris: https://www.xlibris.com/BookStore/BookDetails/599924-eda-and-ilse

7. Judith Carrington, "Recovering Kampmann: Lineage Discovery Connects Dots to NYC," San Antonio Report, June 16, 2014: https://sanantonioreport.org/discovery-treasure-san-antonio-history-new-york-city/

8. Rivers, Ranches, Railroads, & Recreation, Portal to Texas History, p. 98: https://texashistory.unt.edu/ark:/67531/metapth836134/m1/102/

9. Thomas Clayborne Frost obituary, San Antonio Express-News / Legacy: https://www.legacy.com/us/obituaries/sanantonio/name/thomas-frost-obituary?id=7111942

10. TSHA, "Chabot, Frederick Charles": https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/chabot-frederick-charles

11. Frederick Charles Chabot, With the Makers of San Antonio, Google Books bibliographic record: https://books.google.com/books/about/With_the_Makers_of_San_Antonio.html?id=tUcLAAAAYAAJ

48 Dovie (?) Spence / Dale William Spence Sr. Deep Research 7 KB Prepared: 2026-04-26

Dovie (?) Spence / Dale William Spence Sr. Deep Research

Prepared: 2026-04-26

Confidence scale:

  • HIGH: directly supported by a cited public source page
  • MEDIUM: strong inference from multiple public sources, but no single record yet states the whole claim
  • LOW: context or hypothesis only

Scope

This memo is limited to the unresolved Dovie (?) Spence problem and the public-record footprint of Dr. Dale William Spence Sr. It avoids non-public personal data for living people.

Bottom-line findings

1. Dr. Dale William Spence Sr. is firmly established in public records as the Rice / Beaumont / Houston figure described in family context. Public sources show him as a Beaumont High athlete by 1952, a Rice undergraduate from 1952 to 1956, a Marine officer, an LSU doctorate recipient in 1966, a Rice faculty member, and later a Rice professor emeritus. Confidence: HIGH.

2. The currently available public sources in this pass do not identify Dale William Spence Sr.'s parents. I did not find a source-backed public record that names his mother as Dovie, gives Dovie's maiden name, or identifies Dale Sr.'s father. Confidence: HIGH for the negative finding.

3. The repo placeholder Dovie (?) Spence should remain marked unresolved. At this point, "Dovie" is still a family-memory lead, not a documented identity. Confidence: HIGH.

4. Public evidence places Dale Sr. in Beaumont, Texas by 1952 at the latest. That does not disprove an England birth or childhood migration, but it does mean the public record currently supports a Texas youth trajectory more strongly than an adult academic immigration story. Confidence: HIGH for Beaumont by 1952; MEDIUM for the interpretation.

5. The strongest confirmed deceased ancestor in this immediate branch remains Alice Marie Henslee Spence. Public burial listings also support Lee S. Henslee as a deceased older generation anchor in Beaumont. These help fix the family in place geographically, but they do not solve Dovie. Confidence: HIGH for Alice; HIGH for Lee's burial record.

Confirmed public record findings

Dr. Dale William Spence Sr.

  • Rice Athletics states that Dr. Dale Spence won the 1952 Texas state title in the 880 while competing for Beaumont High School, ran for Rice from 1952-1956, later served in the Marine Corps, earned graduate degrees at North Texas and LSU, completed a Baylor postdoc, and was on the Rice faculty from 1963 to 2003.

- Confidence: HIGH

- Source: https://riceowls.com/news/2019/10/11/mens-track-field-pr-club-to-honor-spence-stadel-and-1995-championship-team

  • A Beaumont High School yearbook page for 1952 names Dale Spence among the track standouts and reports that he won a state championship that season.

- Confidence: HIGH

- Source: https://www.e-yearbook.com/yearbooks/Beaumont_High_School_Pine_Burr_Yearbook/1952/Page_234.html

  • A 2020 Rice History Corner comment posted under the name Dale Spence, Colonel, USMCR (Ret) says his Rice undergraduate years were 1952-1956 and ties him directly to the Marine Corps commissioning cohort from that class period.

- Confidence: HIGH

- Source: https://ricehistorycorner.com/2020/05/07/semper-fidelis-date-unknown/

  • LSU's dissertation repository gives the full name Dale William Spence and shows that he received an EdD in 1966.

- Confidence: HIGH

- Source: https://repository.lsu.edu/gradschool_disstheses/1171/

  • PubMed records a 1990 paper by D.W. Spence with affiliation to the Department of Human Performance and Health Sciences, Rice University, Houston, TX 77251-1892.

- Confidence: HIGH

- Source: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/2375667/

  • Rice News in 2019 refers to Dale Spence, professor emeritus of kinesiology.

- Confidence: HIGH

- Source: https://news2.rice.edu/2019/11/11/honoring-our-veterans/

What these sources imply about Dale Sr.

  • Because Dale Spence was already a Beaumont High state champion in 1952 and later described himself as a Rice undergraduate in 1952-1956, his birth year was likely around the mid-1930s.

- Confidence: MEDIUM

- Basis: standard high-school and college-age inference from the dated public sources above

  • Those same sources show that Dale Sr. was in southeast Texas as a teenager. They do not identify his parents, birthplace, or any immigration record.

- Confidence: HIGH

Confirmed deceased ancestors in this immediate branch

Alice Marie Henslee Spence

  • Public burial index entry:

- 1936-2005

- birth place: Rusk, Texas

- burial place: Forest Lawn Memorial Park and Funeral Home, Beaumont, Texas

  • Why it matters here:

- She is the strongest verified deceased Spence-relative anchor in the Beaumont branch.

  • Confidence: HIGH
  • Source: https://peoplelegacy.com/burial-records/alice/spence/

Lee S. Henslee

  • Public burial index entry:

- 1908-1994

- burial place: Forest Lawn Memorial Park and Funeral Home, Beaumont, Texas

  • Why it matters here:

- He is a confirmed deceased older-generation Beaumont anchor immediately adjacent to the Spence branch through Alice Marie Henslee Spence.

  • Confidence: HIGH for the burial record itself
  • Source: https://peoplelegacy.com/burial-records/lee/henslee/

Dovie (?) Spence status

What I did not find

  • No obituary, memorial, dissertation vita, Rice profile, or public article in this pass naming Dale William Spence Sr.'s mother.
  • No source-backed public record tying the given name Dovie to Dale William Spence Sr.
  • No public record establishing Dovie's maiden name.
  • No confirmed deceased older bloodline Spence ancestor above Dale Sr. from this pass.

Working conclusion

Dovie (?) Spence remains an unresolved placeholder and should not be upgraded to a confirmed person without a stronger source such as:

1. a death notice or obituary for Dale Sr.

2. a birth record or delayed birth record for Dale Sr.

3. a census entry showing Dale in a parental household

4. a marriage record naming his parents

5. a military or alumni biographical form that gives next of kin / parents

Confidence: HIGH

Possible surname context

No new surname-context material is added here as evidence for Hunter's direct line. Earlier general Spence surname history remains unlinked to Dale Sr.'s proven parentage and should stay segregated from this problem.

Confidence: HIGH

Unresolved gaps

1. Was Dale William Spence Sr. born in Texas, England, or elsewhere?

2. Who was Dale Sr.'s father?

3. Was his mother actually named Dovie, or is that name a family-memory distortion of another given name?

4. If "Dovie" is correct, what was her maiden name?

5. Is there any public death or burial record yet available for Dale Sr. that names parents?

Exact source list

  • Rice Athletics: https://riceowls.com/news/2019/10/11/mens-track-field-pr-club-to-honor-spence-stadel-and-1995-championship-team
  • Beaumont High yearbook: https://www.e-yearbook.com/yearbooks/Beaumont_High_School_Pine_Burr_Yearbook/1952/Page_234.html
  • Rice History Corner comment thread: https://ricehistorycorner.com/2020/05/07/semper-fidelis-date-unknown/
  • LSU dissertation repository: https://repository.lsu.edu/gradschool_disstheses/1171/
  • PubMed affiliation page: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/2375667/
  • Rice News veterans article: https://news2.rice.edu/2019/11/11/honoring-our-veterans/
  • PeopleLegacy Alice Spence burial index: https://peoplelegacy.com/burial-records/alice/spence/
  • PeopleLegacy Lee Henslee burial index: https://peoplelegacy.com/burial-records/lee/henslee/
49 Kellner / Mollenhauer / Girsewald Deep Follow-Up 10 KB Research date: 2026-04-26

Kellner / Mollenhauer / Girsewald Deep Follow-Up

Research date: 2026-04-26
